Zoom link on Canvas). Prerequisites: • CSC 220: grade of C or better. • CSC 230: grade of C or better. • The prerequisites for this course are strictly enforced for all sections. Concurrent enrollment is not allowed. Textbook: In this class we will be using: Data Abstraction and Problem Solving with C++: Walls and Mirrors, 7th Edition Objectives: Design and architecture high quality and consistent practical applications and solutions, from concept/idea to integration, testing and delivery. Understand, analyze, and utilize appropriate data structures and algorithms for sorting, searching and graphs. Apply ethical computing concepts and practices to solution design and implementation. Tentative Course Outline: • Students will learn and understand the concepts and applications of basic and advanced C++ topics including ADTs, pointer-based implementation of dynamic arrays and linked lists, operator overloading, STLs, inheritance and polymorphism. Students will apply these skills to develop medium sized C++ projects to solve real-world problems. • Students will learn the problem-solving process and describe their solutions to a computational problem in pseudocode. • Students will learn to analyze the time complexity of foundational algorithms such as binary search, insertion sort, selection sort, quick sort, and finding the k-th ranked object. • Recommended C++ IDEs – Microsoft Visual Studio, Eclipse, or IDE of your choice. There are documentation available on Canvas under Resources for installing Visual Studio or Eclipse (requires C++ compiler). • Text editor and command line using GCC or Clang compiler. Communication: • Email is the best way to communicate with me outside of class. • Feel free to send me questions about the software industry or your resume for feedback. • This is a live document; I may make changes to any of these policies and contents at any time during the semester. I will announce changes in class and post them on Canvas.

Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 5 of 6 Course Policy: • Attendance: Students are expected to attend all scheduled lectures in this course. Attendance is vital for understanding course material, participating in class discussions, and achieving academic success. Students are responsible for what they miss if they don’t attend any of the scheduled lectures • Exams: All the exams in this course will be given on paper in the classroom and will be proctored by the instructor. A government photo ID (student ID or driver’s license) is required when turning an exam. If any of the scheduled exam dates conflict with your religious observances, you must notify your instructor during the first two weeks of the semester. Drop and withdrawal from courses are student responsibilities. Since two best scores out of three test scores are used to determine the final grade, no makeup exams are allowed (i.e., you can miss a Midterm but will then need to take the final). • Milestones Assignments: This class has four mandatory milestones assignments. The following are their policies: o Only assignments uploaded to GitHub or Canvas will be considered for grading. o All assignments are due on the designated due date, as specified by the instructor, and it is the student’s responsibility to keep track of all assignment due dates and plan accordingly. o Students are responsible for what they turn in. If you submit a blank assignment, it will be graded as such. Always double check your submissions to make sure what you meant to upload is actually what you uploaded. o Late homework submissions will be subject to penalties. A 10% deduction will be applied for each day that the assignment is overdue, up to a maximum of 7 days. After 7 days, the assignment will be considered as not submitted and will be graded as such (i.e., given a 0). This policy applies only to milestones 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. It is imperative for students to adhere to the specified deadlines, as failing to do so will result in a reduction of their assignment scores. o This policy is designed to emphasize the importance of timely and responsible submission of coursework, while also providing a reasonable window for unforeseen circumstances. Students are strongly encouraged to manage their time effectively to avoid any unnecessary deductions and to ensure the best possible academic outcomes. o Exceptions to this policy must be accompanied by documentation. For instance, if you had Covid or other health issue, then you will need to supply a doctor’s note. • Class Notes: Students may not capture audio, photos or video from class sessions on their own devices without the explicit written permission of the instructor and everyone present, unless part of a DPRC- authorized accommodation. • Office Hours Policies: Office hours are a valuable resource provided by instructors to support students’ learning and offer additional assistance outside of regular lectures. They are intended to foster a deeper understanding of course materials, answer specific questions, provide guidance on assignments, and address any concerns related to the course. It is important to emphasize that office hours are not intended to serve as a replacement for missed lectures. Instructors do not use office hours to re-lecture or repeat the content covered during a missed class. • Instructor Availability:

You can still send an email or message asking for help, but I will be unable to provide a response to your request until the next business day • Final Grades: I create a lot of opportunities for students to do well in this course, and all posted grades are final unless there was a verifiable mistake in the grade calculation. Fairness demands, and university rules, require that all students be marked according to the same standards, so that all students receive the marks they earned. I can’t just raise your final grade for unjustifiable reasons, so I will not respond to any such requests.

Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 6 of 6 • Academic Integrity: Any form of cheating or plagiarism will incur very serious consequences. Students may not post any course materials to any third-party sites (such as Chegg) or post any recordings, screenshots, audio or chat transcripts in any setting outside the class, and that violations of this are subject to student disciplinary action. Any instances of cheating, deceit, fabrication, forgery, plagiarism, unauthorized altering of records or submitting false documents, unauthorized collaboration, unauthorized submission of work previously given credit, or other forms of academic misconduct will be assigned a grade penalty, likely an F or a grade of zero. Failing one or more assignments or examinations for reasons of academic integrity violations may result in a final class grade of F. Students may not withdraw from classes in which they have committed academic misconduct. Consequences for violations of academic integrity may exceed an F on the assignment, examination, or class as determined by the Academic Integrity Review Committee. Members of our academic community have a responsibility to develop an awareness of academic integrity, to cultivate skills to realize honesty in academic and community work, and to sustain actively academic honor as a core value of our community. Students are expected to engage in behaviors that reflect well upon the university. In addition to attending to one’s own actions, the Standards for Student Conduct require that students who witness academic dishonesty notify their faculty/instructor, department chair, or the Office of Student Conduct. Supporting academic integrity enhances the reputation of the University and the value attributed to degrees awarded by the University. To learn more about the university policies regarding academic integrity: https://conduct.sfsu.edu/academic-dishonesty. • Students with disabilities: Students with disabilities who need reasonable accommodations are encouraged to contact the instructor. The Disability Programs and Resource Center (DPRC) is available to facilitate the reasonable accommodations process. Programming Methodology “Programming Methodology is the way of analyzing a particular complex problem by planning the software development and controlling the development process.” • Current most popular methodology is Object Oriented • Create objects and their attributes (nouns) and methods (verbs) • Inheritance, Encapsulation, and Polymorphism • Is AI a methodology?
Is AI a Methodology? “AI won’t take your job, it’s somebody using AI that will take your job.” Richard Baldwin 2023 World Economic Forum’s Growth Summit

Development and Debugging Process • The IDEAL Problem Solver • By John Bransford and Barry Stein • I - Identify • Dev - Product Managers identify a problem (RUP – Inception phase) • Debug - Easy for debugging software (QA or user will find a bug) and look for potential future problems • D - Define • Dev - Product Managers define the problem (RUP – Inception phase) • Debug - What use cases reproduce the error and what doesn’t • E - Explore • Dev - Architects negotiate functionality with Product Managers (RUP – Elaboration phase, iterative) • Debug - Find the problem area and explore alternative fixes • A - Act on a plan • Dev - Architects and developers design and write the code (RUP – Construction, iterative) • Debug - Fix the bug • L - Look at the effects • Dev - QA verifies that the new software works (RUP – Transition, iterative) • Debug - Verify the bug fix and add test case

Testing Type Description Unit Testing A programmatic test that tests the internal working of a unit of code, such as a method or a function. Generally written by the developer. Minimal Acceptance Test (MATs) A quick set of tests that verify a version of software meets the minimum quality level. Standard process is to run MATs before a code merge. Full Regression Testing can take days, so MATs are run to verify basic functionality. Regression Testing Ensures that existing features/functionality that used to work are not broken due to new code changes. Generally written by Quality Assurance. System Testing Complete end-to-end testing is done on the complete software to make sure the whole system works as expected. Smoke Testing A quick test performed to ensure that the software works at the most basic level and doesn’t crash when it’s started. Its name originates from the hardware testing where you just plug the device and see if smoke comes out. Performance Testing Ensures that the software performs according to the user’s expectations by checking the response time and throughput under specific load and environment. User-Acceptance Testing Ensures the software meets the requirements of the clients or users. This is typically the last step before the software is live, i.e. it goes to production. Stress Testing Ensures that the performance of the software doesn’t degrade when the load increases. In stress testing, the tester subjects the software under heavy loads, such as a high number of requests or stringent memory conditions to verify if it works well. Usability Testing Measures how usable the software is. This is typically performed with a sample set of end-users, who use the software and provide feedback on how easy or complicated it is to use the software. Security Testing Now more important than ever. Security testing tries to break a software’s security checks, to gain access to confidential data. Security testing is crucial for web-based applications or any applications that involve money. Generally done by the Security Testing Group (fun job!) https://www.interviewbit.com/software-testing-interview-questions/

Test- Driven Development (TDD) 1. Identify a new feature 2. Write a unit test for that feature (note test is written before the code) 3. Run the test. It should fail. 4. Write code that passes test. • No self documenting code! 5. Optional: refactor code to make it faster, simpler, more supportable, etc.
Test Question • What type of testing have you utilized in your CSC 220/CSC 221 course?

Effective interfaces • User Interface (UI) • Easy to use • Useful error message • Application Programming Interface (API) • Easy to use • Name methods and parameters effectively • Simple parameter list • Exception handling
User Interface – modern UI’s use Model View Controller (MVC)

Professionally written code • Effective and efficient • Does what it is supposed to do • Does not consume more CPU or memory than needed • Performant • Stable/Reliable • Maintainability • Human understandable • Take advantage of Libraries and Frameworks • Documentation, Documentation, and more Documentation • Testing • Modular • Break large problems into smaller problems • Larger methods are generally harder to reuse

Programming Style • Writing easy to read and effective programs is a skill • Strive to be always write clear and understandable code • 90% of the cost for industry code is for maintenance • Clear and understandable code is maintainable code • Follow corporate coding standards • Good names for variables and methods • Always create File and Method comment headers • Comment the code for the next person • Liberal use of whitespace

Experience counts • The more you code, the better you will get at it • Practice, practice and more practice writing code • Spend more time on important code • Good, Fast, and Cheap • Write code fast and cheap. Once determine which is most used, then improve • Listen and learn from those before you

Generative AI as a Crutch • Dependence on Generative AI can be detrimental to your skills • ~80% of generated code is good (this will improve) • How will you determine which 20% is not so good? • Experience is needed to determine good from not so good • Experience comes from practice and more practice

Death by a Thousand Paper Cuts • Working on code is difficult • Little bothersome issues add up • Your own (unmatching) coding style • Use your name or pets’ name for variables • Multiple lines of code in a single line • Hard to understand custom code (i.e., non-standard): • Algorithms • Data structures • Use Design Patterns

Closing Words on Programming Methodology “Any fool can write code that a computer can understand. Good programmers write code that humans can understand.” Martin Fowler Author of multiple OO and UML texts

Introduction to C++ Data Abstraction & Problem Solving with C++: Walls and Mirrors, 7th Edition • Syntax • Memory management • STL • Similarities/Differences from Java

Introduction to C++ • C++ is an object-oriented language based on C • C++ was developed by Bjorne Stroustrap at Bell Labs (1980’s) • CSC 340 doesn’t assume experience in C or C++ • CSC 340 does assume experience with Java
C++ Syntax

C++ Memory Management • In Java, all objects are created through new, and the memory will be reclaimed with the garbage collector when no longer referenced • In C++, objects can be created via new, but the memory is not automatically reclaimed • In C++, if an object doesn’t have a pointer to it, it is a memory leak because it can’t be reclaimed. • Memory leaks are very bad and eventually will bring the software down

C++ and Standard Template Library (STL) • Functionally, similar to Java generics • In C++, best to explicitly include only what is needed #include <list> • In C, code is in .c files and interfaces/declarations in .h (header files) • In C++, header files can contain implementation • .hpp is a convention to differentiate from C headers • However, template code is contained in .hpp files • injected at compile time
C++ vs Java • Memory management • STL vs generics • Java initializes primitive member variables (C++ does not) • In C++, pointers are different than references

Advanced Data Structures • Review Data Structures covered in CSC 220 • If you have not taken a Data Structures class, please take CSC 220 • Implementation • Implementation in C++ • Using Data Structures with STL • Go into more depth for when to use a particular Data Structure • Complexity of algorithms for operations

Data Structure Overview • Provides approach to store, organize and retrieve data efficiently • Basic types: queue, stack, linked list • Advanced types: tree, hash table, graph • Certain data structures lend themselves to certain applications • Database – b-trees • Compiler – hash tables, stack • Data structure + algorithms = programs
Implementation in C++ • Review implementation in java • Assignments are to implement them in C++ • Not from scratch (skeleton/example code will be provided) • Template implementation

When to use a particular Data Structure • Go over scenarios and decide which Data Structure to use • Scenarios will be from Job Interview Questions • Almost all interviews for software development have Data Structure questions • My personal favorites, as well as interview questions on web • The one skill I want you to develop in this class, is the ability to analyze a problem and determine which Data Structure(s) to use to solve it. • Good for Job Interviews • But, more importantly, it’s good to know for your career

Industry information • Programming versus Software Engineering • Writing and Presenting to communicate (i.e., Architectural/Design docs) • Comments as code • Peer review of code • Unit Tests to verify functionality • Earlier you catch an error, the cheaper it is (compile, QA, Regression, Production)

Books For Software Engineers • The Art of Computer Programming - Fundamental Algorithms (Knuth) • Covers most topics for a BS in Computer Science (3 x ~$60) • Unix Network Programming (Stevens) • First 7 chapters give a great overview of Unix and TCP/IP (~$45) • Design Patterns (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides, aka Gang of Four) • Concepts for advanced Object Oriented programming (~$45) • Database Tuning – A Principled Approach (Shasha and Bonnet) • Basics of database tuning (~$65) • Introduction to Parallel Programming (Pacheco and Malensek) • Most modern programs require multi-threading (~$65)

Programming Style • Writing easy to read and effective programs is a skill • Strive to be always write clear and understandable code • 90% of the cost for industry code is for maintenance • Clear and understandable code is maintainable code • Follow corporate coding standards • Good names for variables and methods • Always create File and Method comment headers • Comment the code for the next person • Liberal use of whitespace

Generative AI as a Crutch • Dependence on Generative AI can be detrimental to your skills • ~80% of generated code is good (this will improve) • How will you determine which 20% is not so good? • Experience is needed to determine good from not so good • Experience comes from practice and more practice

Review Week 2 • C++ Syntax • Functions • Arrays and Vectors • Strings • Inheritance

C++ Syntax Multi-line comments start with /* and end with */ // variables begin with a letter or underscore // constants do not change // syntax structure allowing grouping of data & methods // main must be global and return int

C++ Functions • An argument passed by reference is not copied but is accessed directly within the function. • Which variables are passed by Value? Reference? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved void computeMax(int x, int y, int& larger) // void function does not return a value { larger = ((x > y) ? x : y): } // end computeMax int main () { int a = 0; int b = 0; int largerAB = 0; cin >> a >> b; computeMax(a, b, largerAB); cout << "The larger of " << a << " and " << b << " is " << largerAB << ".\n"; }

C++ Passing Arrays to a Functions • Arrays are always passed by reference to a function. • Common ways to pass arrays to function: 1. void modifyArray(int arr[]) // same as #2 2. void modifyArray(int *arr) // same as #1 3. void modifyPointer(int*& arr) // allows you modify array elements and pointer itself; useful when you want to change memory location © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Strings • The C++ Standard Library provides the data type string. To use this library, you include the statements #include <string> using namespace std; • You can concatenate two strings to form another string by using the + operator. • You can manipulate a portion of a string by using the method substr(position, length ) • The first argument specifies the position of the beginning of the substring (remember that 0 is the position of the first character in the string). The second argument is the length of the substring. For example, (e.g., title = "J Perfect's Diary"); title.substr(2, 7) • is the string “ Perfect ”. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • Choose identifiers that describe their purpose—that is, that are self-documenting. Distinguish between keywords, such as int, and user-defined identifiers. • Use a good indentation style to enhance the readability of a program. The layout of a program should make it easy for a reader to identify the program’s modules. • Use a blank line between method or function definitions. • Within these definitions, you should indent individual blocks of code. These blocks are generally—but are not limited to—the actions performed within a control structure, such as a while loop or an if statement. • Named constants make a program easier to modify, e.g., const int NUMBER_OF_MAJORS = 202; • typedef statements make a program easier to modify; if your program performs floating point computations, and you need greater precision than float (change float to double) • typedef float RealType; // original statement • typedef long double RealType; // update to this statement in one spot © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Testing and Programming • Two kinds of testing can be applied to a system: • Open-box testing (white-box or glass-box testing) - design tests knowing how the modules work. • choose a method’s actual arguments in a way that exercises all lines of code in the method. • make sure that all branches of if and switch statements are executed. • Closed-box testing (black-box or functional testing) - you know the name of the method being tested, as well as its argument list, its return type, and its operation contract. • develop tests across a range of valid input, and then check the output against hand-calculated results. • Choose arguments outside of their valid range tests how the module handles incorrect input. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Inheritance • Inheritance allows us to reuse and extend work we have already completed and tested. • You can think of a base class as a parent class. • base class = superclass = parent class • You can think of a derived class as a child class • derived class = subclass = child class • Any instance of the derived class is also considered to be an instance of the base class and can be used in a program anywhere that an instance of the base class can be used • When the keyword public is used with the base class, any of the publicly defined methods or data fields in the base class can be used by instances of the derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overriding Base-Class Methods • You can add as many new members to derived class as desired • You can redefine inherited methods • Called overriding a base-class method. • A method overrides a base-class method when • The two methods have the same name and parameter declarations • Precede a method name with the base-class namespace indicator to tell the compiler to use the base-class version of the method • Without a namespace indicator in front of the method name, the compiler assumes that the programmer is referring to the current class and inserts code to call that version of the method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Virtual Methods, Abstract Classes • Using keyword virtual in front of the prototype • Tells the C++ compiler that the code this method executes is determined at runtime • Allows an application using our class to take advantage of polymorphism when the methods are invoked • Pure virtual method • Virtual method that has no implementation • Abstract class • Has at least one pure virtual method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Review Week 3 • Memory Management • Many differences between C++ and Java • Memory Leaks in C++ • C++ Inheritence

Pointers in C++ CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In Java, an object is always referred to via a pointer variable that points to it; on the other hand, a primitive type value is always referred to directly, and never referred to via a pointer variable that points to it • In C++, you have a choice whether to refer to an instance of any type directly, or via a pointer • In particular, C++, you can create a pointer that points to any type you want (even another pointer), and manipulate the values stored in the pointers, and in the memory they point to, in absolute detail • This is an extremely powerful feature of C and C++, and it exposes some of the implementation details of pointers that are abstracted in Java

Making pointers point CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The “address-of” prefix operator & applied to a variable returns the memory address of the variable int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // initialize pt1 to point to a address memory cell identifier 512000 5 a 512004 -999 b 512000 pt1 512008 cout << "a is:" << a; What is the output? // displays: a is: 5 cout << " &a is: " << &a; cout << " &pt1 is: " << &pt1; cout << " *pt1 is: " << *pt1; cout << " pt1 is: " << pt1; // displays: &a is: 512000 // displays: &pt1 is: 512008 // displays: *pt1 is: 5 // displays: pt1 is: 512000

An abstract picture of memory CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A useful abstract way to draw the contents of memory after those statements execute is shown here. The exact value of the address in the pointer is not shown. Instead, the fact that the pointer named pt1 points to the variable named a is shown with an arrow: int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // initialize pt1 to point to a 5 a: -999 b: pt1: • Note: this picture is impossible in Java, which does not allow pointers to point directly to primitive type variables

Dereferencing pointers CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The “dereferencing” prefix operator * applied to a pointer value “goes to” the location in memory pointed to by the pointer int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // declare and initialize pt1 to point to a cout << *pt1; // print the value of what pt1 points to:5 *pt1 = 10; // assign a value to what pt1 points to cout << a; // print the value of a, which is...10 • In C++ you can have pointers to anything! For example you can create a pointer to a pointer to an int: int ** pp = &pt1; // declare and initialize pp to point to pt1 **pp = 20; // assign a value to what pp points to points to...! cout << a // print the value of a, which is...20

Pitfalls with arrays and pointers in in C++ CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • With a declaration like int a[100]; expressions like a[-33] or a[101] or *(a + 10000) are very dubious, to say the least • However, these are not compiler errors, and may not even give you runtime errors (unless they involve accessing protected memory) • The power of pointer arithmetic can hurt you, if you’re not careful... • For these reasons, though C-style arrays are available in C++, it is advisable to use data structures from the Standard Template Library (STL) such as std::vector instead of basic arrays

Strings as arrays of chars in C++ CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 ’\0’ used to terminate the string • As in C, an array of char elements with an element can be used as a string in C++ • ’\0’ is the null character, an 8-bit integer with value 0 • to contain a string, a char array must contain a null character • the null character is used to determine the end of the string contained in the array • A null-terminated array of char can be initialized with a string literal: char s[] = "hello"; • this creates an array s with 6 elements, not 5! • the null character is included, as the last element of s • this is equivalent to char s[] = {’h’,’e’,’l’,’l’,’o’,’\0’}; or char s[6] = "hello";

Dealing with strings in C++ CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Since in C++ a null-terminated char array (like any array) does not keep information about its own length, extra checking must be done to make sure all accesses to the string are within bounds • This checking is tedious to do, but if not done, leads to serious bugs and exploitable code • Most software security problems are due to unchecked array bounds violations (buffer overflows) in C and C++ programs! • Again, in C++, it is advisable to use data structures from the Standard Template Library (STL): instead of null-terminated char arrays, use std::string for strings!

C++ Destructors • Destructors • destroys an instance of a class when the object’s lifetime ends • A class has one destructor • Automatically called when an object goes out of scope or is deleted • For many classes, you can omit the destructor. In such cases, the compiler generates a destructor for you. • If the class itself creates an object on the free store (heap) by using the new operator, it is a safe and secure programming practice to implement a destructor to ensure that the memory for that object is freed (cover in Interlude 2) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Preprocessor directives • Preprocessor directives • Provides a way to prevent the compiler from reading the class definition a second time, if class definition is included multiple times: • #ifndef PLAIN_BOX // If PLAIN_BOX is not defined, then ...” • #define PLAIN_BOX // defines the name PLAIN_BOX • … • #endif // PLAIN_BOX © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If the compiler had not defined the name PLAIN_BOX, it would process the code that follows until it reached the #endif directive at the end of the file. If another file includes the class definition of PlainBox, the name PLAIN_BOX will already have been defined. That file’s #ifndef directive will cause the preprocessor to skip any of the code that follows, so the compiler will not see the same class definition more than once.

C++ Inheritance • Inheritance allows us to reuse and extend work we have already completed and tested. • You can think of a base class as a parent class. • base class = superclass = parent class • You can think of a derived class as a child class • derived class = subclass = child class • Any instance of the derived class is also considered to be an instance of the base class and can be used in a program anywhere that an instance of the base class can be used • When the keyword public is used with the base class, any of the publicly defined methods or data fields in the base class can be used by instances of the derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ derived class • has the data fields and methods of the base class in addition to the members it defines, • it can access only publicly defined members from the base class • can also revise any inherited public method. • contains copies of inherited data fields, • does not contain a copy of the code for inherited methods • does not inherit constructors from parent class, but it can use it or other parent method to initialize the private data fields © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Inheritance #include <iostream> #include <string> using namespace std; class Vehicle { public: string brand = "Chevrolet"; // Default brand is Chevrolet void playStereo() { cout << "\nBlasting it! \n"; // Simulates playing the stereo } }; // The Car class is derived from the Vehicle class and adds attributes and behaviors specific to a car. class Car: public Vehicle { public: string model = "Corvette"; // Default model is Corvette }; int main() { Car myCar; // Create a Car object named myCar cout << myCar.brand + " " + myCar.model; // Output the brand and model of the car myCar.playStereo(); // Call the playStereo function from the Vehicle class return 0; // Return 0 to indicate successful execution } What is the console output? Chevrolet Corvette Blasting it!

Overriding Base-Class Methods #include <iostream> #include <string> using namespace std; class Vehicle { public: string brand = "Chevrolet"; // Default brand is Chevrolet void playStereo() { cout << "
Play at regular volume
"; // Simulates playing the stereo } }; // The Car class is derived from the Vehicle class and adds attributes and behaviors specific to a car. class Car: public Vehicle { public: string model = "Corvette"; // Default model is Corvette void playStereo() { cout << "

" ; } }; int main() { Car myCar; // Create a Car object named myCar cout << myCar.brand + " " + myCar.model; // Output the brand and model of the car myCar.Vehicle::playStereo(); // Call the playStereo function from the Vehicle class myCar.playStereo(); // Call the playStereo function from the Car class return 0; // Return 0 to indicate successful execution } What is the console output? C++ Review 1. Which variables are Automatic? 2. Where are Automatic variables allocated?

Virtual Methods and Polymorphism • Allow compiler to perform the late binding necessary for polymorphism • Declare methods in base class as virtual, to enable late binding for polymorphism; indicates methods can be overridden • Create variables in the free store (heap), and use pointers to reference them • Change function to accept that pointer • Free the memory in heap; delete and assign to nullptr © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Early versus Late Binding • Use early binding for better performance and when you don't need polymorphism. • Use late binding (virtual functions) for polymorphism, and need to ensure the derived class method is called at runtime, even when using a base class pointer or reference • declare function virtual in the base class, which allows compiler to determine appropriate function to call at runtime, based on object type https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/early-binding-late-binding-c/

Pointers and Program’s Free Store • FIGURE C2-2 Sample program memory layout © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved When a C++ program begins execution, the operating system sets aside memory for • Run-time stack, • Code storage – store your code, or text storage, • Static storage – store any global variables and static variables, • Heap, or free store, which a programmer can use to store data

Deallocating Memory • When memory to which pointer variable points is no longer needed • Deallocate it by using delete operator. • Then set pointer variable to nullptr • Otherwise dangling pointer exists • It would still contain address of object that was deallocated. • Can be source of serious errors. • C++ memory leak tools: https://learn.microsoft.com/en- us/cpp/c-runtime-library/find-memory-leaks-using-the-crt- library?view=msvc-170 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Memory Leaks • Memory leaks occur when • An object has been created in the heap (free stores), but • Program no longer has a way to access © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved LISTING C2-1 Poorly written function that allocates memory in the free store We have memory leak, since someItem and the pointer someBoxPtr are both stored on the stack, which are destroyed after the function ends.

Avoiding Memory Leaks • The best option for preventing a memory leak is to not use a function to return a pointer to a newly created object • You should define a class that has a method for this task • Class takes responsibility for deleting the object in the heap (free store) and ensures that there is no memory leak. At a minimum, such a class will have three parts: 1. a method that creates the object in the free store, 2. a data field that points to the object, and 3. a method—the destructor —that deletes the object when the class instance is no longer needed © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Memory Leaks • Destructors • Every C++ class has a destructor that has the same name as the class, but is preceded by the tilde (~) character. • For objects that are local variables, the destructor is called when the activation record containing the object is removed from the run- time stack. • If the object was created using new and is stored in the free store, the destructor is called when the client uses the operator delete to free the memory allocated to the object. • Often, the compiler-generated destructor is sufficient for a class, but if the class itself creates an object on the free store (heap) by using the new operator, it is a safe and secure programming practice to implement a destructor to ensure that the memory for that object is freed © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Dangling Pointers • FIGURE C2-7 Two pointers referencing (pointing to) the same object © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If you have two (2) or more pointers pointing to the same object (alias), and • you delete the first pointer to the object, and • attempt to execute the method with the second pointer, will result in a runtime error, since the second pointer is a dangling pointer o MagicBox<string>* myBoxPtr = new MagicBox<string>(); o MagicBox<string>* yourBoxPtr = myBoxPtr; Suppose we then execute: delete myBoxPtr; myBoxPtr = nullptr ; yourBoxPtr->getItem(); • Program aborts because yourBoxPtr still references the object’s location in the heap, even though the object no longer exists • yourBoxPtr is a dangling pointer and the program aborts when we try to call a method on that object

Avoiding Dangling Pointers • Set pointer variables to nullptr either initially or when you no longer need them. • If a class has a pointer variable as a data field, the constructor should always initialize that data field, to point either to an object or to nullptr. • Test whether a pointer variable contains nullptr before using it to call a method. • Try to reduce the use of aliases in your program. • Do not delete an object in the free store (heap) until you are certain that no other alias needs to use it. • Set all aliases that reference a deleted object to nullptr when the object is deleted. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Aspects of Object-Oriented Solution Principles of object-oriented programming • Encapsulation: Objects combine data and operations; hides inner details • Inheritance: Classes inherit properties from other classes; allows class reuse • Polymorphism: Objects determine appropriate operations at execution time. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Cohesion • Each module should perform one well-defined task - cohesive • Benefits • Well named, self-documenting, e.g., sort • Easy to reuse • Easier to maintain • More robust; less likely to be impacted by change © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Coupling • Measure of dependence among modules • Dependence • Sharing data structures or calling each other’s methods • Modules should be loosely coupled • Highly coupled modules should be avoided • Benefits of loose coupling in a system • More adaptable to change • Easier to understand • Increases reusability • Has increased cohesion © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Abstraction • Separate purpose of a module from its implementation • For example, you should be able to change the sorting algorithm in an implementation without affecting the rest of the solution • Specifications do not indicate how to implement • Able to use without knowing implementation • Abstraction helps identify details that should be hidden from public view • Ensured no other module can tamper with these hidden details. • Isolation of the modules cannot be total, however • Client must know what tasks can be done, how to initiate a task © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Situations occur for which search-tree implementations are not adequate. • Consider a method which acts as an “address calculator” which determines an array index • Used for add, getValue, remove operations • Called a hash function/hash code • Tells where to place item in a hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • FIGURE 18-9 Address calculator © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Perfect hash function • Maps each search key into a unique location of the hash table • Possible if you know all the search keys • Collision occurs when hash function maps more than one entry into same array location • Hash function should • Be easy, fast to compute • Place entries evenly throughout hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hash Functions • Sufficient for hash functions to operate on integers – examples: • Select digits from an ID number • Generally uneven distribution • Folding – add digits, sum is the table location • Modulo arithmetic h(x) = x mod tableSize • Simple, effective • Use prime number for table size • Convert character string to an integer – use ASCII values • Address potential overflow © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • Open addressing “Open” means to look for an open slot • Linear probing • Quadratic probing • Double hashing • Increase size of hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • FIGURE 18-11 Linear probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Linear probing • Add - search the hash table, starting from the original hash location; if table[h(searchKey)] is occupied, check table[h(searchKey)+1] , table[h(searchKey)+2], … until you find an available location. • Delete –place table location into one of three states: • occupied (currently in use), • empty (has not been used), or • Modify addItem to insert at this location • removed (was once occupied but is now available) • Modify getItem to continue probing • Modify addItem to insert at this location • Items tend to cluster together in the hash table, when using this approach - primary clustering. • Clusters can get close to one another and, merge into a larger cluster. • Large clusters tend to get even larger

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • FIGURE 18-12 Quadratic probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quadratic probing. • You can virtually eliminate primary clusters by adjusting the linear probing scheme. • When two items hash collide, quadratic probing uses the same probe sequence for each item. • The resulting phenomenon—called secondary clustering —delays the resolution of the collision. • Although the research of quadratic probing remains incomplete, it appears that secondary clustering is not a problem.

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • FIGURE 18-13 Double hashing during the addition of 58, 14, and 91 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Double hashing - defines key-dependent probe sequences. • searches the hash table in a linear order, starting at the location h1(key), • second hash function h2 determines the size of the steps taken • h2(key) <> 0 • h2 <> h1 • For example, let h1 and h2 be the primary and secondary hash functions defined as • h1 ( key ) = key mod 11 // 11 is table size and prime number • h2(key) = 7 - (key mod 7) // visits all the table locations (table size and step are prime) If key = 58, • h1 hashes to location 3 (58 mod 11), and • h2 indicates that the probe sequence should take steps of size 5 (7 – 58 mod 7). • In other words, the probe sequence will be 3, 8, 2 (wraps around), 7, 1 (wraps around), 6, 0, 5, 10, 4, 9.

Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure • FIGURE 18-14 Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Separate chaining. Design the hash table as an array of linked chains • Each entry table[i] is a pointer to a chain of linked nodes containing the items that the hash function has mapped into location i • The size of the dictionary is dynamic and can exceed the size of the hash table, because each linked chain can be as long as necessary. • The most time-efficient collision-resolution scheme.
The Efficiency of Hashing • FIGURE 18-15 The relative efficiency of four collision-resolution methods © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

What Constitutes a Good Hash Function? • Is hash function easy and fast to compute? • Does hash function scatter data evenly throughout hash table? • How well does hash function scatter random data? • How well does hash function scatter non-random data? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Review Week 5 • UML • Recursion • Exceptions • Multiple Inheritance

The Unified Modeling Language • The Unified Modeling Language, or UML, is a modeling language used to express object-oriented designs • A use case is a set of textual scenarios or stories that describe the proposed solution, but does not describe how the system works • A use case is not object-oriented, but it is an important part of the analysis of the problem. • A single use case usually has several scenarios. • The main success scenario (or “happy path”) describes how the system satisfies the goals of the user when everything goes smoothly. • A scenario is written from the perspective of the user of the system. • Alternate scenarios should be written only for exception cases. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Sequence Diagram • The UML sequence diagram is a design document that gives guidance on several levels to the programmer implementing a solution – Shows which objects collaborate to solve a particular use-case scenario – Shows the synchronous nature of the messages that are passed between the objects in the scenario, providing the order in which tasks are accomplished – Shows which object is responsible for what task or tasks by handling a particular message – Provides a visual representation of the flow of messages sent between objects during a single scenario © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

© 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Show objects left to right Each object has a lifeline (vertical dashed line) Each lifeline can have 1 or more activation bars (open box on lifeline) To send a message between objects, draw a solid arrow from the activation bar of the calling object to the top of a new activation bar on the lifeline of the called object, with message and parameters Task completes and returns (dashed arrow w/ variable) Objects may send messages to itself The UML Sequence Diagram

The UML Class Diagram © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved association relationship indicated by the solid line with no arrowheads multiplicities (optional) – each bank is associated w/ 0 or more customers, but each customer is associated w/ 1 bank multiplicities (optional) – each customer can have 0 or more accounts, but each account can belong to 1 customer • The Account class is related to the Checking and Savings classes via generalization (inheritance) relationship, indicated by the open triangular arrowhead • Checking and Savings inherit the attributes and operations of the Account class. • Account class name is in italic, which denotes an abstract base class • The getBalance operation in the Account class is also in italic. This indicates that getBalance is not implemented in the Account class but must be implemented within the descendant class. 5 classes: 1) Bank, 2) Customer, 3) Account, 4) Checking and 5) Savings • Bank and Account classes have aggregation (containment) relationship • The open diamond head denotes containing class; banks live longer than accounts • Filled in diamond head is a composition relationship, where lifetimes are the same • Checking and Savings classes each implement the inherited getBalance operation. • This operation will take on polymorphic behavior when called from a parent class object.

Recursive Solutions • A recursive function calls itself • Each recursive call solves an identical, but smaller, problem • Test for base case enables recursive calls to stop • Eventually, one of smaller problems must be the base case • Some recursive solutions are impractical because they are so inefficient • Can provide elegantly simple solutions to problems of great complexity © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Recursive Solutions • Recursion breaks problem into smaller identical problems – An alternative to iteration • FIGURE 2-1 A recursive solution © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • This strategy is called divide and conquer. • Solve the dictionary search problem by first dividing the dictionary into two halves and then conquering the appropriate half. • Solve the smaller problem by using the same divide-and-conquer strategy. • Dividing continues until you reach the base case.

Recursion and Efficiency • Factors that contribute to inefficiency – Overhead associated with function calls – Some recursive algorithms inherently inefficient • Keep in mind – Recursion can clarify complex solutions … but … – Clear, efficient iterative solution may be better © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Throwing Exceptions FIGURE C3-1 Hierarchy of C++ exception classes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Syntax runtime_error exceptions (or one of its derived classes) are thrown by errors that are detected at runtime (e.g., division by zero or numeric overflow) • program will halt, just like an assertion fail • can contain a message that is displayed to the user giving details about the error and the reason the program halted logic_error exceptions (or one of its derived classes) represent errors in program logic (e.g., unmet precondition or failure to satisfy a postcondition, that you could detect before the program executes). • test for an error or unusual condition using an if statement; throw an exception containing a message to our client, if error occurs • To restrict the exceptions, you can include a throw clause in its header, informing you that the function might throw an exception. • Including a throw clause in a function specification ensures that the function can throw only those exceptions. An attempt by the function to throw any other exception will result in a runtime error. • Omitting a throw clause allows a function to throw any exception • You can handle only exceptions of the class logic_error and its derived classes. To handle an exception, you write code that consists of two pieces: • try block - contains the statements that might cause or throw an exception • one or more catch blocks that follow the try block

Handling Exceptions • Code for handling exception – try block – contains statements that might cause or throw an exception – catch block – immediately follows try block with code to react to or catch a particular type of exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved General syntax for a try block followed by one catch block

Handling Exceptions • If no exception occurs and try block completes – Execution continues with statement after catch block • If statement within try block causes exception of type specified in catch block – Remainder of try block abandoned – Execution transfers to statements in catch block – After catch block statements finish, execution jumps to statement after last catch block © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Handling Exceptions • The syntax for catch block resembles that of a function definition – Specifies type of exception, and an identifier – The catch block parameter provides name for caught exception • Steps taken in catch block vary – Simple message – Elaborate update of variables, retry of offending function © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Programmer-Defined Exception Classes Example – constructor provides way for throw statement to identify condition of exception. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Exceptions - Summary • To throw an exception in a function: – Determine what errors, preconditions, postconditions, and unusual conditions you must check and where they should be checked, so your function can correctly perform its task. Usually this is done with an if statement. – If an error condition has occurred, throw an exception using the throw statement. As an argument to the exception class constructor, pass a string description of the reason for the error. – Add a throw clause to the function header indicating the exceptions thrown by the function. – Derive a custom exception class to better identify the conditions that caused the error, though doing so is optional, it is desirable. • To handle an exception or use a function that could throw an exception – Place the statement that might throw an exception in a try block. The fewer statements in the try block the better. – Do not use new to create an object in a try block. – After the try block, place catch blocks for each type of exception that can be thrown by the statements in the try block. – Place the catch blocks in order from the most specific exception classes to the more general exception classes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Public, Private and Protected • Public members can be used by anyone. • Private members can be used only by methods of the class. • Protected members can be used only by methods of the class or any derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Introducing Design Patterns • General, reusable solutions to common problems or challenges in software design • Programming language independent • Describes the structure, behavior, or interaction of software components for recurring design problems in a proven and efficient manner • Multiple Data Structures working together to solve a problem • Important to understand when to use specific Data Structures (advantages/disadvantages) • 23 classic design patterns, which are categorized into three broad types: • Creation - deal with object creation mechanisms, trying to create objects in a way that suits the situation • Structural - deal with how classes and objects are composed to form larger structures • Behavioral - focus on communication between objects and how they interact with each other; help manage responsibilities between objects and reduce dependencies • Interview questions (Midterm 2 and Final)! Design Patterns - Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software, 1994 Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (Gang of Four)

Data Structures and Design Patterns • Data Structures: • Foundation of Software Development; All engineers who develop software must know Data Structures • Modern software cannot be developed without Data Structures • The grammar of software; all software are based on it for development • Design patterns: • Provide proven solutions to common problems, allowing developers to reuse well-tested approaches • Shared vocabulary for developers, making it easier to discuss solutions to common problems • All software products are based on Design Patterns • All Design Patterns are based on Data Structures • By using standard solutions, code becomes easier to understand and maintain

Behavioral - MVC • Developed at PARC in late 70s • Used by almost all modern UI’s • Separation of Concerns design principle • Distinct sections of functionality • Some common uses: • Web application which updates a database • Mobile applications • Highly used • Data and business logic • Common data structures used: • Classes, arrays • Lists, maps, trees • Stacks, queues, graphs • SQL tables • Rendering data • Common data structures used: • UI components (widgets) • Stacks, queues • Lists, trees • Intermediary for model and view • Common data structures used: • Classes, queues, stacks • maps

Behavioral - Observer • Event pub/sub • Used by modern UI’s to reflect data changes • Observers register with Subject • Subject notifies Observers • Calls update() • Some common uses: • News alerts • Stock alerts • Highly used • Subject (observable) whose state changes • Common data structures used: • Lists, arrays, queues • Set, map • Methods or functions (communications) • Observer (listeners) subscribe to the subject; notified when subject state changes • Common data structures used: • Classes • Methods or functions (event handler or callback when event occurs)

Behavioral - Strategy • Define family of algorithms without changing the objects that use them • Algorithm to execute is chosen at runtime • Need different variants of an algorithm reflecting space/time trade-offs • Some common uses: • Sort is dynamically selected based upon type or size of data • Payment systems (e.g., credit card, PayPal, bank transfer) • Discount calculation • Context (wrapper object) holds reference to strategy (can be dynamically swapped) • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array

Behavioral - Template • Strategy - Polymorphism at its best • Defines skeleton of an algorithm • Some common uses: • Data processing (ETL – extract, transform and load) (e.g., read from database, process data, and write to CSV, JSON or XML) • Document processing (e.g., PDF, Word files, HTML) • Template (algorithm skeleton) • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Stacks, queues

Behavioral - Façade • JDBC, ODBC and DBI are good examples • A is the JDBC, ODBC or DBI façade • B, C, F, and E are different implementations • Allows different clients (i.e., Client1 and Client2) to be written once and access multiple databases • Some common uses: • Database access • User interface design where the façade (wrapper) can be used to simplify user authentication, data retrieval and payment processing, instead if having UI interact with each service • Simplify subsystems in large applications • Provide simplified interface to complex subsystem • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Stacks, queues

Creational - Factory • Factory chooses which subclass to instantiate • Allows for tracking of Object creation • Some common uses: • Centralize logic for creating objects • Document management system that needs to create different document types (pdf, Word, etc.) • Highly used • Create objects without exposing the creation logic • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Interfaces/abstract classes

Creational - Builder • Encapsulate complex object creation • Delegate object creation to Builder • Create object with many attributes or parameters • Some common uses: • Create a document that has same content but different structural representations (e.g., XML, JSON) • Create different representations of an object using the same construction process • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array

Structural - Adapter • Wrapper classes • Converts interfaces • Allows incompatible interfaces to work together • Some common uses: • Integrate legacy systems with new code, e.g., old payment gateway to new user interface • Adapting a json API to an XML API • Converting data formats (e.g., XML to JSON or vice versa) • Enable an interface of a class to be used as another interface; allows incompatible interfaces to work together • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Queues, stacks, buffers

Structural - Decorator • Allows new functionality to be added to an object dynamically • Open/Closed design principle • Open for extension • Close for modification • Extension Options • Inheritance • Is a relationship • Composition • Has a relationship • Note that decorators have Component embedded • Some common uses: • Add borders, shadows or other visual effects in a graphical application • Add logging, caching or transaction management, but don’t want to modify original class implementation • Create flexible and reusable where new functionality can be wrapped around existing behavior in object- oriented way • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, sets • Queues, stacks

Kinds of Trees • General tree – Set T of one or more nodes (not empty) – T is partitioned into disjoint subsets • An n-ary tree – A single node r, the root – n possibly empty sets that are n-ary subtrees of r – Each node can have no more than n children • Binary tree – Set of T nodes – either empty or partitioned into disjoint subsets – Single node r, the root – Two (possibly empty) sets – left and right subtrees © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved A binary search tree is a binary tree that is, sorted according to the values in its nodes. For each node n, a binary search tree satisfies the following three properties: 1. n ’s value is greater than all values in its left subtree TL 2. n ’s value is less than all values in its right subtree TR 3. Both TL and TR are binary search trees.

The Height of Trees • Level of a node, n – If n is root, level 1 – If n not the root, level is 1 greater than level of its parent • Height of a tree – Number of nodes on longest path from root to a leaf – T empty, height 0 – T not empty, height equal to max level of nodes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Recursive definition of height For binary trees, it is often convenient to use an equivalent recursive definition of height: • If T is empty, its height is 0. • If T is a nonempty binary tree, then: o height (T) = 1 + max {height (TL), height (TR)} © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Full, Complete, and Balanced Binary Trees FIGURE 15-7 A complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved A complete binary tree of height h is a binary tree that is full down to level h – 1, with level h filled in from left to right: 1. All nodes at level h – 2 and above have two children each 2. When a node at level h – 1 has children, all nodes to its left at the same level have two children each 3. When a node at level h – 1 has one child, it is a left child

Full, Complete, and Balanced Binary Trees • Full binary trees are complete o Binary tree of height h with no missing nodes. o All leaves are at level h o All other nodes each have two children. • Complete binary trees are balanced o Binary tree of height h that is full to level h – 1 and has level h filled in from left to right. • Balanced binary tree o Binary tree in which the left and right subtrees of any node have heights that differ by at most 1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Maximum and Minimum Heights of a Binary Tree • Binary tree with n nodes – Max height is n • To minimize height of binary tree of n nodes – Fill each level of tree as completely as possible – A complete tree meets this requirement © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overloaded Operators • To overload an operator, you define an operator method whose name has the form: operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload • An overloaded operator has more than one meaning – In class instances, must define new meaning for operators such as the equality operator == – For lists they are equal if they have identical lengths and items © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overloading == for Equality Linked list implementation of overloading == #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; public: LinkedList() : head(nullptr) {} // Overloaded == operator to compare two linked lists bool operator==(const LinkedList& other) const { Node* current1 = head; Node* current2 = other.head; // Traverse both lists and compare the data of each node while (current1 && current2) { if (current1->data != current2->data) { return false; // Lists are not equal if any data doesn't match } current1 = current1->next; current2 = current2->next; } } // Both lists should end at the same time (same length) return current1 == nullptr && current2 == nullptr; } int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(1); list2.insert(2); // Compare lists using overloaded == operator if (list1 == list2) { std::cout << "The lists are equal." << std::endl; } else { std::cout << "The lists are not equal." << std::endl; } return 0; }

Overloading = for Assignment Linked list implementation of overloading = // Without overloaded = operator, you end up with shallow copy #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded assignment operator to perform deep copy LinkedList& operator=(const LinkedList& other) { // Self-assignment check if (this == &other) { return *this; } // First, clear the current list this->clear(); // Deep copy: create a new list by copying nodes from the other list Node* temp = other.head; while (temp) { this->insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return *this; } }; int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); /* Use the overloaded assignment operator to copy list1 into list2 */ list2 = list1; return 0; }

int main() { LinkedList list1; LinkedList list2; // Insert values into list1 list1.insert(1); list1.insert(2); list1.insert(3); // Insert values into list2 list2.insert(4); list2.insert(5); list2.insert(6); /* Use the overloaded + operator to concatenate list1 and list2 */ LinkedList list3 = list1 + list2; // Display the result of concatenation std::cout << "Concatenated List (List 1 + List 2): "; list3.display(); return 0; } Overloading + for Concatenation #include <iostream> struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int value) : data(value), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedList { private: Node* head; public: // Overloaded + operator to concatenate two linked lists LinkedList operator+(const LinkedList& other) const { LinkedList result; Node* temp = head; // Insert all elements from the current list (this) while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } // Insert all elements from the other list temp = other.head; while (temp) { result.insert(temp->data); temp = temp->next; } return result; // Return the concatenated result list } };

Guidelines for Overloading Operators © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Overloading an operator is the same as writing a method whose name is operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload. • Overloaded operators are typically placed in the public section of the class declaration. • When you decide to overload an operator, be sure that the new operation closely matches how the operator is already used. If that is not possible, consider creating a method whose name is not operatorsymbol to perform the operation. • You can overload any C++ operator except . .* :: ?: sizeof • Commonly overloaded operators are the assignment (=), equality (= = and !=), and relational (<, <=, >, >=) operators. • You cannot define new operators by overloading symbols that are not already C++ operators. • You cannot change the standard precedence of a C++ operator. • You cannot change the number of arguments for an overloaded operator, since they represent the operator’s operands. • At least one operand of an overloaded operator must be an instance of a class.

The Quick Sort • Another divide-and-conquer algorithm ⁻ Choose a pivot ⁻ Partition an array into items that are oLess than or equal to the pivot and oThose that are greater than or equal to the pivot ⁻ Recursively apply the same process to the partitioned sub-arrays ⁻ Recursion stops when there is one element remaining in the sub-array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-9 A partition about a pivot © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quick sort algorithm 1. Choose a pivot 2. Partition the array 3. Recursively call

The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1. Choose a pivot 2. Start left to right, find 1st entry that is >= pivot 3. Start right to left, find 1st entry that is <= pivot 4. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 5. Continue left and right searches

The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 6. Find next entry that is >= pivot 7. Find next entry that is <= pivot 8. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 9. Continue left and right searches

The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 10. Find next entry that is >= pivot 11. Find next entry that is <= pivot 12. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 13. place the pivot between the subarrays S1 and S2 by swapping a[indexFromLeft] and a[last]; partition complete 14. Recursively call

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Remove root item (10) from a heap. 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item in the root 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from the root to, at worst, a leaf. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, remove is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations Remove an arbitrary item (9) from a heap (unknown index). 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item with the item that is to be removed 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • Find the arbitrary node which is O(n) • Swap with the last node which is O(1) • Remove the last node which is O(1) • Heapify (up or down) which is O(log n) • Thus, to remove an arbitrary node is O(n + log n), which is O(n). 9 6 3 2 5 10 5 6 3 2 10

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-5 Adding 15 to a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Add an item (15) to a heap (opposite of remove). 1. Insert new item at the bottom of the tree 2. Recursively trickle up to its proper place (the parent of the node in items[i] is always stored in items[(i - 1) / 2] —unless, of course, the node is the root). Add efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from a leaf to the root, at worst. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, add is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.

Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-9 Transforming an array into a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved function buildMaxHeap(array): n = length of array # Step 1: Start from the last non-leaf node and move upwards for i from n/2 - 1 down to 0: # from index 2, 1, 0 heapRebuild(array, n, i) function heapRebuild(array, n, i): largest = i # initialize largest to root leftChild = 2 * i + 1 # index 5, array[leftChild] = 10 rightChild = 2 * i + 2 # none # Step 2: Check if the left child exists and is greater than the current largest if leftChild < n and array[leftChild] > array[largest]: largest = leftChild # Step 3: Check if the right child exists and is greater than the current largest if rightChild < n and array[rightChild] > array[largest]: largest = rightChild # Step 4: If largest is not the current node, swap and heapRebuild the affected subtree if largest != i: swap(array[i], array[largest]) heapRebuild(array, n, largest)

Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */

Heap Sort • Heap sort converts an array into a heap to locate the array’s largest item. This step enables the heap sort to sort an array in an efficient manner. • Heap sort, like merge sort, has good worst-case and average- case behaviors – O(n * log n) // applies to the worst and average cases • Heap sort has an advantage over merge sort in that it does not require a second array. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Evolution of Self-Balancing Trees Chatgpt.com - can you please provide a chronology/evolution of the most common self-balancing trees, including 2-3 and 2-3-4 trees? Year Type Rank (Usage) Key Feature Highlights 1962 AVL Tree 5 • First self-balancing BST • Balances via height difference (balance factor ±1) • Rotation-heavy Early, rigidly balanced binary search tree, often used in compilers or memory managers 1970 2-3 Tree 3 • Multi-way search tree • Nodes can hold 1 or 2 keys (2 or 3 children) • Always height-balanced Used in theoretical data structures, academic applications, some tree-based indexers 1972 B-Trees 2 • Generalized form for databases/disks • Balanced tree for block-based storage Standard in databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite), file systems (NTFS, HFS+) 1973 Red-Black Tree 1 • Simulates 2-3-4 Trees using binary representation • Efficient and widely used Widely used in C++ STL (std::map, std::set), Java (TreeMap, TreeSet), Linux kernel 1974 2-3-4 Tree 4 • Extension of 2-3 trees with 2, 3, or 4 children • Maps directly to Red-Black Trees Extends 2-3 Tree with 4-node capability, often used in databases and file systems 1978 Splay Tree 6 • Self-adjusting, no balance info stored • Frequently accessed elements are faster Used in specialized systems (e.g., caches), but rarely in general-purpose libraries 1989 Treap 7 • Combines BST and heap using random priorities • Probabilistic balancing Used in randomized algorithms or competitive programming; rare in real-world applications 1999 AA Tree 9 • Simplified Red-Black Tree • Easier to implement but slightly less efficient Simplified variant of Red-Black Trees, used in educational contexts or hobby projects 2002 Scapegoat Trees 8 • Alternative balancing schemes • Good worst-case guarantees in certain scenarios Theoretically elegant but rarely used in production due to complex rebalancing 2002 Weight- balanced 10 • Alternative balancing schemes • Good worst-case guarantees in certain scenarios Mostly academic or in specific algorithm design contexts, rarely used in industry

Binary Search Tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_tree • The AVL tree was invented by Georgy Adelson- Velsky and Evgenii Landis in 1962 for the efficient organization of information. It was the first self-balancing binary search tree to be invented. • Various height-balanced binary search trees were introduced to confine the tree height, such as AVL trees (1962), Treaps (1989) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treap), and red– black trees (1978) (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93 black_tree).
AVL Trees • An AVL tree – A balanced binary search tree • Maintains its height close to the minimum • Rotations restore the balance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

AVL Trees – Balance Factor • In a binary tree the balance factor of a node X is defined to be the height difference of its two child sub-trees rooted by node X. • The subtree heights are stored at each node for all nodes in an AVL Tree, and the balance factor is calculated based on its subtree heights to check if the tree has become out of balance. • The height of a subtree is the number of edges between the root node of the subtree and the leaf node farthest down in that subtree. • Balance factor values – 0: The node is in balance. – more than 0: The node is "right heavy". – less than 0: The node is "left heavy". • If the balance factor is less than -1, or more than 1, for one or more nodes in the tree, the tree is considered not in balance, and a rotation operation is needed to restore balance. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVL_tree https://www.w3schools.com/dsa/dsa_data_avltrees.php

AVL Trees – Out-of-Balance Cases Case Description Rotation to Restore Balance Left-Left (LL) The unbalanced node and its left child node are both left-heavy. A single right rotation. Right-Right (RR) The unbalanced node and its right child node are both right-heavy. A single left rotation. Left-Right (LR) The unbalanced node is left heavy, and its left child node is right heavy. First do a left rotation on the left child node, then do a right rotation on the unbalanced node. Right-Left (RL) The unbalanced node is right heavy, and its right child node is left heavy. First do a right rotation on the right child node, then do a left rotation on the unbalanced node. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVL_tree https://www.w3schools.com/dsa/dsa_data_avltrees.php

2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-6 A 2-3 tree of height 3 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-3 tree is a tree in which each internal node (nonleaf) has either two or three children • All leaves are at the same level; the tree is height balanced • Data is kept in sorted order, with left subtree containing smaller values and right subtree containing larger values • A node with two children is called a 2-node • A node with three children is called a 3-node

2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-7 Nodes in a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-node, which has two (2) children, must contain a single data item that is > than the left child’s item and < the right child’s item • A 3-node, which has three (3) children must contain two (2) data items, S and L, that satisfy the following relationship: S is > the left child’s item(s) and < than the middle child’s item(s); L > than the middle child’s item(s) and < the right child’s item(s) • A leaf may contain either one (1) or two (2) data items.

2-3-4 Trees T is a 2-3-4 tree of height h if one of the following is true: 1. T is empty, in which case h is 0. 2. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains one data item b. TL and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. r > TL and r < TR 3. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains two data items; b. TL, TM and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smaller r > TL and r < TM; larger r > TM and < TR 4. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains three data items; b. TL, TML, TMR and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smallest r > TL and smallest r < TML d. middle r > TML and middle r < TMR e. largest r > TMR and largest r < TR © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved r TL TR r TL TR r TL TR TM TMR TML
