Red-Black Trees • 1Invented in 1978 Leonidas Guibas and Robert Sedgewick • Self balancing binary search tree; O(log n) search/add/delete • 2Red-black tree properties – Each node is either red or black – Root node is always black in color – Maintain balance during add/remove operations – Nodes that have no child are considered the internal nodes and these nodes are connected to the NIL nodes that are always black in color. The NIL nodes are the leaf nodes in the Red-Black tree. – If the node is Red, then its children should be in Black color. – Every path from a node to any of its descendant's NIL node should have same number of black nodes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red%E2%80%93black_tree#:~:text=AVL%20trees%20can%20be%20colored,trees%20are%20more%20rigidly%20balanced. 2https://www.javatpoint.com/red-black-tree 3https://www.eecs.umich.edu/courses/eecs380/ALG/red_black.html 3A basic red-black tree

2-3-4 vs Red-Black Trees • A 2-3-4 tree: – Balanced – Add/remove operations use one pass from root to leaf – Requires more storage than binary search tree • Red-black tree – Special binary tree which represents 2-3-4 tree – Advantages of a 2-3-4 tree but requires less storage • Represent each 3 node and 4 node in a 2-3-4 tree as equivalent binary search tree – All child pointers in original 2-3-4 tree are black pointers – Red pointers link 2-nodes that now contain values that were in a 3-node or a 4-node. • Worst case search, insert and delete O(log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

STL Items The STL provides support for predefined ADTs through the use of three basic items: containers, iterators, and algorithms: • Containers are objects, such as a list, vector set, etc., that hold other objects. • Iterators provide a way to cycle through the contents of a container. • Algorithms, such as a sorting algorithm, act on containers. STL containers are implemented as templates, similar to the ADTs implemented in this text. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

STL Containers • Types of containers using STL – Container adapters (Restricted Interface) (e.g., stack, queue and priority queue) • provide efficient, restricted, position-based access to the collection • adapters - use other STL containers to implement their operations – Sequence containers (Ordered Collections) (e.g., array, vector, deque (double- ended queue), list and forward_list) • provide efficient sequential access to the collection – Associative containers (Key-Value Based) (e.g., set, map, multiset, and multimap) • provide efficient key-based access to the collection • Operations common to all STL containers – Constructor, destructor – operator = – bool empty() – unit size() • All STL containers are part of the std:: namespace; precede any reference to the container with std::, unless you use statement using namespace std; © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
C++ Arrays, Dynamic Arrays and STL Arrays

Associative Containers • Associative Containers o store their entries according to search keys o two basic associative containers— set and map set uses the entry to be stored as the key and uses a binary tree for its structure map uses a separate entry as the key o both expect unique keys, and o both store entries in ascending order based on these keys o STL multiset and STL multimap are variations of the STL set and STL map, respectively, that permit multiple occurrences of the search keys © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Associative Containers • Associative Containers o The STL containers unordered_set, unordered_map, unordered_multiset, and unordered_multimap are hash-based implementations of the corresponding ordered STL containers. o Since they are hash based, the unordered associative containers are faster for direct access of an entry based on its search key. o The ordered associative containers are better for iterator-based access. o The STLmap and STL unordered_map containers permit direct access to entries using the [ ] operator. o Generally, the operations of the STL map and its variations have the same behavior as those of the STL set, though the parameters are different since the STL map uses a key for each mapped value stored in the container. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

STL Algorithms • One of the strengths of the STL container library is the set of functions defined in the header. • These functions are designed to operate on ranges of elements in any container with iterators. • This makes possible the comparison of differing types of containers, e.g., the entries stored in a vector can be compared to those stored in a map © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

STL Algorithms • Search and compare algorithms search and compare items in a range; they may change a value, they do not modify the structure of the containers • Sequence-modification algorithms change the entries stored in a container within a range specified by two iterators. The entries can be either – Replaced by a specific value using fill – Copied or moved to new locations using copy or move – Removed based on specified conditions using remove, or – Have operations applied to them using transform • Sort or heap operations on a range of elements in a container. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Raw Pointers • Allocate memory in free store by using new operator – Returns reference to newly created object in memory • Store reference to object in a pointer variable – Use pointer variable to access object • Copy reference to another pointer variable – Creates alias to same object • Use delete operator to deallocate object’s memory – Must also set to nullptr any pointer variables that referenced the object © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Raw Pointers • Need to keep track number of aliases that reference an object … else results in – Dangling pointers – Memory leaks – Other errors (program crash, wasted memory, …) • Languages such as Java and Python disallow direct reference to objects – Use reference counting to track number of aliases that reference an object – Known as the “reference count” • Language can detect when object no longer has references – Can deallocate … known as “garbage collection” © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Smart Pointers • C++ now supports “smart” pointers (or managed pointers; part of C++ standard in 2011) – Act like raw pointers – Also provide automatic memory management features • When you declare a smart pointer – Placed on application stack – Smart pointer references an object  object is “managed” © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Smart Pointers • Smart-pointer templates 1. unique_ptr • no other pointer can reference same object • Automatically deletes the owned object when the unique_ptr goes out of scope 2. shared_ptr • provides shared ownership of object • The object is deleted when the last shared_ptr owning it is destroyed. 3. weak_ptr • reference to an object already managed by a shared pointer … does not have ownership of the object • It does not affect the reference count of the object, so it does not prevent it from being deleted when the last shared_ptr is destroyed © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Using Unique Pointers • A unique pointer … – Has solitary ownership of its managed object – Behaves as if it maintains a reference count of either 0 or 1 for its managed object – Can transfer its unique ownership of its managed object to another unique pointer using method move – Cannot be assigned to another unique pointer © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Unique Pointers Uses • Unique ownership of a dynamically allocated object, ensuring that no other part of the program can accidentally modify or delete the object • Automatic resource management without manually calling delete • Transfer ownership of a resource (e.g., across function calls or between objects) without copying it • Avoid memory leaks and dangling pointers, as std::unique_ptr ensures that the object is automatically destroyed when it goes out of scope © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Using Shared Pointers • A shared pointer … – Provides a safe mechanism to implement shared object ownership – Maintains a count of aliases to an object – Decreases or increases reference count of managed object each time instance is created or goes out of scope or is assigned nullptr – Calls destructor of managed object when reference count reaches 0 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Shared Pointer Uses • Shared ownership: When multiple parts of your program need to share ownership of the same object. • Polymorphism: When you need to manage polymorphic objects, and you want to ensure that the correct destructor is called. • Multi-threading: In multi-threaded programs where multiple threads need to access the same resource. • Lazy initialization or caching: When you need a shared resource that should be created only when needed and destroyed automatically when no longer in use. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Using Weak Pointers • Weak pointer only observes managed object – But does not have ownership – Therefore, cannot affect its lifetime • A weak pointer … – References but does not own an object referenced by shared pointer – Cannot affect lifetime of managed object – Does not affect reference count of managed object – Has method lock to provide a shared-pointer version of its reference (if object has been deleted, it returns empty shared_ptr) – Has method expired to detect whether its reference object no longer exists (return true if object does not exist, else it returns false) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Weak Pointer Uses • Circular Dependencies: To break cycles in reference counting (circular references), where mutual ownership can lead to memory leaks. • Observer Patterns: When you need to observe an object (e.g., a subject) without keeping it alive. • Caching Systems: To track objects in a cache without affecting their lifetime. • Resource Management: When you want to track resources in a system without taking ownership and without preventing their destruction. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overloaded Operators • To overload an operator, you define an operator method whose name has the form: operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload • An overloaded operator has more than one meaning – In class instances, must define new meaning for operators such as the equality operator == – For lists they are equal if they have identical lengths and items © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Guidelines for Overloading Operators © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Overloading an operator is the same as writing a method whose name is operatorsymbol • where symbol is the operator that you want to overload. • Overloaded operators are typically placed in the public section of the class declaration. • When you decide to overload an operator, be sure that the new operation closely matches how the operator is already used. If that is not possible, consider creating a method whose name is not operatorsymbol to perform the operation. • You can overload any C++ operator except . .* :: ?: sizeof • Commonly overloaded operators are the assignment (=), equality (= = and !=), and relational (<, <=, >, >=) operators. • You cannot define new operators by overloading symbols that are not already C++ operators. • You cannot change the standard precedence of a C++ operator. • You cannot change the number of arguments for an overloaded operator, since they represent the operator’s operands. • At least one operand of an overloaded operator must be an instance of a class.

Introducing Design Patterns • General, reusable solutions to common problems or challenges in software design • Programming language independent • Describes the structure, behavior, or interaction of software components for recurring design problems in a proven and efficient manner • Multiple Data Structures working together to solve a problem – Important to understand when to use specific Data Structures (advantages/disadvantages) • 23 classic design patterns, which are categorized into three broad types: – Creation - deal with object creation mechanisms, trying to create objects in a way that suits the situation – Structural - deal with how classes and objects are composed to form larger structures – Behavioral - focus on communication between objects and how they interact with each other; help manage responsibilities between objects and reduce dependencies • Interview questions (Midterm 2 and Final)! Design Patterns - Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software, 1994 Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (Gang of Four)

Behavioral - MVC • Developed at PARC in late 70s • Used by almost all modern UI’s • Separation of Concerns design principle – Distinct sections of functionality • Some common uses: – Web application which updates a database – Mobile applications • Highly used • Data and business logic • Common data structures used: • Classes, arrays • Lists, maps, trees • Stacks, queues, graphs • SQL tables • Rendering data • Common data structures used: • UI components (widgets) • Stacks, queues • Lists, trees • Intermediary for model and view • Common data structures used: • Classes, queues, stacks • maps

Behavioral - Observer • Event pub/sub • Used by modern UI’s to reflect data changes • Observers register with Subject • Subject notifies Observers – Calls update() • Some common uses: – News alerts – Stock alerts • Highly used • Subject (observable) whose state changes • Common data structures used: • Lists, arrays, queues • Set, map • Methods or functions (communications) • Observer (listeners) subscribe to the subject; notified when subject state changes • Common data structures used: • Classes • Methods or functions (event handler or callback when event occurs)

Behavioral - Strategy • Strategy - Polymorphism at its best • Define family of algorithms without changing the objects that use them • Algorithm to execute is chosen at runtime • Need different variants of an algorithm reflecting space/time trade-offs • Some common uses: • Sort is dynamically selected based upon type or size of data • Payment systems (e.g., credit card, PayPal, bank transfer) • Discount calculation • Context (wrapper object) holds reference to strategy (can be dynamically swapped) • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array

Behavioral - Template • Defines skeleton of an algorithm • Some common uses: • Data processing (ETL – extract, transform and load) (e.g., read from database, process data, and write to CSV, JSON or XML) • Document processing (e.g., PDF, Word files, HTML) • Template (algorithm skeleton) • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Stacks, queues

Behavioral - Façade • JDBC, ODBC and DBI are good examples • A is the JDBC, ODBC, DBI or MongoDB C++ Driver façade • B, C, F, and E are different implementations – Allows different clients (i.e., Client1 and Client2) to be written once and access multiple databases • Some common uses: – Database access – User interface design where the façade (wrapper) can be used to simplify user authentication, data retrieval and payment processing, instead if having UI interact with each service – Simplify subsystems in large applications • Provide simplified interface to complex subsystem • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Stacks, queues

Creational - Factory • Factory chooses which subclass to instantiate • Allows for tracking of Object creation • Some common uses: – Centralize logic for creating objects – Document management system that needs to create different document types (pdf, Word, etc.) • Highly used • Create objects without exposing the creation logic • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Interfaces/abstract classes

Creational - Builder • Encapsulate complex object creation • Delegate object creation to Builder • Create object with many attributes or parameters • Some common uses: – Create a document that has same content but different structural representations (e.g., XML, JSON) • Create different representations of an object using the same construction process • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array

Structural - Adapter • Wrapper classes • Converts interfaces • Allows incompatible interfaces to work together • Some common uses: – Integrate legacy systems with new code, e.g., old payment gateway to new user interface – Adapting a json API to an XML API – Converting data formats (e.g., XML to JSON or vice versa) • Enable an interface of a class to be used as another interface; allows incompatible interfaces to work together • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, array • Queues, stacks, buffers

Structural - Decorator • Allows new functionality to be added to an object dynamically • Open/Closed design principle – Open for extension – Close for modification • Extension Options – Inheritance • Is a relationship – Composition • Has a relationship • Note that decorators have Component embedded • Some common uses: – Add borders, shadows or other visual effects in a graphical application – Add logging, caching or transaction management, but don’t want to modify original class implementation • Create new functionality to an object dynamically, without modifying its structure; it extends functionality of a class • Common data structures used: • Classes, map • List, sets • Queues, stacks

Kinds of Trees • General tree – Set T of one or more nodes (not empty) – T is partitioned into disjoint subsets • An n-ary tree – A single node r, the root – n possibly empty sets that are n-ary subtrees of r – Each node can have no more than n children • Binary tree – Set of T nodes – either empty or partitioned into disjoint subsets – Single node r, the root – Two (possibly empty) sets – left and right subtrees © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Kinds of Trees FIGURE 15-4 A binary search tree of names © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved A binary search tree is a binary tree that is, sorted according to the values in its nodes. For each node n, a binary search tree satisfies the following three properties: 1. n ’s value is greater than all values in its left subtree TL 2. n ’s value is less than all values in its right subtree TR 3. Both TL and TR are binary search trees.

The Height of Trees • Level of a node, n – If n is root, level 1 – If n not the root, level is 1 greater than level of its parent • Height of a tree – Number of nodes on longest path from root to a leaf – T empty, height 0 – T not empty, height equal to max level of nodes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Full, Complete, and Balanced Binary Trees FIGURE 15-6 A full binary tree of height 3 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved In a full binary tree of height h, all nodes that are at a level less than h have two children each.

Full, Complete, and Balanced Binary Trees FIGURE 15-7 A complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved A complete binary tree of height h is a binary tree that is full down to level h – 1, with level h filled in from left to right: 1. All nodes at level h – 2 and above have two children each 2. When a node at level h – 1 has children, all nodes to its left at the same level have two children each 3. When a node at level h – 1 has one child, it is a left child

Full, Complete, and Balanced Binary Trees • Full binary trees are complete o Binary tree of height h with no missing nodes. o All leaves are at level h o All other nodes each have two children. • Complete binary trees are balanced o Binary tree of height h that is full to level h – 1 and has level h filled in from left to right. • Balanced binary tree o Binary tree in which the left and right subtrees of any node have heights that differ by at most 1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Maximum and Minimum Heights of a Binary Tree • Binary tree with n nodes – Max height is n • To minimize height of binary tree of n nodes – Fill each level of tree as completely as possible – A complete tree meets this requirement © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Basic Sorting Algorithms • The Selection Sort • The Bubble Sort • The Insertion Sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Selection Sort • Select the largest item – Place in last position • Select the next largest item – Place in 2nd to last position • Repeat for remaining unsorted items – Requires several passes over the data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Selection Sort • Analysis – Selection sort is O(n2) – Appropriate for small n – O(n2) grows rapidly • Could be a good choice when – Data moves are costly, – But comparisons are not © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – Minimal additional space required • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Does not preserve relative order of items with equal keys (not stable) – Potentially suitable for low volume only

The Bubble Sort • Compares adjacent items – Exchanges them if out of order – Requires several passes over the data • When ordering successive pairs – Largest item bubbles to end of the array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Bubble Sort • Analysis – Worst case O(n2) – Best case (array already in order) is O(n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – No additional space required – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Potentially suitable for low volume only

The Insertion Sort • Take each item from unsorted region – Insert it into correct order in sorted region © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 11-3 An insertion sort partitions the array into two regions

The Insertion Sort • Analysis – Worst case O(n2) – Best case (array already in order) is O(n) • Unsuitable for large arrays – Unless already sorted © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – No additional space required – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Potentially suitable for low volume only

Merge Sort • Analysis – O(n log n) – Worse case O(n log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Performs well even on large number of items – Simple to implement – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – Requires additional memory O(n) – Requires copying of entries

The Quick Sort • Another divide-and-conquer algorithm ⁻ Choose a pivot ⁻ Partition an array into items that are oLess than or equal to the pivot and oThose that are greater than or equal to the pivot ⁻ Recursively apply the same process to the partitioned sub-arrays ⁻ Recursion stops when there is one element remaining in the sub-array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Quick Sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved •Pivot selection goal • Subarrays each have the same or nearly the same number of entries •Pivot selection strategy • Last entry • First entry • Middle entry • Median-of-three

The Quick Sort • Analysis – Partitioning is an O(n) task – There are either log2 n or 1 + log2 n levels of recursive calls to quickSort. • We conclude – Worst case O(n2) – Average case O(n log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Divide and conquer algorithm that is efficient on large number of items – No additional memory required • Cons – Worst case O(n2) – Does not preserve relative order of items with equal keys (not stable)

The Radix Sort • Different from other sorts – Does not compare entries in an array • Begins by organizing data (say strings) according to least significant letters – Then combine the groups • Next form groups using next least significant letter © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
The Radix Sort FIGURE 11-13 A radix sort of eight integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Radix Sort • Analysis – Requires n moves each time it forms groups – n moves to combine again into one group – Performs these 2 × n moves d times – Thus requires 2 × n × d moves – More appropriate for chain of linked lists than for an array • Radix sort is of order O(n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Radix sort is of order O(n) – Often faster than merge sort or quick sort for large number of items – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – Time complexity grows linearly with the number of digits – Requires same size keys, with standard way of breaking the keys into pieces

The ADT Heap • A heap is a complete binary tree that either is – Empty or … – Whose root contains a value ≥ each of its children and has heaps as its subtrees • It is a special binary tree … different in that – It is ordered in a weaker sense – it will always be a complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
The ADT Heap FIGURE 17-1 (a) A maxheap and (b) a minheap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Remove root item (10) from a heap. 1. remove the last node of the tree and place its item in the root 2. If it is not a heap, then it is a semiheap. Recursively, trickle down the item in the root until it reaches a node in which it will not be out of place (heapify) Removal efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from the root to, at worst, a leaf. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, remove is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.

Algorithms for Array-Based Heap Operations FIGURE 17-5 Adding 15 to a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Add an item (15) to a heap (opposite of remove). 1. Insert new item at the bottom of the tree 2. Recursively trickle up to its proper place (the parent of the node in items[i] is always stored in items[(i - 1) / 2] —unless, of course, the node is the root). Add efficiency. • This item travels down a single path from a leaf to the root, at worst. • The number of array items it must swap is no greater than the height of the tree. • Thus, add is O(log n), which is in fact quite efficient.
Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-8 Array and its corresponding complete binary tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Transforming Binary Tree to Heap FIGURE 17-9 Transforming an array into a heap © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved function buildMaxHeap(array): n = length of array # Step 1: Start from the last non-leaf node and move upwards for i from n/2 - 1 down to 0: # from index 2, 1, 0 heapRebuild(array, n, i) function heapRebuild(array, n, i): largest = i # initialize largest to root leftChild = 2 * i + 1 # index 5, array[leftChild] = 10 rightChild = 2 * i + 2 # none # Step 2: Check if the left child exists and is greater than the current largest if leftChild < n and array[leftChild] > array[largest]: largest = leftChild # Step 3: Check if the right child exists and is greater than the current largest if rightChild < n and array[rightChild] > array[largest]: largest = rightChild # Step 4: If largest is not the current node, swap and heapRebuild the affected subtree if largest != i: swap(array[i], array[largest]) heapRebuild(array, n, largest)

For each item from heap a. Swap the root (largest item) with last item in the heap Swap(myArray[0], myArray[last]) b. Transform heap region to heap c. Decrement last /* reduce heap region/increase sorted region */

Heap Sort • Heap sort converts an array into a heap to locate the array’s largest item. This step enables the heap sort to sort an array in an efficient manner. • Heap sort, like merge sort, has good worst-case and average-case behaviors – O(n * log n) // applies to the worst and average cases • Heap sort has an advantage over merge sort in that it does not require a second array. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Balanced Search Trees FIGURE 19-1 The tallest and shortest binary search trees containing the same data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If you inserted the items: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 into the tree in ascending order, you would obtain a binary search tree of maximum height If you inserted the items: 40, 20, 60, 10, 30, 50, and 70 into the tree in that order, you would obtain a binary search tree of minimum height
AVL Trees • An AVL tree – A balanced binary search tree • Maintains its height close to the minimum • Rotations restore the balance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

AVL Trees – Balance Factor • In a binary tree the balance factor of a node X is defined to be the height difference of its two child sub-trees rooted by node X. • The subtree heights are stored at each node for all nodes in an AVL Tree, and the balance factor is calculated based on its subtree heights to check if the tree has become out of balance. • The height of a subtree is the number of edges between the root node of the subtree and the leaf node farthest down in that subtree. • Balance factor values – 0: The node is in balance. – more than 0: The node is "right heavy". – less than 0: The node is "left heavy". • If the balance factor is less than -1, or more than 1, for one or more nodes in the tree, the tree is considered not in balance, and a rotation operation is needed to restore balance. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AVL_tree https://www.w3schools.com/dsa/dsa_data_avltrees.php

2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-6 A 2-3 tree of height 3 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-3 tree is a tree in which each internal node (nonleaf) has either two or three children • All leaves are at the same level; the tree is height balanced • Data is kept in sorted order, with left subtree containing smaller values and right subtree containing larger values • A node with two children is called a 2-node • A node with three children is called a 3-node

2-3 Trees FIGURE 19-7 Nodes in a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • A 2-node, which has two (2) children, must contain a single data item that is > than the left child’s item and < the right child’s item • A 3-node, which has three (3) children must contain two (2) data items, S and L, that satisfy the following relationship: S is > the left child’s item(s) and < than the middle child’s item(s); L > than the middle child’s item(s) and < the right child’s item(s) • A leaf may contain either one (1) or two (2) data items.

Searching a 2-3 Tree • Search of a 2-3 and shortest binary search tree approximately same efficiency – A binary search tree with n nodes cannot be shorter than log2(n + 1) – A 2-3 tree with n nodes cannot be taller than log2(n + 1) – Node in a 2-3 tree has at most two data items – Although you might visit fewer nodes when searching a 2-3 tree (vs a binary tree), you might have to make more comparisons at each node • Searching 2-3 tree is O(log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Searching a 2-3 Tree FIGURE 19-10 The trees of Figure 19-9 after adding the values 39 down to 32 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

2-3-4 Trees T is a 2-3-4 tree of height h if one of the following is true: 1. T is empty, in which case h is 0. 2. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains one data item b. TL and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. r > TL and r < TR 3. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains two data items; b. TL, TM and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smaller r > TL and r < TM; larger r > TM and < TR 4. T is of the form a. r is a node that contains three data items; b. TL, TML, TMR and TR are both 2-3-4 trees, each of height h-1 c. smallest r > TL and smallest r < TML d. middle r > TML and middle r < TMR e. largest r > TMR and largest r < TR © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. 2-3-4 Trees • Searching and traversing – Simple extensions of corresponding algorithms for a 2-3 tree • Adding data – Like addition algorithm for 2-3 tree – Splits node by moving one data item up to parent node © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Red-Black Trees • 1Invented in 1978 Leonidas Guibas and Robert Sedgewick • Self balancing binary search tree; O(log n) search/add/delete • 2Red-black tree properties – Each node is either red or black – Root node is always black in color – Maintain balance during add/remove operations – Nodes that have no child are considered the internal nodes and these nodes are connected to the NIL nodes that are always black in color. The NIL nodes are the leaf nodes in the Red-Black tree. – If the node is Red, then its children should be in Black color. – Every path from a node to any of its descendant's NIL node should have same number of black nodes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. 2-3-4 vs Red-Black Trees • A 2-3-4 tree: – Balanced – Add/remove operations use one pass from root to leaf – Requires more storage than binary search tree • Red-black tree – Special binary tree which represents 2-3-4 tree – Advantages of a 2-3-4 tree but requires less storage • Represent each 3 node and 4 node in a 2-3-4 tree as equivalent binary search tree – All child pointers in original 2-3-4 tree are black pointers – Red pointers link 2-nodes that now contain values that were in a 3-node or a 4-node. • Worst case search, insert and delete O(log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Searching and Traversing a Red-Black Tree • A red-black tree is a binary search tree • Thus, search and traversal – Use algorithms for binary search tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

B-Tree • A self-balancing multiway search tree • Designed for efficient data access in large datasets • Common in: – Databases – File systems – Indexing structures

B-Tree • Generalization of 2-3 or 2-3-4 trees • Every node stores multiple keys • Nodes may have multiple children (not just two) • All leaves are at the same depth • Keeps height low, even for large amounts of data • Balanced structure with sorted keys • Keeps disk access minimal

B-Tree • Maximum children per node: m • Maximum keys per node: m – 1 • Minimum children per node: ⌈m/2⌉ • Minimum keys per node: ⌈m/2⌉ - 1 • Root can have fewer keys/children 10 | 20 12 | 17 5 | 6 | 7 30 B-tree of order 4

B-Tree System / Context Typical Order (m) Small in-memory B-trees 4–10 File systems (e.g., NTFS, ext4) 100–200 Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle) 100–1000+ B+ trees in modern SSD-optimized systems 128–512+

B-Tree • Search: O(log n) • Insert/Delete: O(log n) • Few levels = faster disk access • Especially efficient when disk I/O is expensive • Designed for external storage • Minimize disk reads/writes • Store many keys per node → shallow trees • Great for: – Database indexes (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) – File systems (e.g., NTFS, HFS+, ext4)

All rights reserved Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds a minimum spanning tree for a weighted undirected graph. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prim%27s_algorithm) 1. Grow the tree by one edge: Of the edges that connect the tree to vertices not yet in the tree, find the minimum-weight edge, and transfer it to the tree. 3. Repeat step 2 (until all vertices are in the tree).

Traveling Salesperson Problem • Famous problem which has not been solved efficiently. • There are numerous algorithms – Brute Force Algorithm O(n!) • generate all possible city permutations and calculate each route's length – Nearest Neighbor Algorithm O(n2) • starts at a random city and repeatedly visits the nearest city until all have been visited • Approximation – Bellman-Held-Karp algorithm O(2n * n2) • Fastest known algorithm for solving general problem © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved https://www.networkpages.nl/a-big-breakthrough-in-the-euclidean-travelling-salesman- problem/#:~:text=For%20the%20Travelling%20Salesman%20Problem,to%20come%20to%20an%20answer.

Traveling Salesperson Problem • Bellman-Held-Karp algorithm O(2n * n2) • Assume n = 100 cities • Perform roughly 2n * n2 steps to come to an answer. • It takes computer roughly 2100 *10,000=2 * 1033 (a number with 33 digits) steps to give an answer using this algorithm. • Consider you have a 2GHz processor, then this performs 2 * 109 operations per second. • This processor needs 1024 seconds to give an answer to this problem. • How many years are there in 1024 seconds? – ~3.17×1016 years – The age of the universe is estimated to be 13.8 billion years, or ~ 1.38×1010 years. • Companies use algorithms that can solve specific instances of TSP very fast. • They don’t solve the general problem but they use the structure of a specific case to solve it fast. • In practice these algorithms give solutions that are optimal or almost optimal, but that is not guaranteed to be the case. • Fastest known algorithm for solving general problem © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Processing Data in External Storage Chapter 21 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Look at External Storage • External storage – Used when program reads/writes data to/from a C++ file • Generally there is more external storage than internal memory • You can create files for either sequential access or direct access • Many programming languages, including C++, support both sequential access and direct access of files. • Direct access files essential for external data collections © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Look at External Storage FIGURE 21-1 Internal and external memory © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Look at External Storage FIGURE 21-2 A file partitioned into blocks of records © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • The size of a block—that is, the number of bits of data it can contain—is determined by both the hardware configuration and the system software of the computer. • In general, an individual program has no control over this size. Therefore, the number of records in a block is a function of the size of the records in the file.

Look at External Storage • Direct access I/O – Involves blocks instead of records – All I/O is at the block level rather than at the record level. – You can read and write a block of records, but you cannot read or write an individual record. • Buffer stores data (blocks) temporarily • Record updated within block (in buffer) • Work to minimize block I/O – Takes more time for disk access © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-3 Sorting a block of an external file F by merging the results of internal sorts and using two external work files F1 and F2 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-3 Sorting a block of an external file F by merging the results of internal sorts and using two external work files F1 and F2 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • An external file contains 1,600 employee records. • You want to sort these records by Social Security number. • Each block contains 100 records, and thus the file contains 16 blocks B1, B2, and so on to B16. • Assume that the program can access only enough internal memory to manipulate about 300 records (three blocks’ worth) at one time. • The file is far too large to fit into internal memory all at once. • Merge sort - merge two sorted segments—such as arrays—of data records into a third sorted segment that is the combination of the two.

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-3 Sorting a block of an external file F by merging the results of internal sorts and using two external work files F1 and F2 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Read a block from F into internal memory • Sort its records by using an internal sort, and • Write the sorted block out to F1 before you read the next block from F. • After you process all 16 blocks of F, F1 contains 16 sorted runs R1, R2, and so on to R16 • F1 contains 16 blocks of records, with the records within each block sorted among themselves, as Figure 21-3 a illustrates

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-3 Sorting a block of an external file F by merging the results of internal sorts and using two external work files F1 and F2 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Each merge step merges pairs of sorted runs to form larger sorted runs. • With each merge step, the number of blocks in each sorted run doubles, and thus the total number of sorted runs is halved. • Figure 21-3b shows the first merge step merges eight pairs of sorted runs from F1 (R1 with R2, R3 with R4, . . ., R15 with R16) to form eight sorted runs, each two blocks long, which are written to F2.

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-3 Sorting a block of an external file F by merging the results of internal sorts and using two external work files F1 and F2 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • The next merge step merges four pairs of sorted runs from F2 (R1 with R2, R3 with R4, . . ., R7 with R8) to form four sorted runs, each four blocks long, which are written back to F1, as Figure 21-3 c illustrates. • The next step merges the two pairs of sorted runs from F1 to form two sorted runs, which are written to F2. (See Figure 21-3 d.) • The final step merges the two sorted runs into one, which is written to F1. At this point, F1 will contain all of the records of the original file in sorted order

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-4 Phase 2 of an external sort: Merging sorted runs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Merge the runs a piece at a time, due to internal memory limitation • Divide the program’s internal memory into three arrays, in1, in2, and out, each capable of holding 100 records (the block size). • Read block-sized pieces of the runs into the two in arrays and merge them into the out array. • If an in array is exhausted, read the next piece of the run into an in array • When the out array becomes full, write it to one of the files. • To begin merge, start this step with the pair of runs R1 and R2, which are in the first and second blocks, respectively, of the file F1. (See Figure 21-3 a.) • At first merge step each run contains only one block, an entire run can fit into one of the in arrays. You can thus read R1 and R2 into the arrays in1 and in2, and then merge in1 and in2 into out. • However, although the result of merging in1 and in2 is a sorted run two blocks long (200 records), out can hold only one block (100 records). Thus, when in the course of the merge out becomes full, you write its contents to the first block of F2, as Figure 21-4 a illustrates

Sorting Data in an External File Figure 21-4 Phase 2 of an external sort: Merging sorted runs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • The merging of in1 and in2 into out then resumes. • The array out will become full for a second time only after all of the records in in1 and in2 are exhausted. • At that time, write the out contents to the second block of F2. • Merge the remaining seven pairs from F in the same manner and append the resulting runs to F2. • This first merge step is easier than the others, because the initial runs are only one block in size, and thus each can fit entirely into one of the in arrays.

Sorting Data in an External File (cont.) Figure 21-4 Phase 2 of an external sort: Merging sorted runs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • In subsequent merge steps, read the first block of R1 —which is the first block B1 of the file—into in1, and read the first block of R2 —which is B5 —into in2, as Figure 21- 4 b illustrates. • Then, as it did earlier, the algorithm merges in1 and in2 into out. • As soon as you finish moving all of the records from either in1 or in2, you must read the next block from the corresponding run; e.g., if you finish in2 first, you must read the next block of R2 —which is B6 —into in2 before the merge can continue. • Detect when the in arrays become exhausted as well as when the out array becomes full.

Basic Data Management Operations on External Dictionaries Figure 21-5 Shifting across block boundaries © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • One simple dictionary implementation - store the records in order by their search key, perhaps sorting the file by using the external merge sort algorithm developed in the previous section. • Once sorted, you can easily traverse the file in sorted order. • PRO - To perform the retrieval operation on the sorted file, you can use a binary search algorithm. • Number of records in the block is typically small, and thus the time required to scan the block sequentially is insignificant compared to the time required to read the block from the file; common simply to scan the block sequentially • CON - add and remove operations must shift dictionary items; very expensive • Inserting into block k, requires shift in block k, and every block after it.

Indexing an External File Figure 21-6 A data file with an index © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Leave the data file in a disorganized state; maintain an organized index to it • To locate a record in the data file, search the index for the corresponding entry • An index to the data file is simply another file, called the index file, that contains an index record for each record in the data file, just as a library catalog contains an entry for each book in the library. • An index record has two parts: 1. A key, which contains the same value as the search key of its corresponding record in the data file, and 2. A “pointer,” which shows the number of the block in the data file that contains this data record. (This index record’s pointer is an integer, not a C++ pointer.) • You can determine which block of the data file contains the record whose search key equals searchKey by searching the index file for the index record whose key equals searchKey.

Indexing an External File Advantages of an index file • Index record smaller than a data record • Data file need not be kept in any particular order • Possible to maintain several indexes simultaneously; e.g., <name, pointer> and <socSec, pointer> • Index file reduces number of block accesses • Shift index records, not data records © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Indexing an External File Figure 21-7 A data file with a sorted index file © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Index records are far smaller than the data records, the index file contains far fewer blocks than the data file • Performing insertion or removal, you shift only index records; you do not keep the data file in any particular order, so you can insert a new data record into any convenient location in the data file. • You never need to shift records in the data file; shift records in the index file to create an opening for a corresponding index entry in its proper sorted position. • The index file contains many fewer blocks than the data file, so the maximum number of block accesses required is greatly reduced • This scheme is an improvement over maintaining a sorted data file • Better implementations by using either hashing or search trees to organize the index file

External Hashing Figure 21-8 A hashed index file © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • In the external hashing scheme, each entry of table contains a pointer to the beginning of a chain, where each chain consists of blocks of index records, i.e., you hash an index file rather than the data file • Associated with each entry table[i] is a linked chain of blocks of the index file. • Each block of table[i] ’s linked chain contains index records whose keys (and corresponding data records’ search keys) hash into location i. • To form the linked chains, you must reserve space in each block for a block pointer, the integer block number of the next block in the chain

External Hashing Addition when an index file uses external hashing. 1. Add data record into data file. a) The new record can go anywhere you want. b) If a previous removal has left a free slot in the middle of the data file, you can insert it there. c) If no slots are free, you insert the new data record at the end of the last block, or, if necessary, you append a new block to the end of the data file 2. Add corresponding index record into index file. a) Insert into the index file an index record that has key value searchKey and pointer value p (number of the block) b) Because the index file is hashed, you first apply the hash function to searchKey , letting i = h(searchKey) c) Insert the index record <searchKey, p > into the chain of blocks that the entry table[i] points to. d) You can insert this record into any block in the chain that contains a free slot, or, if necessary, you can allocate a new block and link it to the beginning of the chain © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

External Hashing Removal when an index file uses external hashing 1. Search index file for corresponding index record a. apply the hash function to searchKey , letting i = h(searchKey) b. Search the chain of index blocks pointed to by the entry table[i] for an index record whose key value equals searchKey i. If you do not find such a record, the data file does not contain a record whose search key equals searchKey. ii. If you find an index record <searchKey , p>, you remove it from the index file after noting the block number p , which indicates where in the data fi le you can find the data record to be removed. 2. Remove data record from data file a. The data record is in block p of the data file. b. Access this block, search the block for the record, remove the record, and write the block back to the file. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

External Hashing • Operations getItem, add, and remove requires low number of block accesses • Never have to access more than one block of the data file, and at worst you have to access all of the blocks along a single hash chain of the index file • Take measures to keep the length of each of the chains quite short • Make the size of the array table large enough so that the average length of a chain is near one block • Hash function should scatter the keys evenly • Possibly structure each chain as an external balanced search tree • The hashing implementation is preferred when you need to perform retrieval, insertion, and removal operations on a large external dictionary. • This implementation is not practical for certain other operations, such as sorted traversal, retrieval of the smallest or largest item, and range queries that require ordered data. • When these types of operations are added to the basic dictionary operations, you should use a search-tree implementation instead of hashing © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

B-Trees Figure 21-10 An index file organized as a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Each block of the file is a node in the tree and contains three child pointers, each of which is the integer block number of the child. • A child pointer value of –1 plays the role of a null pointer, and thus, for example, a leaf will contain three child pointers with the value –1. • If you organized the index file into a 2-3 tree, each node (block of the index file) would contain either one or two index records, each of the form <key, pointer>, and three child pointers. • The pointer portion of an index record has nothing to do with the tree structure of the index file; pointer indicates the block (in the data file) that contains the data record whose search key equals key. • To help avoid confusion, the pointers in the tree structure of the index file will be referred to as child pointers. • You must organize the index records in the tree so that their keys obey the same search-tree ordering property as an internal 2-3 tree. • This organization allows you to retrieve the data record with a given value in its search key.
B-Trees Figure 21-10 An index file organized as a 2-3 tree © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

B-Trees Adding a record to a B-tree 1. Add data record to data file 2. Add a corresponding index record to index file a. split and merge nodes of the tree just as you do for the internal version © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Removing a record from a B-tree 1. Locate index record in index file 2. Remove data record from data file a. split and merge nodes of the tree just as you do for the internal version

B-Trees • Generalize the 2-3 tree to a structure that is even more suitable for an external environment. • Add more children per node reduces the height of the search tree but increases the number of comparisons at each node during the search for a value. • In an external environment, the advantage of keeping a search tree short far out weighs the disadvantage of performing extra work at each node. • As you traverse the search tree in an external environment, you must perform a block access for each node visited. • Because the time required to access a block of an external file is, far greater than the time required to process the data in that block once it has been read in, the overriding concern is to reduce the number of block accesses required. • Reduce the height of the tree, even at the expense of requiring more comparisons at each node. • In an external search tree, you should thus allow each node to have as many children as possible, with only the block size as a limiting factor. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
B-Trees Figure 21-11 Nodes with two, three, and m children and their search keys © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

B-Trees Figure 21-12 A full tree whose internal nodes have five children © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved This search tree has the minimum possible height, its balance is too difficult to maintain in the face of insertions and removals.

B-Trees © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • B-tree of degree m (number of children nodes) and has the following characteristics: – Require all the leaves of the search tree be at the same level—that is, the tree is balanced – Allow each internal node to have between m-1 and [m /2] + 1 children, and each internal node has one more child than it has records. • An exception to this rule is that the root of the tree can contain as few as one record and can have as few as two children.

B-Trees Figure 21-13 A B-tree of degree 5 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • 5 is the maximum and 3 is the minimum number of children that an internal node—other than the root—in the tree can have • Note: each internal node has between m-1 and [m /2] + 1 children, and each internal node has one more child than it has records (except the root)
Multiple Indexing Figure 21-16 Multiple index files © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Summary • An external file is partitioned into blocks. Each block typically contains many data records, and a block is generally the smallest unit of transfer between internal and external memory. That is, to access a record, you must access the block that contains it. • You can access the ith block of a direct access file without accessing the blocks that precede it. In this sense direct access files resemble arrays. • Before you can process (for example, inspect or update) a record, you must read it from an external file into internal memory. Once you modify a record, you must write it back to the file. • Block accesses are typically quite slow when compared to other computer operations. Therefore, you must carefully organize a file so that you can perform tasks by using only a few block accesses. Otherwise, response time can be very poor. • You can modify the merge sort algorithm, so that it can sort an external file of records without requiring all of the records to be in internal memory at one time. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Summary • An index to a data file is a file that contains an index record for each record in the data file. An index record contains both the search key of the corresponding data record and the number of the block in the data file that contains the data record. • If a record is inserted into or removed from a data file, you must make the corresponding change to the index file. If a data file has more than one index file, you must update each index file. Thus, multiple indexing has an overhead. • You can organize an index file by using either hashing or a B-tree. These schemes allow you to perform the basic dictionary operations by using only a few block accesses. • Although external hashing generally permits retrievals, insertions, and removals to be performed more quickly than does a B-tree, it does not support such operations as sorted traversals or range queries. This deficiency is one motivation for multiple indexing. • You can have several index files for the same data file. Such multiple indexing allows you to perform different types of operations efficiently, such as retrieval by name and retrieval by Social Security number. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Introduction to Relational Databases and Indexing • SQL is a very important language to learn – 90% of Software Engineering jobs require SQL • In the RDBMS – Data are stored in tables – B-trees are called Indexes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Efficient Access to Table Data • Common SELECT looks like this: SELECT <col list> FROM <table> WHERE <condition> E.g. SELECT name, salary FROM Employee WHERE employee_id = 101; • Simplest approach: Scan every row in the table and check if the condition is met (employee_id = 101) – For tables with large number of rows, this will lead to thousands/millions of reads from the disk and will flush the data cache – Highly inefficient

Balanced Tree Index • Not always perfectly balanced • Need to be close to balanced for O(log n) • Flexible if large number of entries per node, then don’t need constant rebalancing – Large key values negate this benefit (i.e., large concatenated column values) • Sometimes, better to just drop the index and recreate it

Inserting a Data Entry into a B- Tree: Redistribution technique Root 19 24 33 2* 3* 5* 7* 14* 16* 19* 20* 22* 24* 27* 29* 33* 34* 38* 39* 14 Root 19 24 33 2* 3* 5* 7* 8* 14* 19* 20* 22* 24* 27* 29* 33* 34* 38* 39* 8 Insert 8*: 1. Search for the correct leaf node L. 2. If N is full then check the immediate sibling leaf nodes (L-1 and L+1). 3. Redistribute the entries. 4. Copy-up the first entry value (smallest value) from the second node of the node- pair under consideration. 16*

Inserting a Data Entry into a B- Tree: Redistribution technique Find correct leaf L for the new data entry. Add the data entry into L: 1. If L has a free slot, then done! 2. Else, must split L (into L and a new leaf node L2) 2A. Redistribute the 2d+1 entries evenly among L and L2 L: old page L2: new page
Root 19 24 33 2* 3* 5* 7* 14* 16* 19* 20* 22* 24* 27* 29* 33* 34* 38* 39* 14 Inserting a Data Entry into a B- Tree: Redistribution technique

Inserting into a B-Tree 1. If the parent node (P) of the leaf node L has a free entry, then: 1. Copy up the smallest key value in the new leaf node, L2, to the parent node P. 2. Also, add a pointer to L2 from the node P. 2. Else, must split the node P into nodes P and P2. (These are non-leaf index nodes.) 1. Sort and compile the 2d+1 entries. 2. Of the 2d+1 entries: Assign the first d to P, and the last d to P2. 3. The one middle key entry is pushed up to the parent of P, along with the pointer to P2. 4. Step 2.3 Is applied recursively if the parent node of P is full. 5. If the root node R is split into nodes R and N then: 1. Add a new root node RNEW, and add pointers from this new root to nodes R and N, which are both internal nodes now. 2. Update the pointer for the root to point to RNEW.

Find block p in the data file into which you can insert the new record, which is any block with a vacant slot or a new block. Insert a corresponding index record into the index file. 1. Insert the index record into the index file, which is a B-tree of degree 5. The first step is to locate the leaf of the tree in which this index record belongs by determining where the search for 55 would terminate 2. Insert the new index record into L, causing it to contain five records

B-Trees (cont.) Figure 21-14 (a through d) The steps for adding 55 to a B-tree; (e) splitting the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Insert a corresponding index record into the index file (cont.) 3. split L into L1 and L2 4. P now has six children and five records, so it must be split into P1 and P2 5. record with the middle key value (56) is moved up to P ’s parent, Q 6. P ’s children must be distributed appropriately, as happens with a 2-3 tree when an internal node is split (Figure 21-14 d)

B-Trees Figure 21-14 (a through d) The steps for adding 55 to a B-tree; (e) splitting the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved An insertion might cause splitting to propagate all the way up to the root (Figure 21-14 e).

B-Trees Figure 21-15 (a through e) The steps for removing 73; (f) removing the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Removal from a B-tree 1. Locate the index record with the desired key value (leaf L). 2. Note value of p (delete data record in subsequent step)

B-Trees Figure 21-15 (a through e) The steps for removing 73; (f) removing the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Removal from a B-tree (cont.) 3. L now contains one value (recall that a node must contain at least two values), and since L ’s siblings cannot spare a value, you merge L with one of the siblings and bring down a record from the parent P (Figure 21-15 c). 4. P now has only one value and two children, and since its siblings cannot spare a record and child, you must merge P with its sibling R and bring a record down from P’s parent, Q . Because P is an internal node, its children must be adopted by R. (See Figure 21-15 d.)

B-Trees Figure 21-15 (a through e) The steps for removing 73; (f) removing the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Removal from a B-tree (cont.) 4. After this merge, P’s parent Q is left with only two children and one record. In this case, however, Q ’s sibling T can spare a record and a child, so you redistribute children and records among T , Q and the parent N to complete the removal. (See Figure 21-15 e.)

B-Trees Figure 21-15 (a through e) The steps for removing 73; (f) removing the root © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Removal from a B-tree (cont.) 5. If a removal propagates to the root, leaving it with one record and two children, you are finished because the definition of a B-tree allows this situation. 6. If a future removal causes the root to have a single child and no records, you remove the root so that the tree’s height decreases by 1, as Figure 21-15 f illustrates. 7. The removal of the index record is complete, and you now must remove the data record, by accessing block p (noted from initial step), delete data record, and write to file.

Data Structure Overview • Provides approach to store, organize and retrieve data efficiently • Basic types: array, linked list • Advanced types: tree, hash table • Certain data structures lend themselves to certain applications • UI that will display 25 rows of data? • Cache search by key (no FIFO)? • Data structures + algorithms = programs

When to use a particular Data Structure • Go over scenarios and decide which Data Structure to use • Scenarios will be from Job Interview Questions • Almost all interviews for software development have Data Structure questions • My personal favorites, as well as interview questions on web • The one skill I want you to develop in this class, is the ability to analyze a problem and determine which Data Structure(s) to use to solve it. • Good for Job Interviews • But, more importantly, it’s good to know for your career

Data Structures • Question will supply a problem • Data Structure will need to be recommended • Algorithm will need to be recommended (i.e., is recursion suitable) • Array • Linked List • Hash Table • Trees
Array • Contiguous area of memory containing multiple data of the same type • Pros • O(1) access by index • Cons • Static data size and expensive to expand
Linked List • Linear data structure consisting of nodes connected by pointers • Pros • Dynamic sizing with insert/update/delete is straightforward • Cons • Finding a particular key is O(n)
Hash Table (Dictionary) • Major components: • Array of buckets • Hashcode to determine index into Array • Collision resolution algorithm • Pros • O(1) access via key • Cons • No ordered retrieval

Tree • Hierarchical structure of nodes • One parent • Zero or more children • BST Pros • Efficient searching • Fast insertion and deletion O(log n) • BST Cons • Memory overhead • Imbalanced trees • Complexity - recursion

Binary Search Tree • Binary Tree Traversal • Preorder • Inorder – sorted order traversal • Postorder • Pseudocode for each of these traversals • Complete/Full tree definitions • Searching Binary Search Tree Algorithm • Pseudocode for searching a BST

Sample Data Structure Interview Questions • What criteria do you use to determine which Data Structure to use? • What Data Structure would use to: • Lookup a unique key as quickly as possible? • Keep a sorted order for data? • What Data Structures supply O(1) access? • What Data Structure(s) would you use for a Cache Manager? • What is the reason for each of the Data Structures?

Cohesion • Each module should perform one well-defined task • Benefits • Well named, self-documenting • Easy to reuse • Easier to maintain • More robust © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Coupling • Measure of dependence among modules • Dependence • Sharing data structures or calling each other’s methods • Modules should be loosely coupled • Highly coupled modules should be avoided © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Class Diagram artifacts • Class block - Name, attributes, methods • Association – solid line with no arrowheads • Multiplicities – numbers at the end of the lines to show cardinality • Aggregation – open diamond • Stand alone, are not joined at the hip; Bank can exist without an Account • Composition – filled-in diamond • Must appear together, Ownership; Room can’t exist without a House

Memory • Memory distribution in a typical operating system process • Stack – the stack is for local variables and for maintaining a record of function calls. The stack grows from the top of memory down towards the heap. • Heap - The heap is for dynamically allocated data items. The heap grows from the top of the static data area up as data items are allocated. • Static Data - This is a block of reserved space in RAM for all the global and static variables from your program. Allocated once and lasts for duration of a program. Stack Free Memory Heap Static Data

Automatic, static, and dynamic data in Java and C++ • A running program typically has 3 kinds of data in it: • automatic • static • dynamic • These kinds of data differ in: • what region of machine memory the data is stored in • when and how memory for the data is allocated (“created”) • when and how memory for the data is deallocated (“destroyed”) • Both Java and C++ have basically these same 3 kinds of data, with some differences • (Note: these are kinds of data, i.e. variables. Executable instructions are stored somewhat differently.)

Automatic data • Automatic data is called "automatic" because it is automatically created and destroyed when your program runs • Examples of automatic data in both Java and C++: formal parameters to functions, local variables in functions (if the variables are not declared static) • Memory for automatic data is allocated on the runtime stack • Memory for automatic data is allocated when execution reaches the scope of the variable’s declaration • Memory for automatic data is deallocated when execution leaves that scope

Static data • Static data is called "static" because it essentially exists during the entire execution of your program • Examples of static data in Java and C++: variables declared static (or, in C++, variables declared outside any function and class body) • Memory for static data is allocated in the static data segment • Memory for static data is allocated when your program starts, or when execution reaches the static data declaration for the first time • Memory for static data is deallocated when your program exits

Dynamic data • Dynamic data is called "dynamic" because it is allocated (and, in C/C++, destroyed) under explicit programmer control • Example of dynamic data in Java: all objects (instances of a class), including arrays. Dynamic data is created using the new operator • Example of dynamic data in C++: any variables created using the new operator. In C++, any type variable can be created using new • Memory for dynamic data is allocated from the "heap" • In Java, dynamic data exists from the time new is called until the object is reclaimed by the garbage collector • In C++, dynamic data exists from the time it is allocated with the new operator until it is deallocated with the delete operator

Dynamic data bugs • Automatic and static data is created and destroyed automatically. As a programmer, you should know how and when this happens, but you do not need to take steps to make it happen • Because the standard C++ runtime environment does not have a garbage collector, both allocation and deallocation of dynamic data must be done under programmer control • This leads to common bugs that can be very hard to track down. • We’ll look at these types of bugs: • Inaccessible objects • Memory leaks • Dangling pointers

Data Structures and Design Patterns • Data Structures are the foundation of Software Development • All engineers that develop software must know Data Structures • Modern software cannot be developed without Data Structures • Data Structures are the grammar of software • Grammar means the basics that all software are based on for development • Products are based on Design Patterns • All software products are based on Design Patterns • All Design Patterns are based on Data Structures
Behavioral - MVC • Developed at PARC in late 70s • Used by almost all modern UI’s • Separation of Concerns design principle • Distinct sections of functionality

Behavioral - Façade • JDBC is a good example • A is the JDBC façade • B, C, F, and E are different implementations • Allows different clients (i.e., Client1 and Client2) to be written once and access multiple databases
Creational - Factory • Factory chooses which subclass to instantiate • Allows for tracking of Object creation

Database Optimization • Table design • High updates on data should have many tables with few columns • High reads on data should have fewer tables with many columns • Index definitions • Balanced Trees (B-trees) • Searchable and join columns indexed

Tough use cases for RDBMS • Multimedia • Streaming audio or video files • Genomic data (DNA) • 3 Billion base pairs composed of 4 letters (ACTG) • Searches for strings like %searchString% • Wildcards at the beginning of search strings • Distributed number of high reads • Billions of users reading billions of posts (social media)
