All Server-Side Machines • Basic Machine Statistics (top, df, ntop) • Look for non-essential processes and kill them • Make sure they don’t come back on a reboot • Memory – verify total memory consumption is NOT close to physical memory (top) • Do NOT make the server swap out • CPU – check which process is consuming the CPU (top) • Should be the server software on the machine • Disk – verify the machine is NOT running out of disk space (df) • Network I/O – verify network I/O is NOT maxed out (ntop) • Write a script that collects this information hourly to a file • Gives a historic view of machine statistics • Zip up older files • Are all machines in layer overloaded or just one server? • For instance, if all machines in the application server layer are maxed out, scale horizontally • If only one machine is maxed out, need to dig deeper into the processes on that one machine
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet

Browser to Web Server Interaction • If all interactions with the server are slow, investigate this interaction • Monitor the client connection and interaction with the web server • Time how long pages take to load on the client • The http access log file shows how long it took in the server to SERVE page • Difference in time is in network transportation • Surprising how often customer complaints are in network transport • Is the client on a slow internet connection • Is the client in a VPN that is slowing down the connection • Verify connectivity speeds of client machine to internet

Stateless Interfaces • Web Server interactions should be stateless • Allows for horizontal scaling • Stateful interfaces locks in user thread to a particular server machine • FTP does this with the current directory • Server machine needs to maintain state (i.e., lots of memory) • Strive for stateless HTTP interfaces

Recognizing Patterns in interactions • Look for patterns in the API interactions • Are department retrievals always followed by employee retrievals • If patterns exist • Consider embedding child objects in the parent tree • Beware of going too deep (generally, one level is good enough)

Apache Access Log • Location of log file depends on installation and is also configurable • Contents in the access log file are also configurable • Configure web server to show the time spent to serve the request • Admin will be able to make this modification • Compare the TOTAL difference in time between client display and web server request times • How many calls are there between client and server to display a page?

Reducing time on Web Server • Network Cache Server optimizes interaction between client and web server • Network Cache Server optimizes static/dynamic pages and streaming media • Home page gets accessed by millions • Serving pages from Network Cache Server reduces calls to web server • Inktomi is one of the leaders in this area
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet

Log messages • Log the Enterprise Context ID (ecid) when entering method • All log messages should be configured with ecid and timestamp • Log ecid, with each message, so messages can be correlated • If debug logging, log the parameters • Do NOT have a generic log(Level.DEBUG, “Entering with id: “ + id); which checks if DEBUG mode is set in the method. • Surround the log() call with a “if (Level.DEBUG) { log(Level.DEBUG, msg); }” • Be careful if parameters have PII data (should have standards on what can be logged) • Log when leaving method • Correlating log file contents via ecid can then be used to determine execution time
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet

Profilers • If possible, attach a profiler to the Application Server process • Possible on development servers, but not production • Profilers give very detailed information (much more than APM) • UI is similar to debuggers • Start the server with profiler flags • From profiler UI, attach the profiler to the server process • Start recording • Perform operation • Stop recording
Profiler (cont)

Application Performance Monitors (APM) • APM can run on production servers with some overhead (<5%) • Depends on the amount of data collected • Relies on injection (code is executed at every method invocation/exit) • Filtering is available so less data is collected and sent • Sends snapshot information to APM server • Also contains the application server statistics (CPU, memory, etc.)
APM (cont)

Log messages • Access to production servers is highly restricted • Cannot attach debugger to production servers, so log messages are critical to backtrack/debug issues • Cannot change jar files on production servers to add debug messages • Environments are updated on a schedule (biweekly or monthly) • Put “enough” debug messages to help track issues • Have admin change the logging level for your package

Log files • Exceptions should not be thrown as part of typical executions (expensive) • Do NOT swallow exceptions (i.e., catch blocks that don’t log messages nor rethrow exceptions) • Log files get cycled (configurable size limit) • Periodically need to check the total file size used (admin scripts) • Analyze logs for WARNING/ERROR messages • Especially for DBMS Transaction rollback • sed, awk, grep, etc., are heavily used • Careful with logging DEBUG messages • Lots of data gets written and slows down the server • If turned on, make sure admin turns off once done
Sample log contents

History Columns • Good to have several history columns to track updates to a row • Username (record who made the last update) • Not so useful if the APPID is always recorded • Try to use the userId • Timestamp (record when last updated) • Version number (counter for number of updates) • High version numbers are indicative of a “hotspot” on the DBMS • Many ORMs support history columns natively

Configure JFRs • Java Flight Recorder files are critical to track down performance issues • JVM needs to be started with JFR enabled • Overhead is “reasonable” and is generally < 1% • Needs a commercial license • JFR files (JFRs) are quite large (depends on configuration) • Generally configured to be dumped hourly • Create one on exit (i.e., server crashed) • Java Mission Control (JMC) is GUI to read/analyze JFRs

JFRs • Events • Duration of wait events (critical sections) • Caches can be hot spot if constantly updated • Sample event to get a snapshot (configurable intervals) • Lots of information available • Memory • Strings are the most allocated object • Investigating String allocations are a waste of time (just too many of them) • Helpful to track down where memory is used • Hopefully, biggest consumers are caches • DB Connections, Threads, Memory close to being maxed out

Hotspots • Places in the code where multiple threads get funneled through • Solution is to parallelize hot spots • Traffic analogies are applicable to some server issues • Think of the Bay Bridge toll plaza • Many lanes to get through the hotspot • A bus can get many people through faster (higher throughput) • An individual time could be slower • Ensure optimizing one operation does not negatively impact other operations
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet

DBMS interactions • Avoid chatty calls into the DBMS • Batch up SQL statements if possible • Instead of looping through multiple “WHERE order_id = ?” statements, use “WHERE order_id IN (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)” • Careful with DBMS interactions in a looping structure • Need to verify that there is a “reasonable” limit to number of times through a loop • Do NOT initialize Connection/Statement in a looping code • Pull any initialization out of the looping code and reuse in the loop • Do a join to get parent/child (1 to many) relationships instead of two calls • Put resultSet.close() and statement.close() calls into finally blocks
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet
Typical Web Architecture Database Application Server Web Server Client/Browser Internet

Automatic Workload Repository (AWR) • AWRs are critical for DBMS performance analysis • Stored as html files • Hourly (default) and stored for 8 days • Can be run at anytime (need admin access) • Top ten runtime queries (overall time, CPU, memory, I/O, etc.) • Top ten most executed queries • This is where PreparedStatements are significant • Do NOT dynamically create statements with primary key searches

Query Optimization • 3rd Normal Form for OLTP tables • Denormalized for OLAP tables • Avoid table scans • Any WHERE condition should have an index on the column • Joins need to have adequate key coverage • Number of tables minus one conditions • For GROUP BY queries, push all possible conditions to WHERE clause versus HAVING clause (HAVING clause should only have aggregate conditions)

Triggers and Stored Procedures • Multiple SELECTs into a sproc • JDBC can detect changed ResultSet • Hibernate (ORM) cannot and will discard results after the first ResultSet • Downsides • Sproc syntax is vendor dependent • Updates to sproc code requires DBA access • Updating customer site can get ugly

Week 1 Page 1 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Arrays, pointers and pointer arithmetic • The interface/implementation distinction in C++ • C++ class templates Data Structures and Algorithm Analysis in C++ : Weiss Ch 1 Thanks to Paul Kube from UCSD for the slides

Pointers in C++ Page 2 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In Java, an object is always referred to via a pointer variable that points to it; on the other hand, a primitive type value is always referred to directly, and never referred to via a pointer variable that points to it • In C++, you have a choice whether to refer to an instance of any type directly, or via a pointer • In particular, C++, you can create a pointer that points to any type you want (even another pointer), and manipulate the values stored in the pointers, and in the memory they point to, in absolute detail • This is an extremely powerful feature of C and C++, and it exposes some of the implementation details of pointers that are abstracted in Java

Contents and addresses Page 3 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Every “memory cell” in a computer has two attributes: • a value (the contents of the cell) • the address of the cell • In a typical machine, the smallest addressable cell contains 8 bits (one byte) • A cell may also have a variable name (identifier) associated with it in a program • you can think of the variable name as an abstraction of the address int a= 5, b= -999; // create 2 integer variables address memory cell identifier 512000 5 a 512004 -999 b

Pointers Page 4 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A pointer is a variable whose value is a memory address • To declare and create a pointer variable in C++, use a declaration statement of the form <typename> * <identifier> • This creates a pointer to a variable of type <typename>, and makes <identifier> the name of this pointer variable For example: int * pt1; // create a pointer-to-int named pt1 int * pt1, * pt2; // create two pointer-to-ints, named pt1, pt2 int * pt1, pt2; // create one pointer-to-int, and one int!!!

Making pointers point Page 5 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The “address-of” prefix operator & applied to a variable returns the memory address of the variable int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // initialize pt1 to point to a address memory cell identifier 512000 5 a 512004 -999 b 512000 pt1 512008 cout << "a is:" << a // displays 5 cout << " &a is: " << &a // displays 512000 cout << " &pt1 is: " << &pt1 // displays 512008 cout << " *pt1 is : " << *pt1 // displays 5 cout << " pt1 is : " << pt1 // displays 512000

An abstract picture of memory Page 6 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A useful abstract way to draw the contents of memory after those statements execute is shown here. The exact value of the address in the pointer is not shown. Instead, the fact that the pointer named pt1 points to the variable named a is shown with an arrow: int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // initialize pt1 to point to a 5 a: -999 b: pt1: • Note: this picture is impossible in Java, which does not allow pointers to point directly to primitive type variables

Dereferencing pointers Page 7 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The “dereferencing” prefix operator * applied to a pointer value “goes to” the location in memory pointed to by the pointer int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // declare and initialize pt1 to point to a cout << *pt1; // print the value of what pt1 points to:5 *pt1 = 10; // assign a value to what pt1 points to cout << a; // print the value of a, which is...10 • In C++ you can have pointers to anything! For example you can create a pointer to a pointer to an int: int ** pp = &pt1; // declare and initialize pp to point to pt1 **pp = 20; // assign a value to what pp points to points to...! cout << a // print the value of a, which is...20

References in C++ Page 8 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Besides having pointers to variables, C++ also permits creating references to existing variables • To declare and create and initialize a reference variable in C++, use a declaration statement of the form <typename> & <identifier> = <existing_variable> • This creates a reference to <existing_variable>, which must be of type <typename>, and makes <identifier> the name of this reference • A reference is an alias: it provides another name for an existing variable • For example: int a = 5; int & b = a; // b and a now both refer to the same variable b = 6; std::cout << a << std::endl; // prints 6

An abstract memory picture of references Page 9 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A reference is an alias: it sets up another name for an existing variable int a = 5; int & b = a; // b is now an alias for a • Note: this picture is impossible in Java, which does not allow multiple names for the same variable • (Java does allow multiple pointer variables to point to the same object, but that is not the same thing at all!) 5 a: b:

Reference function parameters • References are most useful in application to function parameters, where they permit true pass-by-reference semantics, avoiding any argument copying • For example, you can easily write a true swap function using reference parameters: void swap(int& a, int& b) { int tmp = a; a = b; b = tmp; } int x = 5, y = 10; swap(x,y); std::cout << x << ", " << y << std::endl; // prints 10, 5 • Note that the same effect can be had by using pointers, but with messier syntax: void swap(int* a, int* b) { int tmp = *a; *a = *b; *b = tmp; } int x = 5, y = 10; swap(&x,&y); std::cout << x << ", " << y << std::endl; // prints 10, 5 Page 10 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2

The const label in C++ Page 11 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • C++ permits specifying variables (including formal parameters to functions), and member functions of classes, as const • This can be used in several ways. Examples: • Declaring a variable to be const means that its value cannot change; therefore it must be given a value in its declaration: const double TEMP = 98.6; // TEMP is a const double ( Equivalent: double const TEMP = 98.6; ) • Declaring a pointer to be a pointer to a const means that the pointer can’t be used to change the value of anything it points to: int const * ptr; // ptr is a pointer to a const int ( Equivalent: const int * ptr; ) • (More on const-labeled parameters and member functions later.)

Reading C++ type declarations Page 12 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • C++ type declarations can be more easily understood when read ‘backwards’, that is right to left, keeping in mind that * means ‘pointer to’ and & means ‘reference to’: int * p // p is a pointer an int int const * p // p is a pointer to a const int // which means the same thing as... const int * p // p is a pointer to an int that is const int * const p // p is a const pointer to an int int const * const p // p is a const pointer to a const int // which means the same thing as... const int * const p // p is a const pointer to an int that is const int & p // p is a reference an int int const & p // p is a reference to a const int // which means the same thing as... const int & p // p is a reference to an int that is const • Note that * and & mean something different if they occur in expressions instead of type declarations: • In expressions, these are unary prefix operators! • * is the pointer dereference operator, and & is the address-of operator

Pointers to const, and const pointers Page 13 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Creating a pointer to a const... const int c = 3; // create a const int variable // equivalent: int const c = 3; const int * p; // create a pointer to a const int // equivalent: int const * p; p = &c; // make p point to c *p = 4; // ERROR: p is pointer to const • Creating a const pointer... int d = 5; // create an int variable int * const q = &d; // create and initialize a const ptr to int *q = 9; // okay... changing what q points to q = &d; // ERROR: q itself is const!

Pointers to consts and const pointers... Page 14 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 const int d int c = 5; = 3; // create a const int variable // create an (ordinary, non-const) int variable const int * p; // create a pointer to a const int int * const int * r; q; // // create create a a const pointer to an int (VS diff) pointer to an int p = &c; // okay: p is pointer to const p = &d; // okay: increasing “constness” *p = 9; d = 9; // // ERROR: okay can’t use p to change a value r = &d; // okay: r is pointer to an (ordinary) int r = &c; // ERROR: decreasing “constness” (value of //type const int can’t be assigned //to entity type of int) q = &c; // ERROR: can’t assign to const pointer (VS) const int * const s = &c; // create and initialize // a const ptr to a const int

References to const, and const references Page 15 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Creating a reference to a const... const int c = 3; // create a const int variable // equivalent: int const c = 3; const int & r = c; // create and initialize reference to it // equivalent: int const & r = c; r = 4; // ERROR: r is reference to const • Creating a const reference... • Actually, all references are const, in that the alias is established when the reference is created, and cannot be changed later int d = 5, e = 6; // create int variables int & s = d; // create and initialize a reference to d s = e; // // // just changes the value of what s refers to d, s are still aliases of the same variable which now has value 6

Pointer arithmetic in C++ Page 16 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • These arithmetic operators are defined on pointer arguments: ++, --, +, -, +=, -= • A pointer variable stores an integer number, which is a memory address • However, arithmetic operations on a pointer variable do not necessarily change that number in the way you would expect • For example, if p is a pointer, p++ does not necessarily make the number stored in p one larger than it was before • ... it makes the number stored in p larger by an amount equal to sizeof(*p)

Pointer arithmetic, cont’d Page 17 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Following our example... int a=5, b= -999; int* pt1 = &a; // pt1 contains address 512000 pt1++; // now pt1 contains address 512004...(VS) *pt1 = 10; // assign a value to what pt1 points to cout << b; // print the value of b, which is... pt1 = pt1 + 1; // now pt1 contains address 512008 (VS) cout << *pt1; // print the value at this address pt1 = pt1 - 2; // now pt1 contains address 512000 again

Pointer arithmetic, cont’d again Page 18 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • When you add an integer to a pointer in C++, the result is of the same type as the pointer, and the increment is the integer times the size of the object the pointer is declared to point to • When you subtract one pointer from another in C++, the result is of integer type, and is equal to the number of objects of type the pointers are declared to point to that would fit between where the two pointers point (fractional part discarded) • Adding two pointers is illegal (and it doesn’t make any sense to do it) double* p1; double* p2; p1 - 3 // // // the address of before the one double* valued the 3rd “double” cell in memory p1 points to... this is a expression! p1 - p2 // // // the number of double-sized cells in memory between the one p2 points to and the one p1 points to... this is an int-valued expression! p1 + p2 // illegal

Other operators and pointers... Page 19 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • These relational operators are defined for pointer arguments: ==, <, <=, >, >= • Pointer literal constants: in C++ 2011, nullptr is the “null pointer” of any type • the integer literal constant 0 can also be used, but is not now considered good style • with #include <cstddef>, the identifier NULL can also be used, though that is not good C++ style either • note that in a boolean context, the null pointer is interpreted as "false", and any other pointer value is interpreted as "true" • Unary ! operator: For any pointer expression p, !p is 1 if p is the null pointer; else 0

Arrays and pointers in C and C++ Page 20 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In C or C++, an array name acts like a const pointer to the first element of the array • C arrays are always contiguous in memory: second element is right after the first, etc. int a[99]; int* p; // create an array of 99 ints // create a pointer-to-int p = a; // makes p points to the first element of a p = & a[0]; // this also makes p point to first element of a a = p; // ERROR: can’t assign to an array name, it’s const p[3] // array subscripts work on pointers... // this is exactly the same as *(p + 3) *(a + 3) // and pointer arithmetic works on array names... // this is exactly the same as a[3] // which is also exactly the same as 3[a] // (believe it or not!)

Pitfalls with arrays and pointers in in C++ Page 21 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • With a declaration like int a[100]; expressions like a[-33] or a[101] or *(a + 10000) are very dubious, to say the least • However, these are not compiler errors, and may not even give you runtime errors (unless they involve accessing protected memory) • The power of pointer arithmetic can hurt you, if you’re not careful... • For these reasons, though C-style arrays are available in C++, it is advisable to use data structures from the Standard Template Library (STL) such as std::vector instead of basic arrays

Strings as arrays of chars in C++ Page 22 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 ’\0’ used to terminate the string • As in C, an array of char elements with an element can be used as a string in C++ • ’\0’ is the null character, an 8-bit integer with value 0 • to contain a string, a char array must contain a null character • the null character is used to determine the end of the string contained in the array • A null-terminated array of char can be initialized with a string literal: char s[] = "hello"; • this creates an array s with 6 elements, not 5! • the null character is included, as the last element of s • this is equivalent to char s[] = {’h’,’e’,’l’,’l’,’o’,’\0’}; or char s[6] = "hello";

Dealing with strings in C++ Page 23 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Since in C++ a null-terminated char array (like any array) does not keep information about its own length, extra checking must be done to make sure all accesses to the string are within bounds • This checking is tedious to do, but if not done, leads to serious bugs and exploitable code • Most software security problems are due to unchecked array bounds violations (buffer overflows) in C and C++ programs! • Again, in C++, it is advisable to use data structures from the Standard Template Library (STL): instead of null-terminated char arrays, use std::string for strings!
Appendix Page 24 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2

Writing, compiling, and running programs in Java and C++ Page 25 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The traditional first example when learning a programming language is the simplest possible program that performs some output: a console application that says “Hello world!” • Let’s look at and compare Java and C++ “Hello world” programs • In Java: public class HelloWorld { public static void main (String args[]) { System.out.println("Hello World!"); } }

Compiling and running “Hello World” in Java Page 26 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In Java , all code must be inside the body of a class definition; in this case, there is just one class, a public class named HelloWorld • A public class definition has to appear in a file with the same name as the class, and a .java extension; in this case, HelloWorld.java • The public class HelloWorld contains a public static method named main that takes an array of Strings as argument • That main method is the entry point when HelloWorld is run as a program • From the command line, compile a Java source code file using the javac compiler: javac HelloWorld.java • ... which will produce a file HelloWorld.class of Java Virtual Machine bytecodes • Run the program by running the java interpreter, telling it the name of the class you want to run as a program: java HelloWorld • ... which will print to standard output: Hello World!

“Hello World” in C++ Page 27 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A simple version of a “Hello World” console application in C++: #include <iostream> int main() { std::cout << "Hello World!" << std::endl; return 0; }

Compiling and running “Hello World” in C++ Page 28 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In C++ , code can appear outside a class definition (as in that example) • In C++, there is no requirement about the name of a source code file, but it is conventional to use a .cpp extension; for example, hw.cpp • A C++ program must contain exactly one function named main which returns an int • That main function is the entry point of the program • From the command line, compile a C++ source code file using the g++ compiler: g++ hw.cpp • ... which will produce a file a.out of executable native machine code • Run the program: a.out • ... which will print to standard output: Hello World!

Parts of the hello world program in C++ Page 29 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 #include <iostream> • This #include directive tells the compiler to read in the contents of the system header file iostream at this point. This file contains declarations of variables and functions necessary for doing I/O in C++. int main () { • To run, every C++ program must contain a definition of a function named “main”. When the program starts, the operating system calls this function. main() is declared to return an int value. Here, we are not using any arguments to main, but you can, if you want to process command line arguments. std::cout << "Hello World!" << std::endl; • Send the string “Hello World!” to standard output, followed by end-of-line. << is the bitwise left-shift operator, overloaded to implement stream I/O. The std:: is a namespace qualifier (put using namespace std; near the top of the file to avoid it). return 0; • Since main is declared to return an int, we better do so. Returning from main ends your program; it is traditional to return 0 if it is terminating normally, and to return a nonzero value if it is terminating because of some error condition.

The C++ compiler Page 30 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • We will use the GNU C++ compiler, g++ • This is a very good C/C++ compiler, freely available for many platforms • (Other C++ compilers may differ in some features) • If you have a .cpp file that contains a definition of main(), and that uses only functions defined in it or included from the standard libraries, you can just compile it as was shown, creating an executable file named a.out • If you want another name for the resulting executable, you can use the -o flag, with the name you want. This will compile hw.c and create an executable named hello: g++ hw.c -o hello • If you want to include symbolic information in the executable file which is useful for debugging, add the -g flag: g++ -g hw.c -o hello • If your C++ program involves several files, you can compile them separately, and link them to produce an executable file. More on that later!

Boolean expressions in C++, cont’d Page 31 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The fact that integer values are interpreted as booleans means you can write some terse (and maybe hard to understand) code in C++. For example, in the recursive version of factorial, you could write: // precondition: n >= 0 int factorial(int n) { if(n) return (n * factorial(n-1)); else return 1; } • It also means you can introduce hard-to-see bugs. What does the following version of factorial do (note this will compile in C++ but not Java)? // precondition: n >= 0 int factorial(int n) { if(n=0) return 1; else return (n * factorial(n-1)); }

Separate compilation and linking in C++ Page 32 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The source code files for a module can be compiled into an "object" file , with .o extension • (One or more .o files can be archived into a single library file; see man ar) • A client program wishing to use the module must then #include the module’s .hpp header file, be compiled and linked to the module’s .o file or library • How to do that?

Separate compilation and linking with g++ Page 33 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • To compile a C++ source code file and produce an object file (not a runnable program) you specify the "-c" flag to the compiler • Suppose there is a graphics module, implemented in a file graphics.cpp. It doesn’t contain a definition of main(), so it can’t produce a runnable program, but we we can compile it and produce an object file: g++ -c graphics.cpp compiles module graphics.cpp, producing object file graphics.o • Then a client program that uses the graphics module (and #includes graphics.hpp), and that defines main() can be written and also separately compiled: g++ -c client.cpp compiles client.cpp, producing object file client.o • To produce a runnable program, you need to link together all the needed object files. The C compiler can perform this linking step. At the same time, you can use the "-o" flag to give the result a different name than a.out: g++ client.o graphics.o -o cmd links object files, producing executable cmd

C++ Functions • An argument passed by value is copied within the function. • The function cannot alter the arguments passed in. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Functions • An argument passed by reference is not copied but is accessed directly within the function. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved void computeMax(int x, int y, int& larger) // void function does not return a value { larger = ((x > y) ? x : y): } // end computeMax int main () { int a = 0; int b = 0; int largerAB = 0; cin >> a >> b; computeMax(a, b, largerAB); cout << "The larger of " << a << " and " << b << " is " << largerAB << ".\n"; }

C++ Passing Arrays to a Functions • Arrays are always passed by reference to a function. • Common ways to pass arrays to function: 1. void modifyArray(int arr[]) // same as #2 2. void modifyArray(int *arr) // same as #1 3. void modifyPointer(int*& arr) // allows you modify array elements and pointer itself; useful when you want to change memory location © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. C++ Vectors • A vector is similar to a one-dimensional array. • an object of a standard C++ class named vector • is a part of the Standard Template Library , or STL • The STL is a collection of data types you can use in your programs. • These data types are not part of the official C++ language, but they have been added to the built-in data types. • To use a vector in your program, you must begin it with the following statements: #include <vector> using namespace std; © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Strings • The C++ Standard Library provides the data type string. To use this library, you include the statements #include <string> using namespace std; • You can concatenate two strings to form another string by using the + operator. • You can manipulate a portion of a string by using the method substr(position, length ) • The first argument specifies the position of the beginning of the substring (remember that 0 is the position of the first character in the string). The second argument is the length of the substring. For example, (e.g., title = "J Perfect's Diary"); title.substr(2, 7) • is the string “ Perfect ”. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • Choose identifiers that describe their purpose—that is, that are self-documenting. Distinguish between keywords, such as int, and user-defined identifiers. • Use a good indentation style to enhance the readability of a program. The layout of a program should make it easy for a reader to identify the program’s modules. • Use a blank line between method or function definitions. • Within these definitions, you should indent individual blocks of code. These blocks are generally—but are not limited to—the actions performed within a control structure, such as a while loop or an if statement. • Named constants make a program easier to modify, e.g., const int NUMBER_OF_MAJORS = 202; • typedef statements make a program easier to modify; if your program performs floating point computations, and you need greater precision than float (change float to double) • typedef float RealType; // original statement • typedef long double RealType; // update to this statement in one spot © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • Two kinds of testing can be applied to a system: • Open-box testing (white-box or glass-box testing) - design tests knowing how the modules work. • choose a method’s actual arguments in a way that exercises all lines of code in the method. • make sure that all branches of if and switch statements are executed. • Closed-box testing (black-box or functional testing) - you know the name of the method being tested, as well as its argument list, its return type, and its operation contract. • develop tests across a range of valid input, and then check the output against hand-calculated results. • Choose arguments outside of their valid range tests how the module handles incorrect input. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Points • Header (.h ) files in C++ provide a mechanism to partially separate the design of a class from the implementation in the source , or implementation (.cpp ), file. • The header file must also contain a description of the data fields for the class and any private methods used by the class • Elements of the class • Private data fields // good practice • restricts access to the data field to just the class in which it is defined • Typically, provide methods—such as setItem (setters) and getItem (getters)— to access the data fields • Class controls how and whether other classes can access the data fields • If a derived class might need to access or modify the data fields of the base class, you should still make the data fields private but provide protected methods so that any derived classes can access or modify the data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Inheritance • Inheritance allows us to reuse and extend work we have already completed and tested. • You can think of a base class as a parent class. • base class = superclass = parent class • You can think of a derived class as a child class • derived class = subclass = child class • Any instance of the derived class is also considered to be an instance of the base class and can be used in a program anywhere that an instance of the base class can be used • When the keyword public is used with the base class, any of the publicly defined methods or data fields in the base class can be used by instances of the derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ derived class • A derived class • has the data fields and methods of the base class in addition to the members it defines, • it can access only publicly defined members from the base class • can also revise any inherited public method. • contains copies of inherited data fields, • does not contain a copy of the code for inherited methods • does not inherit constructors from parent class, but it can use it or other parent method to initialize the private data fields © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Overriding Base-Class Methods • You can add as many new members to derived class as desired • You can redefine inherited methods • Called overriding a base-class method. • A method overrides a base-class method when • The two methods have the same name and parameter declarations • Precede a method name with the base-class namespace indicator to tell the compiler to use the base-class version of the method • Without a namespace indicator in front of the method name, the compiler assumes that the programmer is referring to the current class and inserts code to call that version of the method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Virtual Methods, Abstract Classes • Using keyword virtual in front of the prototype • Tells the C++ compiler that the code this method executes is determined at runtime • Allows an application using our class to take advantage of polymorphism when the methods are invoked • Pure virtual method • Virtual method that has no implementation • Abstract class • Has at least one pure virtual method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Major Concepts for Week 3 • Encapsulation • Same concept as in Java • Polymorphism • Key difference • Memory Management • Many differences

Introduction to C++ • C++ is an object-oriented language based on C • C++ was developed by Bjorne Stroustrap at Bell Labs (1980’s) • CSC 340 doesn’t assume experience in C or C++ • CSC 340 does assume experience with Java

C++ Syntax Multi-line comments start with /* and end with */ // variables begin with a letter or underscore // constants do not change // syntax structure allowing grouping of data & methods // main must be global and return int
C++ Functions • An argument passed by value is copied within the function. • The function cannot alter the arguments passed in. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Functions • An argument passed by reference is not copied but is accessed directly within the function. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved void computeMax(int x, int y, int& larger) // void function does not return a value { larger = ((x > y) ? x : y): } // end computeMax int main () { int a = 0; int b = 0; int largerAB = 0; cin >> a >> b; computeMax(a, b, largerAB); cout << "The larger of " << a << " and " << b << " is " << largerAB << ".\n"; }

C++ Functions • A constant reference parameter is a reference parameter that is tagged as const. • The function uses the actual argument that is passed to such a parameter, not a copy of it, yet cannot modify it. • For example, for the function f that begins void f( const int& x, int y, int& z) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved x is a constant reference parameter

C++ Passing Arrays to a Functions • Arrays are always passed by reference to a function. • Common ways to pass arrays to function: 1. void modifyArray(int arr[]) // same as #2 2. void modifyArray(int *arr) // same as #1 3. void modifyPointer(int*& arr) // allows you modify array elements and pointer itself; useful when you want to change memory location © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

By Reference or By Value? • Parameters are passed by value • Copy of the parameter is made on stack • If the parameter is a class, it’s by value (class is copied) • If the parameter is a pointer/address, pointer itself is still by value (i.e., pointer is copied) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Vectors • A vector is similar to a one-dimensional array. – an object of a standard C++ class named vector – is a part of the Standard Template Library , or STL • The STL is a collection of data types you can use in your programs. • These data types are not part of the official C++ language, but they have been added to the built-in data types. • To use a vector in your program, you must begin it with the following statements: #include <vector> using namespace std; © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Vectors • You can declare a vector in one of three ways: 1. Place the type of data it will hold in angle brackets and the number of elements in parentheses: a) vector<double> firstVector(10); // Vector to hold 10 doubles b) vector <string> myVector(12); // Vector to hold 12 strings c) The size you specify when declaring the vector is only its initial size. A vector can grow in size when you add entries. 2. You can place initial values into a vector when you declare it, by writing a second argument: a) vector<int> intVector(5, -1); // Vector to hold 5 integers, initialized to - 1 b) When the elements in the vector are allocated, they are given the value of the second argument. 3. You can also create an empty vector—a vector with no elements—by omitting its size and the parentheses: a) vector<char> letterVector; // An empty vector of characters © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Vectors • Using [] stores a value in an existing element, the same way when using an array. double x = firstVector[5]; // Places sixth entry in x myVector[3] = "This is a sample string."; // Sets fourth entry's value • If you are not sure how many elements the vector has, you can call the method size, as in the following example: cout << intVector.size() << endl; // Displays the number of elements in intVector • If vector is full, we could use the push_ back method to add additional values: firstVector.push_back(2.3); // Grow vector and store value 2.3 firstVector.push_back(3.4); // Grow vector and store value 3.4 • At this point, calling the size method would be updated, since two additional elements have been added to the vector. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Vectors • You also can reduce the size of a vector by removing either its last element or all of its elements. • To remove only the last element, you can use the pop_back method. • This method shortens the vector but does not give you the entry in the removed element. • You must save that entry before calling pop_back. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Strings • The C++ Standard Library provides the data type string. To use this library, you include the statements #include <string> using namespace std; • You can concatenate two strings to form another string by using the + operator. • You can manipulate a portion of a string by using the method substr(position, length ) • The first argument specifies the position of the beginning of the substring (remember that 0 is the position of the first character in the string). The second argument is the length of the substring. For example, (e.g., title = "J Perfect's Diary"); title.substr(2, 7) • is the string “ Perfect ”. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Classes • In C++, a class has the following form: • By default, all members in a class are private —they are not directly accessible by any program that uses the class—unless you designate them as public. However, explicitly indicating the private and public portions of a class is a good programming practice. • An object is an instance of a class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Constructors and Destructors • Classes have special methods, called constructors and destructors, for the creation and destruction of its objects. • A constructor creates and initializes new objects, or instances, of a class. • A destructor destroys an instance of a class, when the object’s lifetime ends. • A typical class has several constructors, but only one destructor. For many classes, you can omit the destructor. In such cases, the compiler will generate a destructor for you. • In C++, a constructor has the same name as the class. Constructors have no return type—not even void —and cannot use return to return a value. Constructors can have arguments. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Constructors and Destructors • The first constructor in Sphere is the default constructor : Sphere(); • A default constructor by definition has no arguments. Typically, a default constructor initializes data members to values that the class implementation chooses. For example, the implementation • sets theRadius to 1.0. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Constructors and Destructors • When you declare an instance of the class, you implicitly invoke a constructor. For example, the statement Sphere unitSphere; • invokes the default constructor, which creates the object unitSphere and sets its radius to 1.0. Notice that you do not include parentheses after unitSphere. • The next constructor in Sphere is Sphere(double initialRadius); • It creates a sphere object of radius initialRadius. This constructor needs only to initialize the private data member theRadius to initialRadius. Its implementation is Sphere::Sphere(double initialRadius) { theRadius = initialRadius; } // end constructor • You implicitly invoke this constructor by writing a declaration such as Sphere mySphere(5.1); • Typically, you place the implementation of a class’s constructors and other methods in an implementation file whose name ends in .cpp. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
End Appendix A © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

A function is a construct that contains statements. 5.Every statement in C++ must end with a semicolon (;), known as construct that contains statements. 6.In C++, a comment is preceded by 2 forward slashed (//) on a line, called a line comment, or enclosed between /* and */ on one or several lines, called a block comment or paragraph comment. 7.Keywords, or reserved words, have a specific meaning to the compiler and cannot be used in the program for other purposes. Examples of keywords are using, namespace, int, and return. 8.C++ source programs are case sensitive. 9.You can develop C++ applications from the command window or by using an IDE such as Visual Studio or Xcode. 10.Programming errors can be categorized into 3 types: syntax errors, runtime errors, and 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 20 C++ Basics

Key Points 1.The cin object along with the stream extraction operator (>>) can be used to read an input from the console. 2.Identifiers are names for naming elements in a program. An identifier is a sequence of characters that consists of letters, digits, and underscores (_). An identifier must start with a letter or an underscore. It cannot start with a digit. An identifier cannot be a reserved word. 3.Choosing descriptive identifiers can make programs easy to read. 4.Declaring a variable tells the compiler what type of data a variable can hold. 5.In C++, the equal sign (=) is used as the assignment operator. 6.A variable declared in a function must be assigned a value. Otherwise, the variable is called uninitialized and its value is unpredictable. 7.A named constant or simply constant represents permanent data that never changes. 8.A named constant is declared by using the keyword const. 9.By convention, constants are named in uppercase. 10.C++ provides integer types (short, int, long, unsigned, short, unsigned int, and unsigned long) that represent signed and unsigned integers of various sizes. 11.Unsigned integers are nonnegative integers. 12.C++ provides floating-points types (float, double, and long double) that represent 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 21 C++ BASICS

Key Points 13.C++ provides operators that perform numeric operations: + (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), / (division), and % (modulus). 14.Integer arithmetic (/) yields an integer result. 15.In C++, the % operator is for integers only. 16.The numeric operators in a C++ expression are applied the same way as in an arithmetic expression. 17.The increment operator (++) and the decrement operator (--) increment or decrement a variable by 1. 18.C++ provides augmented operators += (addition assignment), -= (subtraction assignment), *= (multiplication assignment), /= (division assignment), and %= (modulus assignment). 19.When evaluating an expression with values of mixed types, C++ automatically casts the operands to appropriate types. 20.You can explicitly cast a value from one type to the other using the <static_cast>(type) notation or the legacy c-style (type) notation. 21.In computer science, midnight of January 1, 1970 is known as the UNIX epoch. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 22 C++ BASICS

Key Points 1.A bool type variable can store a true or false value. 2.Internally, C++ uses 1 to represent true and 0 for false. 3.If you display a bool value to the console, 1 is displayed I the value is true and 0 if the value is false. 4.In C++ you can assign a numeric value to a bool variable. Any nonzero value evaluates to true and zero value evaluates to false. 5.The relational operators (<, <=, ==, !=, >, >=) yield a Boolen value. 6.The quality testing operator is two equal signs (==), not a single equal sign (=). The later symbol is for assignment. 7.Selection statements are used for programming with alternative courses of actions. There are several types of selection statements: if statements, two-way if-else statements, nested if statement, multi-way if-else statements, switch statements, and conditional expressions. 8.The various if statements all make control decisions based on a Boolean expression. Based on the true and false evaluation of the expression, these statements take one of two possible courses. 9.The Boolean operators &&, ||, and ! Operate with Boolean values and variables. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 23 C++ BASICS

Key Points 10.When evaluating p1 && p2, C++ first evaluates p1 and then evaluates p2 if p1 is true; if p1 is false, it does not evaluate p2. 11.When evaluating p1 || p2, C++ first evaluates p1 and then evaluates p2 if p1 is false; if p1 is true, it does not evaluate p2. 12.Therefore, && is referred to as the conditional or short-circuit AND operator, and || is referred to as the conditional or short-circuit OR operator. 13.The switch statement makes control decisions based on a switch expression. 14.They keyword break is optional in switch statement, but it is normally used at the end of each case in order to skip the remainder of the switch statement. If the break statement is not present, the next case statement will be executed. 15.The operators in expressions are evaluated in the order determined by the rules of parentheses, operator precedence, and operator associativity. 16.Parentheses can be used to force the order of evaluation to occur in any sequence. 17.Operators with higher precedence are evaluated earlier. For operators of the same precedence, their associativity determines the order of evaluation. 18.All binary operators except assignment operators are left-associative; assignment operators are right-associative. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 24 C++ BASICS

Key Points 1.C++ provides the mathematical functions sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan, exp, log, log10, pow, sqrt, cell, floor, min, max, and abs for performing mathematical functions. 2.Character type (char) represents a single character. 3.The character \ is an escape character and an escape sequence starts with the escape character followed by another character or a combination of digits. 4.C++ allows you to use escape sequences to represent special characters such as ‘\t’ and ‘\n’. 5.The characters ‘ ‘, ‘\t’, ‘\f’, ‘\r’, and ‘\n’ are known as the whitespace characters. 6.C++ provides the functions isdigit, isalpha, isalnum, islower, isupper, isspace for testing whether a character is a digit, letter, digit or letter, lowercase, uppercase, and whitespace. It also contains the tolower and to toupper functions for returning a lowercase or uppercase letter. 7.A string is a sequence of characters. A string value is enclosed in matching double quotes(“). A character value is enclosed in matching single quotes “’”. 8.You can declare a string object using the string type. A function that is invoked from a specific object is called an instance function. 9.You can get the length of a string by invoking its length() function, and retrieve a character at the specified index in the string using the at(index) 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 25 C++ BASICS

C++ BASICS Variables: •A variable, whose name is a C++ identifier, represents a memory location that contains a value of a particular data type. •When possible, avoid uninitialized variables of a primitive data type. Literal Constants vs. Named Constants: • int i = 4; • const double PI = 3.14159; // 4 is a literal constant // PI is a named constant, a named floating constant // same as final in Java Enumerations: •enum {SUN, MON, TUE, WED, THU, FRI, SAT}; // const int SUN = 0; and so on •By default, the values assigned to the constants --- called enumerators--- begin with zero and are consecutive. •To assign explicit value: enum {PLUS = ‘+’, MINUS = ‘-’}; •Naming an enumeration makes a distinct integral type: • enum Season {WINTER, SPRING, SUMMER, FALL}; • Season whichSeason; // whichSeason now has all the 4 seasons. The typedef Statement: •Does not create a new type but a new name/alias/shortcut for an existing data type • typedef double Real; // Can use ‘Real’ for a group of variables. Easy to manage. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 27

C++ BASICS C++ Data Types: 1.Primitive Data Types: These data types are built-in or predefined data types and can be used directly by the user to declare variables: int, char , float, bool… 2.Abstract or user defined data type: These data types are defined by user like defining a class in C++ or a structure. Primitive Types: • Java has 8 primitive types: boolean, byte, char, short, int, long, float, and double • Java primitives have precise sets of possible values on • C++ primitive types have unspecified ranges. Integer Types: • int can be 16, 32, or 64 bits, depending on the platform. • The precise range also vary from machine to machine. • int can be augmented with short or long. • short and long can be by themselves without int. • 2 modifiers for int are signed and unsigned. Also applied to any of the integer types (or char). • unsigned int are never negative • short ranges -32, 768 to 32,767 and unsigned short ranges 0 to 65,535 because unsigned instructs the compiler to interpret what would normally be the sign bit as an extra bit. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 28

C++ BASICS Floating Point Types: • C++ floating point types include: float and double • Like Java, a double provides more significant digits than a float Char Type: • Stores characters but unspecified size and unspecified signed or unsigned. • Each Java character is 16 bit Unicode. • C++ compilers usually use 8 bits and store ASCII characters. • C++ has all the Java escape sequences and • \a for the bell, \? for question mark, and \v for vertical tab • Include cctype to use isupper, islower, isalpha, toupper and tolower… like java.lang.Character • C++ now supports wcahr_t type and wide-character literal Boolean Type: • bool has values: true or false. But they are actual integers 0 and 1. • 0 means false. • Nonzero means true. • A null pointer is false. Otherwise, true. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 29

Changes to the original do not affect the copy. •Java strings are immutable. •C++ strings are mutable. • Using the array indexing operator [] can access and change individual characters. • += does the job for Java StringBuilder •C++ substr() • C++ parameters: starting position and length of substring • Java parameters: starting position and first nonincluded position •To obtain a equivalent primitive strings, instead of the string library type, use c_str string member function. 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 30

This is done by comparing their corresponding characters on by one from left to right. string s1 = "ABC"; string s2 = "ABE"; // ASCII C 67 // ASCII E 69 cout << (s1 == s2) << endl; cout << (s1 != s2) << endl; cout << (s1 > s2) << endl; // Displays 0 (means false) // Displays 1 (means true) // Displays 0 (means false) // Displays 0 (means false) // Displays 1 (means true) // Displays 1 (means true) cout << (s1 >= s2) << endl; cout << (s1 < s2) << endl; cout << (s1 <= s2) << endl; 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 31

C++ Basics Implicit type conversions: •Automatic conversion from one data type to another can occur during assignment and during expression evaluation. •For assignments, the data type of the expression on the right-hand side of the assignment operator is converted to the data type of the item on the left- hand side just before the assignment occurs. Floating-point values are truncated, not rounded, when they are converted integral values. •Integral promotion: If the operands of an operator differ in data type, the data type that is lower in the following hierarchy is converted to on that is higher (int is lowest): int  unsigned  long  unsigned long  float  double  long double •Explicit type conversions: • To explicitly convert from one data type to another, use static cast: 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 36

C++ BASICS Unsigned and Negative Integers •Unsigned integers can only hold non-negative numbers: unsigned u = 5; •Assigning a negative value to an unsigned integer can lead to confusion: • unsigned int u = -4; • count << u << endl; // 4294967292 •Integer division involving negative numbers is platform dependent: -23/4 = -5 or -6 •Right shifting a negative number is machine dependent. •Negative numbers should not be used with remainder because it is machine dependent: 21 % (-5) = - 1 or 1 •Math routines use double, not float  use double 2/17/2019 CSC340, DUC TA, SFSU 42

C++ Memory Management • In Java, all objects are created through new, and the memory will be reclaimed with the garbage collector when no longer referenced • In C++, objects can be created via new, but the memory is not automatically reclaimed • In C++, if an object doesn’t have a pointer to it, it is a memory leak because it can’t be reclaimed. • Memory leaks are very bad and eventually will bring the software down

C++ and Standard Template Library (STL) • Functionally, similar to Java generics • In C++, best to explicitly include only what is needed #include <list> • In C, code is in .c files and interfaces/declarations in .h (header files) – In C++, header files can contain implementation – .hpp is a convention to differentiate from C headers • However, template code is contained in .hpp files – injected at compile time

Important Themes in Programming • Strive for modularity in all phases of the problem-solving process, beginning with the initial design of a solution • Develop more smaller modules versus fewer larger modules – Helps with debugging – Improves supportability and readability – Reduces redundant code • Hide the exact representation of these data members from modules that use the object by making all of the data members private; the object should provide – a simple method—called an accessor (getter) —that returns the value of the data member and – another method—called a mutator (setter)—that sets the value of the data member • Proper Use of Reference Arguments – Value parameters, (do not write & after the parameter’s data type), pass values into the method, where the value of each argument is copied into the parameter; any change that the method makes to these parameters is not reflected in the actual arguments back in the calling program. – Reference parameter becomes an alias (another name) for the corresponding argument, changes to the parameter within the method also occur in the argument in the calling module. – When copying the argument is expensive, use pass-by-constant reference instead of pass-by- value. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • To reduce coupling among modules, you should restrict the calls that a method can make. A method should call only other methods: – Defined within the same class – Of argument objects – Of objects created within the method – Of objects contained within the same class as data members • Do NOT use global variables. • Check for errors in both its input and its logic and attempt to behave gracefully and consistently when it encounters them. A method should check for certain types of errors, such as invalid input or argument values. • Appropriate action in the face of an error can range from ignoring erroneous data and continuing execution to terminating the program. A common technique is for a method to return a boolean value to the calling module to indicate that it has encountered an error. • The method leaves error handling to the calling module. In general, methods should either return a value or throw an exception instead of displaying a message when an error occurs. (Write to an error log file.) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • Choose identifiers that describe their purpose—that is, that are self-documenting. Distinguish between keywords, such as int, and user-defined identifiers. • Use a good indentation style to enhance the readability of a program. The layout of a program should make it easy for a reader to identify the program’s modules. • Use a blank line between method or function definitions. • Within these definitions, you should indent individual blocks of code. These blocks are generally—but are not limited to—the actions performed within a control structure, such as a while loop or an if statement. • Named constants make a program easier to modify, e.g., const int NUMBER_OF_MAJORS = 202; • typedef statements make a program easier to modify; if your program performs floating point computations, and you need greater precision than float (change float to double) – typedef float RealType; // original statement – typedef long double RealType; // update to this statement in one spot © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • Two kinds of testing can be applied to a system: – Open-box testing (white-box or glass-box testing) - design tests knowing how the modules work. • choose a method’s actual arguments in a way that exercises all lines of code in the method. • make sure that all branches of if and switch statements are executed. – Closed-box testing (black-box or functional testing) - you know the name of the method being tested, as well as its argument list, its return type, and its operation contract. • develop tests across a range of valid input, and then check the output against hand-calculated results. • Choose arguments outside of their valid range tests how the module handles incorrect input. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming • This book uses a javadoc-style comment (originated with the Java programming language). • These comments start with /** and end with */ and contain tags that classify different parts of your documentation. • Several utility programs are available to read these comments and generate HTML-based documentation. • The advantage of using javadoc-style comments—and a documentation system, like doxygen—is that you can edit the documentation for your source code as you make changes to that code. • The documentation lives directly in the source code—as javadoc-style comments—making it very easy and convenient to keep your documentation up to date. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Important Themes in Programming Essential features of program documentation 1. An initial comment at the top of each source code file that includes: a) File name (@file) b) Statement of purpose c) Author (@author) d) Date (@date) e) Optional file version number (@version) 2. Initial comments for each class that includes: a) Name of class and its header file (@class) b) Statement of purpose 3. Initial comments for each method or function that includes: a) Statement of purpose b) Description of each argument in the argument list (@param) c) Preconditions (@pre) d) Postconditions (@post) e) Exceptions thrown (@throw) f) Return value (@return) 4. Standard C++ comments in the body of each method or function to explain important features or subtle logic © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Classes C++ Interlude 1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Points • Header (.h ) files in C++ provide a mechanism to partially separate the design of a class from the implementation in the source , or implementation (.cpp ), file. • The header file must also contain a description of the data fields for the class and any private methods used by the class • Elements of the class – Private data fields // good practice • restricts access to the data field to just the class in which it is defined • Typically, provide methods—such as setItem (setters) and getItem (getters)— to access the data fields • Class controls how and whether other classes can access the data fields – If a derived class might need to access or modify the data fields of the base class, you should still make the data fields private but provide protected methods so that any derived classes can access or modify the data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Constructors • Elements of the class (cont.) – Constructors • Allocates memory for new instances of a class • Can initialize the object’s data to specified values • A typical class has several constructors – default constructor initializes data fields to values that the class implementation chooses – Other constructors have parameters. These parameterized constructors initialize data fields to values chosen by the client but approved by the constructor. • Have the same name as the class • Have no return type—not even void—and cannot use return to return a value • The compiler decides which constructor to call by matching the argument list supplied by the client with the parameters of the available constructors. A match occurs when the arguments and parameters correspond in number, data type, and order © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Destructors • Elements of the class (cont.) – Destructors • destroys an instance of a class when the object’s lifetime ends • A class has one destructor • For many classes, you can omit the destructor. In such cases, the compiler generates a destructor for you. • If the class itself creates an object on the free store (heap) by using the new operator, it is a safe and secure programming practice to implement a destructor to ensure that the memory for that object is freed (cover in Interlude 2) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Methods • Elements of the class (cont.) – Methods • Implement the algorithms that solve a problem • A method conveys three important pieces of information to a client wishing to use our class: – method name, – number and types of its parameters, and – method’s return type. • methods a client can use are in the public section of the class declaration © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Getters/Setters • Elements of the class (cont.) – An accessor method in a class (getters) • Accesses, or gets, the value of a data field • Name often begins with the word get • Declaring all accessor methods as const is good practice – ItemType getItem() const; • The const keyword signals to the compiler that the method does not change the data fields of the object – A mutator method in a class (setters) • Changes the value of a data field • Often, the name of a mutator method begins with the word set • Cannot be declared as const, because they must initialize the data fields of an object. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Preprocessor directives • Elements of the class (cont.) – Preprocessor directives • Lines included in the code of programs preceded by a hash sign (#) • Not program statements but directives for the preprocessor • The preprocessor examines the code before actual compilation of code begins and resolves all these directives before any code is actually generated by regular statements, e.g.: – #define TABLE_SIZE 100 – int table1[TABLE_SIZE] // equivalent to int table1[100] – #undef TABLE_SIZE – #define TABLE_SIZE 200 – int table2[TABLE_SIZE] // equivalent to int table2[200] © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Preprocessor directives (cont.) • Elements of the class (cont.) – Preprocessor directives (cont.) • Provides a way to prevent the compiler from reading the class definition a second time, if class definition is included multiple times: – #ifndef PLAIN_BOX // If PLAIN_BOX is not defined, then ...” – #define PLAIN_BOX // defines the name PLAIN_BOX – … – endif PLAIN_BOX © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If the compiler had not defined the name PLAIN_BOX, it would process the code that follows until it reached the #endif directive at the end of the file. If another file includes the class definition of PlainBox, the name PLAIN_BOX will already have been defined. That file’s #ifndef directive will cause the preprocessor to skip any of the code that follows, so the compiler will not see the same class definition more than once.

C++ Templates • Templates are a way to allow functions and classes to use the same code for many different data types. https://www.w3schools.com/cpp/cpp_templates.asp #include <string> #include <iostream> template <class MyType> // declares the template class MyClass { public: MyType first; MyType second; MyClass(MyType f, MyType s) { first = f; second = s; } }; int main() { // Use MyClass with strings MyClass<std::string> stringObj("Hello", "World"); std::cout << stringObj.first << " " << stringObj.second << "\n"; // Use MyClass with integers MyClass<int> intObj(4, 5); std::cout << intObj.first << " " << intObj.second << "\n"; return 0; } class MyType is a template parameter that makes MyType possible to represent any data type creates a MyClass object where MyType means string creates a MyClass object where MyType means int

C++ Inheritance • Inheritance allows us to reuse and extend work we have already completed and tested. • You can think of a base class as a parent class. – base class = superclass = parent class • You can think of a derived class as a child class – derived class = subclass = child class • Any instance of the derived class is also considered to be an instance of the base class and can be used in a program anywhere that an instance of the base class can be used • When the keyword public is used with the base class, any of the publicly defined methods or data fields in the base class can be used by instances of the derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ derived class • A derived class – has the data fields and methods of the base class in addition to the members it defines, – it can access only publicly defined members from the base class – can also revise any inherited public method. – contains copies of inherited data fields, – does not contain a copy of the code for inherited methods – does not inherit constructors from parent class, but it can use it or other parent method to initialize the private data fields © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

C++ Inheritance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved We can create and use a ToyBox object as follows: string favoriteToy = "Jack-in-the-Box"; ToyBox<string> myToyCase(favoriteToy, RED); // A red toy box string oldToy = myToyCase.getItem(); // oldToy is a Jack-in-the-Box favoriteToy = "Spinning Top"; myToyCase.setItem(favoriteToy); // myToyCase now holds a Spinning Top ToyBox 1) defines constructors and specific new method, 2)inherits data field item, and methods setItem, getItem from PlainBox class

Overriding Base-Class Methods • You can add as many new members to derived class as desired • You can redefine inherited methods – Called overriding a base-class method. • A method overrides a base-class method when – The two methods have the same name and parameter declarations • Precede a method name with the base-class namespace indicator to tell the compiler to use the base-class version of the method • Without a namespace indicator in front of the method name, the compiler assumes that the programmer is referring to the current class and inserts code to call that version of the method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Virtual Methods, Abstract Classes • Using keyword virtual in front of the prototype – Tells the C++ compiler that the code this method executes is determined at runtime – Allows an application using our class to take advantage of polymorphism when the methods are invoked • Pure virtual method – Virtual method that has no implementation • Abstract class – Has at least one pure virtual method © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
