Week 4 Page 1 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Memory Management • Thanks to Paul Kube for the slides

Pictures of memory Page 2 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • It is useful to abstract the essentials about the contents of memory by drawing simple pictures... • Here is a picture of the contents of memory after those statements (either the Java or the C++ version) execute: • The pointer variable x points to the object, an instance of the class C, which contains an instance variable named a with value 5: • Always be aware of the difference between a pointer and what it points to! x: a: 5 int a=5; int* ptr = &a;

Memory Memory distribution in a typical operating system process • Stack – the stack is for local variables and for maintaining a record of function calls. The stack grows from the top of memory down towards the heap. • Heap - The heap is for dynamically allocated data items. The heap grows from the top of the static data area up as data items are allocated. • Static Data - This is a block of reserved space in RAM for all the global and static variables from your program. Allocated once and lasts for duration of a program. Stack Free Memory Heap Static Data

Differences between Java and C++ Page 4 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Pointers: • In Java, declaring a variable of class type creates a pointer that can point to an instance of that type (an object) • in C++, you have to explicitly specify that the variable is a pointer by using * after the typename • Dereferences: • In Java, the dot operator . dereferences a pointer and accesses a member of the object pointed to • in C++, the -> operator does that; or, you can use a combination of the dereference operator * and the member access operator . That is, these are equivalent in C++: x->getA() and (*x).getA()

Memory management in Java and C++ Page 5 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • In Java, all objects are created using new, and memory an object takes is automatically reclaimed by the garbage collector when it can no longer be accessed in your program: C x = new C(); // create an object x = null; // object is inaccessible; no problem, gc will free • In C++, objects can be created using new, but memory for such objects is not automatically reclaimed: C* x = new C(); // create an object using new x = nullptr; // object is inaccessible; // that’s a problem, it will not be freed • That is a memory leak! In C++, an object created using new must be explicitly freed using delete for its memory to be reclaimed for later use in your program C* x = new C(); // create an object using new ... // do things with the object delete x; // free the object using delete x = nullptr; // make pointer null to avoid dangling pointer • Note: some objects created in other ways in a C++ program are reclaimed automatically. Understanding memory management is important in C++ programming

Automatic, static, and dynamic data in Java and C++ Page 6 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A running program typically has 3 kinds of data in it: • automatic • static • dynamic • These kinds of data differ in: • what region of machine memory the data is stored in • when and how memory for the data is allocated (“created”) • when and how memory for the data is deallocated (“destroyed”) • Both Java and C++ have basically these same 3 kinds of data, with some differences • (Note: these are kinds of data, i.e. variables. Executable instructions are stored somewhat differently.)

Automatic data Page 7 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Automatic data is called "automatic" because it is automatically created and destroyed when your program runs • Examples of automatic data in both Java and C++: formal parameters to functions, local variables in functions (if the variables are not declared static) • Memory for automatic data is allocated on the runtime stack • Memory for automatic data is allocated when execution reaches the scope of the variable’s declaration • Memory for automatic data is deallocated when execution leaves that scope

Static data Page 8 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Static data is called "static" because it essentially exists during the entire execution of your program • Examples of static data in Java and C++: variables declared static (or, in C++, variables declared outside any function and class body) • Memory for static data is allocated in the static data segment • Memory for static data is allocated when your program starts, or when execution reaches the static data declaration for the first time • Memory for static data is deallocated when your program exits

Dynamic data Page 9 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Dynamic data is called "dynamic" because it is allocated (and, in C/C++, destroyed) under explicit programmer control • Example of dynamic data in Java: all objects (instances of a class), including arrays. Dynamic data is created using the new operator • Example of dynamic data in C++: any variables created using the new operator. In C++, any type variable can be created using new • Memory for dynamic data is allocated from the "heap" • In Java, dynamic data exists from the time new is called until the object is reclaimed by the garbage collector • In C++, dynamic data exists from the time it is allocated with the new operator until it is deallocated with the delete operator

Example of dynamic objects in C++ Page 10 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Consider this C++ function. The object created with new is dynamic and must be explicitly destroyed with delete. Other variables (i, j, x) are automatic. int foo(int i) { int j = i + 1; C* x; x = new C(); x->setA(j); j = x->getA(); delete x; return j; }

Example of automatic objects in C++ Page 11 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Now consider this C++ function. • Here x refers directly to an object, an instance of the class C (not a pointer!), created automatically on the stack, and initialized with the default constructor. It will automatically be destroyed when the function returns. Other variables (i,j) are also automatic. int foo(int i) { int j = i + 1; C x; x.setA(j); j = x.getA(); return j; } • Note the use of the ordinary C++ member access operator . • Note that Java does not permit creating objects as automatic data on the stack like this

Dynamic data bugs Page 12 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Automatic and static data is created and destroyed automatically. As a programmer, you should know how and when this happens, but you do not need to take steps to make it happen • Because the standard C++ runtime environment does not have a garbage collector, both allocation and deallocation of dynamic data must be done under programmer control • This leads to common bugs that can be very hard to track down. • We’ll look at these types of bugs: • Inaccessible objects • Memory leaks • Dangling pointers

Inaccessible objects Page 13 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • The inaccessible object bug: changing the value of the only pointer to an object, so you can’t access the object anymore thing* p = new thing(); thing* q = new thing(); p = q; // the first thing is now inaccessible

Memory leaks Page 14 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • Memory leaks: forgetting to delete dynamic data (often related to inacessible objects) • (these are hard to get in Java, because of its garbage collector) void foo() { double* q = new double(3.0); /* stuff, but no delete q */ } ... for(i=0;i<1000000;i++) foo(); // massive memory leak!

Dangling pointers Page 15 of 68 CSE 100, UCSD: LEC 2 • A "dangling pointer" is a pointer variable that contains a non-null address that is no longer valid... the pointer isn’t null, but it isn’t pointing to a valid object either • (Hard to get in Java, with no pointers to automatic variables, and good garbage collection) int* bar() { int i; return &i; // automatic variable, allocated on stack // return pointer to automatic variable... // bad news! Stack frame is popped upon return } ... int* p; int* q; p = new int(99); q = p; delete p; p = 0; // q and p are now dangling pointers // q is still dangling!
CSC 340 Programming Methodology Week 3 UML Class Diagrams

UML Diagrams • UML has many different diagram types • Sequence, State, Timing, Use Case, etc. • In industry, most often used is the Class Diagram • Useful during analysis and design • Finished diagram can also be used as documentation • Become familiar with the Class Diagram!

Class Diagrams • Proportion wise, this diagram in industry is used much more than other UML Diagrams (100 : 1 or more) • Class Diagram will be one of the first diagrams (in addition to an ERD) you will see at a job • You will need to be able to read and understand the Class Diagram

Important Class Diagram artifacts • Class block - Name, attributes, methods • Association – solid line with no arrowheads • Multiplicities – numbers at the end of the lines to show cardinality • Aggregation – open diamond • Stand alone, are not joined at the hip; Bank can exist without an Account • Composition – filled-in diamond • Must appear together, Ownership; Room can’t exist without a House

Data Abstraction: The Walls Chapter 1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Data Abstraction: The Walls • Data abstraction is a technique for increasing the modularity of a program—for building “walls” between a program and its data structures • During the design of a solution, you will need to perform operations on the data, therefore need to define abstract data types (ADTs). • After you specified the operations of an ADT, you consider data structures for implementation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Object-Oriented Concepts • Object-oriented analysis and design (OOAD) – Process for solving problems • Solution – Computer program consisting of system of interacting classes of objects • Object – Has set of characteristics, behaviors related to solution – Known as an instance of the class – When you create an object of a class, you instantiate the object © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Object-Oriented Analysis & Design • Requirements of a solution – What solution must be, do • Object-oriented design – Describe solution to problem, which meets requirements – Express solution in terms of software objects and object interaction – Create one or more models of solution © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Aspects of Object-Oriented Solution Principles of object-oriented programming • Encapsulation: Objects combine data and operations; hides inner details • Inheritance: Classes inherit properties from other classes; allows class reuse • Polymorphism: Objects determine appropriate operations at execution time. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Cohesion • Each module should perform one well-defined task - cohesive • Benefits – Well named, self-documenting, e.g., sort – Easy to reuse – Easier to maintain – More robust; less likely to be impacted by change © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Coupling • Measure of dependence among modules • Dependence – Sharing data structures or calling each other’s methods • Modules should be loosely coupled – Highly coupled modules should be avoided • Benefits of loose coupling in a system • More adaptable to change • Easier to understand • Increases reusability • Has increased cohesion © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Specifications FIGURE 1-1 The task sort is a module separate from the MyProgram module © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Operation Contracts • Documents – How method can be used – What limitations it has • Specify – Purpose of modules – Data flow among modules – Pre-, post-condition, input, output of each module © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Unusual Conditions Ways to address invalid conditions: • Assume they will not happen • Ignore such situations • Guess at client’s intentions • Return value that signals problem • Throw an exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Abstraction • Separate purpose of a module from its implementation – For example, you should be able to change the sorting algorithm in the previous example without affecting the rest of the solution • Specifications do not indicate how to implement – Able to use without knowing implementation © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Information Hiding • Abstraction helps identify details that should be hidden from public view – Ensured no other module can tamper with these hidden details. • Isolation of the modules cannot be total, however – Client must know what tasks can be done, how to initiate a task © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Minimal and Complete Interfaces • Interface for a class made up of publicly accessible methods and data • Complete interface for a class – Allows programmer to accomplish any reasonable task • Minimal interface for a class – Contains method if and only if that method is essential to class’s responsibilities © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Abstract Data Types (ADT) • An ADT: a collection of data and a set of operations on data • A data structure: an implementation of an ADT within a programming language • Typical operations on data – Add data to a data collection. – Remove data from a data collection. – Ask questions about the data in a data collection. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Designing an ADT • Evolves naturally during the problem-solving process – What data does a problem require? – What operations does a problem require? • ADTs typically have initialization and destruction operations – Assumed but not specified at this stage © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Memory Allocation for Variables and Early Binding of Methods • Declare variable x to have data type int – C++ compiler allocates memory cell to hold an integer – Use the identifier x to refer to this cell • E.g. int x = 5; • A function’s locally declared variables – Placed into an activation record with parameters and bookkeeping data – Activation record placed on run-time stack – Activation record destroyed when function finished © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Memory Allocation for Variables and Early Binding of Methods • When you create an object, the storage for data members of an object – E.g., PlainBox<string> myPlainBox; – Placed into an activation record for the currently executing function or method. – Data fields placed on the run-time stack just as primitive data types are. – if you invoke the setItem method for myPlainBox, compiler knows to invoke the corresponding method for PlainBox • myPlainBox.setItem(“First item”); • This is early binding, made during compilation – Cannot be altered during execution © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Memory Allocation for Variables and Early Binding of Methods • Automatic memory management and early binding sometimes insufficient – Need to take advantage of polymorphism. – Must access an object outside of the function or method that creates it. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Early versus Late Binding // C++ Program to illustrate early binding #include<iostream> using namespace std; class Base { public: void show() {cout << " In Base 
"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() {cout << "In Derived 
"; } }; int main() { Base* bp = new Derived; bp->show(); // RUN-TIME POLYMORPHISM delete bp; bp = nullptr; return 0; } https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/early-binding-late-binding-c/ // C++ Program to illustrate late binding #include<iostream> using namespace std; class Base { public: virtual void show() {cout << " In Base 
"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() {cout << "In Derived 
"; } }; int main() { Base* bp = new Derived; bp->show(); // RUN-TIME POLYMORPHISM delete bp; bp = nullptr; return 0; } This will execute This will execute

Early versus Late Binding – Use early binding for better performance and when you don't need polymorphism. – Use late binding (virtual functions) for dynamic behavior and when you need to work with objects of different derived classes through a common base class pointer or reference. https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/early-binding-late-binding-c/

Pointers and Program’s Free Store FIGURE C2-2 Sample program memory layout © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved When a C++ program begins execution, the operating system sets aside memory for • Run-time stack, • Code storage – store your code, or text storage, • Static storage – store any global variables and static variables, • Heap, or free store, which a programmer can use to store data

Pointers and Program’s Free Store FIGURE C2-3 Run-time stack and free store after myboxPtr points to a MagicBox object and its data member item is set © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Variables placed in the heap persist in memory even when the function or method that created them ends • When a programmer allocates memory from the free store (heap) for a variable, the programmer must deallocate that memory when the variable is no longer needed, else memory leak

Pointers and Program’s Free Store FIGURE C2-4 myBoxPtr and the object to which it points © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved This newly created object has no programmer-defined name; the only way to access its methods is indirectly via the pointer that new creates: myBoxPtr->

Pointers and Program’s Free Store FIGURE C2-5 Two pointer variables that point to the same object © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If you have two pointer variables that can point to the same type of object, you can make them point to the same object by using the assignment operator: somePtr = toyPtr; /* somePtr point to same object which toyPtr is pointing to; note that the object itself is not copied */ If you declare a pointer variable but do not immediately create an object for it to reference, you should set the pointer to nullptr. C++ does not initialize the pointer for you: ToyBox<int>* myToyPtr = nullptr;

Deallocating Memory • When memory to which pointer variable points is no longer needed – Deallocate it by using delete operator. – Then set pointer variable to nullptr • Otherwise dangling pointer exists – It would still contain address of object that was deallocated. – Can be source of serious errors. • C++ memory leak tools: https://learn.microsoft.com/en- us/cpp/c-runtime-library/find-memory-leaks-using-the-crt- library?view=msvc-170 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Memory Leaks • Memory leaks occur when – An object has been created in the heap (free stores), but – Program no longer has a way to access © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved LISTING C2-1 Poorly written function that allocates memory in the free store We have memory leak, since someItem and the pointer someBoxPtr are both stored on the stack, which are destroyed after the function ends.

Avoiding Memory Leaks Figure C2-6 An assignment that causes an inaccessible object. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • To prevent memory leak, • Do not create the 2nd object • Initialize yourBoxPtr to nullptr, or Set it to myBoxPtr • More subtle memory leak occurs when function or method creates an object in heap, and loses the pointer by not returning that pointer or not storing it in a class member

Avoiding Memory Leaks • Memory leak prevention – The best option for preventing a memory leak is to not use a function to return a pointer to a newly created object – You should define a class that has a method for this task – Class takes responsibility for deleting the object in the heap (free store) and ensures that there is no memory leak. At a minimum, such a class will have three parts: 1. a method that creates the object in the free store, 2. a data field that points to the object, and 3. a method—the destructor —that deletes the object when the class instance is no longer needed © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Memory Leaks • Destructors – Every C++ class has a destructor that has the same name as the class, but is preceded by the tilde (~) character. – For objects that are local variables, the destructor is called when the activation record containing the object is removed from the run-time stack. – If the object was created using new and is stored in the free store, the destructor is called when the client uses the operator delete to free the memory allocated to the object. – Often, the compiler-generated destructor is sufficient for a class, but if the class itself creates an object on the free store (heap) by using the new operator, it is a safe and secure programming practice to implement a destructor to ensure that the memory for that object is freed © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Dangling Pointers • Situations that can cause dangling pointer – if you do not set a pointer variable to nullptr after using delete – If you declare a pointer variable but do not assign it a value © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Avoiding Dangling Pointers FIGURE C2-7 Two pointers referencing (pointing to) the same object © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved If you have two (2) or more pointers pointing to the same object (alias), and • you delete the first pointer to the object, and • attempt to execute the method with the second pointer, will result in a runtime error, since the second pointer is a dangling pointer o MagicBox<string>* myBoxPtr = new MagicBox<string>(); o MagicBox<string>* yourBoxPtr = myBoxPtr; Suppose we then execute: delete myBoxPtr; myBoxPtr = nullptr ; yourBoxPtr->getItem(); • Program aborts because yourBoxPtr still references the object’s location in the heap, even though the object no longer exists • yourBoxPtr is a dangling pointer and the program aborts when we try to call a method on that object

Avoiding Dangling Pointers • Set pointer variables to nullptr either initially or when you no longer need them. • If a class has a pointer variable as a data field, the constructor should always initialize that data field, to point either to an object or to nullptr. • Test whether a pointer variable contains nullptr before using it to call a method. • Try to reduce the use of aliases in your program. • Do not delete an object in the free store (heap) until you are certain that no other alias needs to use it. • Set all aliases that reference a deleted object to nullptr when the object is deleted. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Virtual Methods and Polymorphism • Allow compiler to perform the late binding necessary for polymorphism – Declare methods in base class as virtual, to enable late binding for polymorphism; indicates methods can be overridden – Create variables in the free store (heap), and use pointers to reference them – Change function to accept that pointer – Free the memory in heap; delete and assign to nullptr © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Virtual Methods and Polymorphism • Key points about virtual methods – A virtual method is one that a derived class can override. – A derived class does not need to override an existing implementation of an inherited virtual method. – Any of a class’s methods may be virtual. However, if you do not want a derived class to override a particular method, the method should not be virtual. – Constructors cannot be virtual. – Destructors can and should be virtual. Virtual destructors ensure that future descendants of the object can deallocate themselves correctly. – A virtual method’s return type cannot be overridden © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Dynamic Allocation of Arrays • An ordinary C++ array is statically allocated • Can use new operator to allocate an array dynamically © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved arraySize can change during program execution MAX_SIZE cannot change during program execution

Dynamic Allocation of Arrays • delete returns a dynamically allocated array to system for reuse • Increase size of dynamically allocated array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Array-Based vs. and Link-Based • Arrays easy to use, but have fixed size – Not always easy to predict number of items in ADT – Array could waste space – Increasing size of dynamically allocated array can waste storage and time – Can access array items directly with equal access time – An array-based implementation is a good choice for a small bag • Linked chains do not have fixed size – In a chain of linked nodes, an item points explicitly to the next item – Link-based implementation requires more memory – Must traverse a linked chain to access its ith node – Time to access ith node in a linked chain depends on i © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 18-3 A dictionary entry Search key Data Value insert a new item into proper position based on the value of search key insert a new item into any convenient location

Figure 18-4 Data members for two sorted linear implementations of the ADT dictionary for the data in Figure 18-1 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Possible Implementations • A linear array-based implementation of the ADT dictionary must shift data during an insertion or deletion in sorted order, which can be expensive, particularly for large dictionaries. • A linear link-based implementation of the ADT dictionary eliminates the need to shift data, it does not support the insertion or removal operations any more efficiently than an array-based implementation, because you cannot perform a binary search in a reasonable fashion.

Three Scenarios A. Addition and traversal in no particular order – Unsorted order is efficient – Array-based versus pointer-based B. Retrieval – Sorted array-based can use binary search – Binary search impractical for link-based – Max size of dictionary affects choice C. Addition, removal, retrieval, traversal in sorted order – Add and remove need to find position, then add or remove from that position – Array-based best for find, link-based best for addition/removal © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Situations occur for which search-tree implementations are not adequate. • Consider a method which acts as an “address calculator” which determines an array index – Used for add, getValue, remove operations • Called a hash function – Tells where to place item in a hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation FIGURE 18-9 Address calculator © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Perfect hash function – Maps each search key into a unique location of the hash table – Possible if you know all the search keys • Collision occurs when hash function maps more than one entry into same array location • Hash function should – Be easy, fast to compute – Place entries evenly throughout hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hash Functions • Sufficient for hash functions to operate on integers – examples: – Select digits from an ID number • Generally uneven distribution – Folding – add digits, sum is the table location – Modulo arithmetic h(x) = x mod tableSize • Simple, effective • Use prime number for table size – Convert character string to an integer – use ASCII values • Address potential overflow © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • Approach 1: Open addressing – Linear probing – Quadratic probing – Double hashing – Increase size of hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-11 Linear probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Linear probing • Add - search the hash table, starting from the original hash location; if table[h(searchKey)] is occupied, check table[h(searchKey)+1] , table[h(searchKey)+2], … until you find an available location. • Delete –place table location into one of three states: • occupied (currently in use), • empty (has not been used), or • Modify addItem to insert at this location • removed (was once occupied but is now available) • Modify getItem to continue probing • Modify addItem to insert at this location • Items tend to cluster together in the hash table, when using this approach - primary clustering. • Clusters can get close to one another and, merge into a larger cluster. • Large clusters tend to get even larger

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-12 Quadratic probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quadratic probing. • You can virtually eliminate primary clusters by adjusting the linear probing scheme. • When two items hash collide, quadratic probing uses the same probe sequence for each item. • The resulting phenomenon—called secondary clustering —delays the resolution of the collision. • Although the research of quadratic probing remains incomplete, it appears that secondary clustering is not a problem.

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-13 Double hashing during the addition of 58, 14, and 91 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Double hashing - defines key-dependent probe sequences. • searches the hash table in a linear order, starting at the location h1(key), • second hash function h2 determines the size of the steps taken • h2(key) <> 0 • h2 <> h1 • For example, let h1 and h2 be the primary and secondary hash functions defined as • h1 ( key ) = key mod 11 // 11 is table size and prime number • h2(key) = 7 - (key mod 7) // visits all the table locations (table size and step are prime) If key = 58, • h1 hashes to location 3 (58 mod 11), and • h2 indicates that the probe sequence should take steps of size 5 (7 – 58 mod 7). • In other words, the probe sequence will be 3, 8, 2 (wraps around), 7, 1 (wraps around), 6, 0, 5, 10, 4, 9.

Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure • You should not double the size of the array, since the size of the hash table should be prime. • You do not move the items from the original hash table to the new hash table, since your hash function may have been impacted, e.g., x mod tableSize • You need to apply your new hash function to every item in the old hash table before placing it into the new hash table. (Rehashing); expensive • Approach 2: Resolving collisions by restructuring the hash table – Buckets • Define each has table location table[i] as an array, aka, bucket, where you store the collided items into in this array • Too small – only postponed collision problem • Too big – wasted storage – Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure FIGURE 18-14 Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Separate chaining. Design the hash table as an array of linked chains • Each entry table[i] is a pointer to a chain of linked nodes containing the items that the hash function has mapped into location i • The size of the dictionary is dynamic and can exceed the size of the hash table, because each linked chain can be as long as necessary. • The most time-efficient collision-resolution scheme.
The Efficiency of Hashing FIGURE 18-15 The relative efficiency of four collision-resolution methods © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

What Constitutes a Good Hash Function? • Is hash function easy and fast to compute? • Does hash function scatter data evenly throughout hash table? • How well does hash function scatter random data? • How well does hash function scatter non-random data? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Dictionary Traversal: An Inefficient Operation Under Hashing • Entries hashed into table[i] and table[i+1] have no ordering relationship • Hashing does not support well traversing a dictionary in sorted order – Generally better to use a search tree • In external storage possible to see – Hashing implementation of getValue – And search-tree for ordered operations simultaneously © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Unified Modeling Language Appendix C © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Unified Modeling Language • The Unified Modeling Language, or UML, is a modeling language used to express object-oriented designs • A use case is a set of textual scenarios or stories that describe the proposed solution, but does not describe how the system works • A use case is not object-oriented, but it is an important part of the analysis of the problem. • The main success scenario (or “happy path”) describes how the system satisfies the goals of the user when everything goes smoothly. • A scenario is written from the perspective of the user of the system. • Alternate scenarios should be written only for exception cases. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Sequence Diagram • A UML sequence diagram, or interaction diagram, – shows how two or more objects will interact with one another over time within a single scenario (process flow), after use cases and potential objects are created. – allows you to visualize the messages sent among the objects in a scenario and the order in which those messages happen – defines the responsibilities of the objects: • What must the object “remember,” or keep track of, and • what must the object do for other objects? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Sequence Diagram © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Show objects left to right Each object has a lifeline (vertical dashed line) Each lifeline can have 1 or more activation bars (open box on lifeline) To send a message between objects, draw a solid arrow from the activation bar of the calling object to the top of a new activation bar on the lifeline of the called object, with message and parameters Task completes and returns (dashed arrow w/ variable) Objects may send messages to itself

The Unified Modeling Language © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved In Figure C-2, a new account is created. Known as stereotype, which use left and right guillemets (<< and >>) A stereotype is used to identify a unique characteristic of an element in any UML diagram.

The UML Sequence Diagram • Is a design document that gives guidance on several levels to the programmer implementing a solution • Shows which objects collaborate to solve a particular use-case scenario • Shows the synchronous nature of the messages that are passed between the objects in the scenario, providing the order in which tasks are accomplished • Shows which object is responsible for what task or tasks by handling a particular message • Provides a visual representation of the flow of messages sent between objects during a single scenario © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Class Diagram The class diagram (static diagram) shows the attributes and operations of an individual class and how multiple classes are related to one another in a language-independent way. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Class with a name, with no attributes or operations Class with attributes in the middle, and operations at the bottom Class with no attributes (empty middle section), and operations at the bottom

The UML Class Diagram • The UML syntax for the attributes of a class is [visibility] name [:type][=defaultValue] [{property}] • where – All elements within square brackets are optional. – visibility can be • + for public accessibility, (open for all) • - for private accessibility (default, if omitted; open to member functions and friends), or • # for protected accessibility (open to member functions and friends, and member functions/friends of derived classes) . – name is the name of the attribute. (Required.) – type is the data type of the attribute. (e.g., integer, string) – defaultValue is the default value of the attribute. – property (changeable or frozen/constant). © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
The UML Class Diagram • The attributes for the Bank class in Figure C-3b could now be written as –name: string –routingNum: integer © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Class Diagram • The UML syntax for a class’s operations is: [visibility] name ([parameterList]) [:type] [{property}] • where o All elements within square brackets are optional. o Visibility: +public, -private or #protected, (default, if omitted, to public). o name is the name of the operation (Required) o type is the data type of the result returned from the operation (e.g., void, integer, float,…) o property - The only property relevant to us is query, which indicates the operation will not modify any of the data. o parameterList is either empty—in which case the parentheses are still required—or contains a comma-delimited list of parameters to the operation. The syntax for each parameter looks like the following: [direction] name:type [=defaultValue] • where o All elements within square brackets are optional. o direction shows whether the parameter is used for input (in), output (out), or both input and output (inout). o name is the name of the parameter. o type is the data type of the parameter. (e.g., integer, float,…) o defaultValue is the value of this parameter if no corresponding actual argument is provided when this operation is called. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Class Diagram • The Bank class operations in Figure C-3b could be written as +authorize(in name: string, in identifier: string): void {query} -createAccount() • What accessibility does authorize have? – public • What accessibility does createAccount have? – private © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The UML Class Diagram © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved association relationship indicated by the solid line with no arrowheads multiplicities (optional) – each bank is associated w/ 0 or more customers, but each customer is associated w/ 1 bank multiplicities (optional) – each customer can have 0 or more accounts, but each account can belong to 1 customer • The Account class is related to the Checking and Savings classes via generalization (inheritance) relationship, indicated by the open triangular arrowhead • Checking and Savings inherit the attributes and operations of the Account class. • Account class name is in italic, which denotes an abstract base class • The getBalance operation in the Account class is also in italic. This indicates that getBalance is not implemented in the Account class but must be implemented within the descendant class. 5 classes: 1) Bank, 2) Customer, 3) Account, 4) Checking and 5) Savings • Bank and Account classes have aggregation (containment) relationship • The open diamond head denotes containing class; banks live longer than accounts • Filled in diamond head is a composition relationship, where lifetimes are the same • Checking and Savings classes each implement the inherited getBalance operation. • This operation will take on polymorphic behavior when called from a parent class object.

The Unified Modeling Language • You should use the UML to quickly draw various aspects of the current problem to better understand it during OOA and to create a set of objects during OOD that can collaborate to solve this problem. • Often small teams of programmers will draw several UML diagrams on a whiteboard and, after working out the kinks in the diagrams, take digital pictures of them for future reference. • These design diagrams are considered an artifact, or document, associated with the analysis and design. However, the ultimate implementation—that is, the solution—might not exactly match the original design. Thus, the design diagrams might not document the solution. • UML can conceptually model a problem domain in terms of software objects, independently of a programming language. • The UML enables the members of a programming team to communicate visually with one another, thereby gaining a common understanding of the system that is being built. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Situations occur for which search-tree implementations are not adequate. • Consider a method which acts as an “address calculator” which determines an array index – Used for add, getValue, remove operations • Called a hash function – Tells where to place item in a hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation FIGURE 18-9 Address calculator © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hashing as a Dictionary Implementation • Perfect hash function – Maps each search key into a unique location of the hash table – Possible if you know all the search keys • Collision occurs when hash function maps more than one entry into same array location • Hash function should – Be easy, fast to compute – Place entries evenly throughout hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Hash Functions • Sufficient for hash functions to operate on integers – examples: – Select digits from an ID number • Generally uneven distribution – Folding – add digits, sum is the table location – Modulo arithmetic h(x) = x mod tableSize • Simple, effective • Use prime number for table size – Convert character string to an integer – use ASCII values • Address potential overflow © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing • Approach 1: Open addressing – Linear probing – Quadratic probing – Double hashing – Increase size of hash table © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-10 A collision © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-11 Linear probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Linear probing • Add - search the hash table, starting from the original hash location; if table[h(searchKey)] is occupied, check table[h(searchKey)+1] , table[h(searchKey)+2], … until you find an available location. • Delete –place table location into one of three states: • occupied (currently in use), • empty (has not been used), or • Modify addItem to insert at this location • removed (was once occupied but is now available) • Modify getItem to continue probing • Modify addItem to insert at this location • Items tend to cluster together in the hash table, when using this approach - primary clustering. • Clusters can get close to one another and, merge into a larger cluster. • Large clusters tend to get even larger

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-12 Quadratic probing with h(x) = x mod 101 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quadratic probing. • You can virtually eliminate primary clusters by adjusting the linear probing scheme. • When two items hash collide, quadratic probing uses the same probe sequence for each item. • The resulting phenomenon—called secondary clustering —delays the resolution of the collision. • Although the research of quadratic probing remains incomplete, it appears that secondary clustering is not a problem.

Resolving Collisions with Open Addressing FIGURE 18-13 Double hashing during the addition of 58, 14, and 91 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Double hashing - defines key-dependent probe sequences. • searches the hash table in a linear order, starting at the location h1(key), • second hash function h2 determines the size of the steps taken • h2(key) <> 0 • h2 <> h1 • For example, let h1 and h2 be the primary and secondary hash functions defined as • h1 ( key ) = key mod 11 // 11 is table size and prime number • h2(key) = 7 (key mod 7) // visits all the table locations (table size and step are prime)

Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure • You should not double the size of the array, since the size of the hash table should be prime. • You do not move the items from the original hash table to the new hash table, since your hash function may have been impacted, e.g., x mod tableSize • You need to apply your new hash function to every item in the old hash table before placing it into the new hash table. (Rehashing); expensive • Approach 2: Resolving collisions by restructuring the hash table – Buckets • Define each has table location table[i] as an array, aka, bucket, where you store the collided items into in this array • Too small – only postponed collision problem • Too big – wasted storage – Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Resolving Collisions - Table Restructure FIGURE 18-14 Separate chaining © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Separate chaining. Design the hash table as an array of linked chains • Each entry table[i] is a pointer to a chain of linked nodes containing the items that the hash function has mapped into location i • The size of the dictionary is dynamic and can exceed the size of the hash table, because each linked chain can be as long as necessary. • The most time-efficient collision-resolution scheme.

The Efficiency of Hashing • Load factor measures how full a hash table is • Unsuccessful searches – Generally require more time than successful • Do not let the hash table get too full © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
The Efficiency of Hashing FIGURE 18-15 The relative efficiency of four collision-resolution methods © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

What Constitutes a Good Hash Function? • Is hash function easy and fast to compute? • Does hash function scatter data evenly throughout hash table? • How well does hash function scatter random data? • How well does hash function scatter non- random data? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Dictionary Traversal: An Inefficient Operation Under Hashing • Entries hashed into table[i] and table[i+1] have no ordering relationship • Hashing does not support well traversing a dictionary in sorted order – Generally better to use a search tree • In external storage possible to see – Hashing implementation of getValue – And search-tree for ordered operations simultaneously © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Background • Sometimes it is not possible for a method to return a boolean value when an unusual situation prevents it from completing its task • Preconditions for a method not always met – Method might return a false to indicate this – But not always possible • Example – Stack method peek() called on empty stack which contains items of type bool – Return cannot be sure if return is normal or an exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Assertions • Express an assertion either as a comment or by using the C++ function assert – #include <cassert> – Make assertions about variables, objects – Assertion in form of boolean expression that should be true at that point in program – False halts program execution • Mainly used to validate pre- or post-conditions • This is a debugging tool – Not a substitute for an if statement – Should not be deployed in final program © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Assertions #include <cassert> #include <iostream> int main() { std::cout << "Hello world 1" << std::endl; int x = 7; /* Some big code in between and let's say x is accidentally changed to 9 */ x = 9; std::cout << "Hello world 2" << std::endl; // Programmer assumes x to be 7 in rest of the code assert(x==7); /* Rest of the code */ std::cout << "Hello world, again" <<std::endl; return 0; } • Assertions • can be useful when you are testing and debugging your program. • is not be the best solution for a final shipping program. • A violation of a function’s preconditions may be a simple mistake that the client can fix. • Such a situation does not warrant terminating program execution. What is the console output? Hello world 1 Hello world 2 Assertion failed: x == 7, … Program halts. This is not executed.

Throwing Exceptions • Alternate way of communicating or returning information to function’s client • Thrown exception bypasses normal execution, – Control immediately returns to client. • Can contain information about the error or unusual condition that helps you resolve the issue and possibly try the function again © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Throwing Exceptions FIGURE C3-1 Hierarchy of C++ exception classes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Syntax runtime_error exceptions (or one of its derived classes) are thrown by errors that are detected at runtime (e.g., division by zero or numeric overflow) • program will halt, just like an assertion fail • can contain a message that is displayed to the user giving details about the error and the reason the program halted logic_error exceptions (or one of its derived classes) represent errors in program logic (e.g., unmet precondition or failure to satisfy a postcondition, that you could detect before the program executes). • test for an error or unusual condition using an if statement; throw an exception containing a message to our client, if error occurs • To restrict the exceptions, you can include a throw clause in its header, informing you that the function might throw an exception. • Including a throw clause in a function specification ensures that the function can throw only those exceptions. An attempt by the function to throw any other exception will result in a runtime error. • Omitting a throw clause allows a function to throw any exception • You can handle only exceptions of the class logic_error and its derived classes. To handle an exception, you write code that consists of two pieces: • try block - contains the statements that might cause or throw an exception • one or more catch blocks that follow the try block

Throwing Exceptions LISTING C3-3 Revised findBox function that throws an exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • If you can resolve the unusual situation in a reasonable manner, your code should do so instead of throwing an exception. • If several resolutions to an abnormal occurrence are possible, and you want the client to choose one, you could/should throw an exception. • If you detect that a programmer has used your method incorrectly, you can throw a runtime exception. Recall that this action will halt program execution.

Handling Exceptions • Code for handling exception – try block – contains statements that might cause or throw an exception – catch block – immediately follows try block with code to react to or catch a particular type of exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved General syntax for a try block followed by one catch block

Handling Exceptions • try block – Contains statements that might cause or throw an exception • catch block – One or more catch blocks immediately follow try block – Contain code to react to or catch particular type of exception © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Handling Exceptions • If no exception occurs and try block completes – Execution continues with statement after catch block • If statement within try block causes exception of type specified in catch block – Remainder of try block abandoned – Execution transfers to statements in catch block – After catch block statements finish, execution jumps to statement after last catch block © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Handling Exceptions • The syntax for catch block resembles that of a function definition – Specifies type of exception, and an identifier – The catch block parameter provides name for caught exception • Steps taken in catch block vary – Simple message – Elaborate update of variables, retry of offending function © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Handling Exceptions LISTING C3-4 Trying the function findBox © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Multiple catch Blocks • try block may cause more than one type of exception – Can have many catch blocks associated with it • catch blocks must be ordered – Most specific classes first – More general classes last © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Multiple catch Blocks © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Arrange catch blocks in order of specificity, catching the most specific one first. • Since all exception classes have exception as an ancestor, try to avoid using exception in a catch block. • If you must catch exceptions of type exception , do so in the last catch block.

Uncaught Exceptions © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • When an exception can occur within a loop, you must decide whether to: o place a try block within the body of the loop, in which case an exception will be caught each time the loop cycles or o place the entire loop within a try block—in which case the exception would be caught only once • Neither choice is better than the other, but if you make the wrong choice, the client likely will not get the necessary information.

Programmer-Defined Exception Classes • There is a C++ Standard Library exception class defined. • You may also want to define your own exception class. • Usually, C++ exception class exception , or one of its derived classes, is the base class for programmer defined exception classes – Provides a standardized interface for working with exceptions. • Exception class typically consists of a constructor that has a string parameter © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Programmer-Defined Exception Classes Example – constructor provides way for throw statement to identify condition of exception. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Exceptions - Summary • To throw an exception in a function: – Determine what errors, preconditions, postconditions, and unusual conditions you must check and where they should be checked, so your function can correctly perform its task. Usually this is done with an if statement. – If an error condition has occurred, throw an exception using the throw statement. As an argument to the exception class constructor, pass a string description of the reason for the error. – Add a throw clause to the function header indicating the exceptions thrown by the function. – Derive a custom exception class to better identify the conditions that caused the error, though doing so is optional, it is desirable. • To handle an exception or use a function that could throw an exception – Place the statement that might throw an exception in a try block. The fewer statements in the try block the better. – Do not use new to create an object in a try block. – After the try block, place catch blocks for each type of exception that can be thrown by the statements in the try block. – Place the catch blocks in order from the most specific exception classes to the more general exception classes. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Inheritance Revisited • Derived class inherits public/protected members of its base class • Do not inherit the constructors and destructor • Can add new members it defines • Can revise any inherited methods • Inheritance enables the reuse of existing classes © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Multiple Inheritance • Derived class can have more than one base class – multiple inheritance • A method in a derived class redefines a nonvirtual method in the base class if the two methods have the same name and parameter declarations • An instance of a derived class contains copies of inherited data members, but the code for inherited methods is not copied. • A derived class cannot access the private members of the base class directly by name, even though they are inherited. Inheritance does not imply access. • A derived class can use the base class’s public methods to set or get the value of base item(s) indirectly. • If a new method has the same name as an ancestor method— setItem, for example—instances of the new class will use the new method, while instances of the ancestor class will use the original method (early (static) binding). © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Early versus Late Binding // C++ Program to illustrate early (static) binding #include<iostream> using namespace std; class Base { public: void show() {cout << " In Base 
"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() {cout << "In Derived 
"; } }; int main() { Base* bp = new Derived; bp->show(); // RUN-TIME POLYMORPHISM delete bp; bp = nullptr; return 0; } https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/early-binding-late-binding-c/ // C++ Program to illustrate late (dynamic) binding #include<iostream> using namespace std; class Base { public: virtual void show() {cout << " In Base 
"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() {cout << "In Derived 
"; } }; int main() { Base* bp = new Derived; bp->show(); // RUN-TIME POLYMORPHISM delete bp; bp = nullptr; return 0; } This will execute This will execute

Multiple Inheritance • Derived class can have more than one base class – multiple inheritance • A method in a derived class redefines a nonvirtual method in the base class if the two methods have the same name and parameter declarations © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved #include <iostream> class DigitalClock { public: void turnOn() { std::cout << " DigitalClock 's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class Radio { public: void turnOn() { std::cout << " Radio's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class DigitalRadio : public DigitalClock, public Radio { // Inherits turnOn() from both DigitalClock and Radio }; int main() { DigitalRadio obj; obj.turnOn(); // ERROR: which turnOn() to call? } ambiguity ERROR: which turnOn() to call?

Multiple Inheritance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved #include <iostream> class DigitalClock { public: void turnOn() { std::cout << " DigitalClock 's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class Radio { public: void turnOn() { std::cout << " Radio's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class DigitalRadio : public DigitalClock, public Radio { // Inherits turnOn() from both DigitalClock and Radio }; int main() { DigitalRadio obj; obj.turnOn(); // ERROR: which turnOn() to call? obj.Radio::turnOn() Ambiguity Resolution - Explicit qualification: specify which base class's turnOn method using the scope resolution operator (::) Ambiguity Resolution - Explicit qualification

Multiple Inheritance © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved #include <iostream> class DigitalClock { public: virtual void turnOn() { std::cout << " DigitalClock 's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class Radio { public: void turnOn() { std::cout << " Radio's turnOn" << std::endl; } }; class DigitalRadio : public DigitalClock, public Radio { public: void turnOn() override { std::cout << " DigitalRadio turnOn" << std::endl; } }; int main() { DigitalRadio obj; obj.turnOn(); // DigitalRadio’s turnOn will be called Ambiguity Resolution:override - You can explicitly define a turnOn method in the derived class DigitalRadio to resolve the ambiguity 1. Add virtual to one of the base classes 2. Redefine(override) the method in the derived class Ambiguity Resolution - Override the method in the derived class (virtual inheritance)

Public, Private and Protected • Public members can be used by anyone. • Private members can be used only by methods of the class. • Protected members can be used only by methods of the class or any derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Public, Private and Protected Inheritance • Public inheritance: Public and protected members of the base class remain, respectively, public and protected members of the derived class. • Private inheritance: Public and protected members of the base class are private members of the derived class. • Protected inheritance: Public and protected members of the base class are protected members of the derived class. • In all cases, private members of a base class remain private to the base class and cannot be accessed by a derived class. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Is-a and As-a Relationships • Is-a relationships • Public inheritance implements an is-a relationship • In general, a derived class is type-compatible with all of its ancestor classes. • As-a relationships • When public inheritance is inappropriate, (e.g., stack is not a list) • if your class needs access to the protected members of another class or • if you need to redefine methods in that class, • you can form an as-a relationship between your classes; that is, you can use private inheritance class Stack : private List /* List’s public and protected members, if any, would be private members of Stack */ © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Abstract Base Class • Class that contains at least one pure virtual method. • Is used only as the basis for derived classes and thus defines a minimum interface for its descendants. • Has descendants but no instances. • Should, in general, omit implementations except for the destructor and methods that provide access to private data members. That is, virtual methods in an abstract base class usually should be pure. • Must implement any virtual method that is not pure, thereby providing a default implementation if a derived class chooses not to supply its own. © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Recursive Solutions • A recursive function calls itself • Each recursive call solves an identical, but smaller, problem • Test for base case enables recursive calls to stop • Eventually, one of smaller problems must be the base case • Some recursive solutions are impractical because they are so inefficient • Can provide elegantly simple solutions to problems of great complexity © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Recursive Solutions Questions for constructing recursive solutions 1. How to define the problem in terms of a smaller problem of same type? 2. How does each recursive call diminish the size of the problem? 3. What instance of problem can serve as base case? 4. As problem size diminishes, will you reach base case? © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Recursive Solutions • Recursion breaks problem into smaller identical problems – An alternative to iteration • FIGURE 2-1 A recursive solution © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • This strategy is called divide and conquer. • Solve the dictionary search problem by first dividing the dictionary into two halves and then conquering the appropriate half. • Solve the smaller problem by using the same divide-and-conquer strategy. • Dividing continues until you reach the base case.

A Recursive Valued Function: The Factorial of n • An iterative solution • A recursive solution © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Note: Do not use recursion if a problem has a simple, efficient iterative solution
A Recursive Valued Function: The Factorial of n FIGURE 2-2 fact(3) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved n n - 1 n n - 1 n n - 1 n

The Box Trace 1. Label each recursive call 2. Represent each call to function by a new box 3. Draw arrow from box that makes call to newly created box 4. After you create new box, execute body of function 5. On exiting function, cross off current box and follow its arrow back © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Box Trace FIGURE 2-4 The beginning of the box trace © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1. Label each recursive call - you return to point A after each recursive call, substitute the computed value, and continue evaluating n*fact(n-1) Each box is activation record – typical compiler implementation of a function call 2. Represent each call to function by a new box – update local environment (arg values, local variables, placeholder for value returned (label placeholder to correspond to step 1 label, and value of function itself 3. Draw arrow to newly created box 4. Execute function 5. On exiting the function, cross off the current box and follow its arrow back to the box that called the function. int fact(int n) { if (n==0) { return 1;} else { return n * fact(n-1);}}

The Box Trace FIGURE 2-5 Box trace of fact(3) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1. Label each recursive call 2. New box for each recursive call; update variables, placeholder 3. Draw arrow 4. Execute function Steps 1 – 4 are repeated int fact(int n) { if (n==0) { return 1;} else { return n * fact(n-1);}} A

The Box Trace FIGURE 2-5 Box trace of fact(3) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 5a. On exiting the function, cross off the current box 5c. This box now becomes the current box, and the label on the arrow specifies the exact location at which execution of the function should continue. 5b. follow its arrow back to the box that called the function 5d. Substitute the value returned by the just- terminated function call for the appropriate placeholder in the current box. int fact(int n) { if (n==0) { return 1;} else { return n * fact(n-1);}} A

Organizing a Parade • Will consist of bands and floats in single line. – You are asked not to place one band immediately after another • In how many ways can you organize a parade of length n ? – P (n) = number of ways to organize parade of length n – F (n) = number of parades of length n, end with a float – B (n) = number of parades of length n, end with a band • Then P(n) = F(n) + B(n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Organizing a Parade • Possible to see • P(1) = 2 • P (2) = 3 • P (n) = P(n – 1) + P(n – 2) for n > 2 • Thus a recursive solution • Solve the problem by breaking up into cases © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

What Is a Good Solution? • A program incurs a real and tangible cost. – Computing time – Memory required – Difficulties encountered by users – Consequences of incorrect actions by program • A solution is good if … – The total cost incurs … – Over all phases of its life … is minimal © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

What Is a Good Solution? • Important elements of the solution – Good structure – Good documentation – Efficiency • Be concerned with efficiency when – Developing underlying algorithm – Choice of objects and design of interaction between those objects © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Measuring Efficiency of Algorithms • Important because – Choice of algorithm has significant impact • Examples – Responsive word processors – Grocery checkout systems – Automatic teller machines – Video machines – Life support systems © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Measuring Efficiency of Algorithms • Analysis of algorithms – The area of computer science that provides tools for contrasting efficiency of different algorithms – Comparison of algorithms should focus on significant differences in efficiency – We consider comparisons of algorithms, not programs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Measuring Efficiency of Algorithms • Difficulties with comparing programs (instead of algorithms) – How are the algorithms coded – What computer will be used – What data should the program use • Algorithm analysis should be independent of – Specific implementations, computers, and data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

The Execution Time of Algorithms • An algorithm’s execution time is related to number of operations it requires. – Algorithm’s execution time is related to number of operations it requires. • Example: Towers of Hanoi – Solution for n disks required 2n – 1 moves – If each move requires time m – Solution requires (2n – 1) × m time units © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Execution Time Evolution • In RDBMS, access times are critical – RAM is in nanoseconds – In 1980s-1990s, hard disk access times ~20 millis – Modern SSDs access times ~9 microseconds • Query optimizers had to be rewritten – All access times are now parameterized/metadata – No more hardcoding of values © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Algorithm Growth Rates • Measure an algorithm’s time requirement as function of problem size • Most important thing to learn – How quickly algorithm’s time requirement grows as a function of problem size – Demonstrates contrast in growth rates © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved
Algorithm Growth Rates FIGURE 10-1 Time requirements as a function of problem size n © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Analysis and Big O Notation • Algorithm A is said to be order f (n), – Denoted as O( f (n)) – Function f(n) called algorithm’s growth rate function – Notation with capital O denotes order • Algorithm A of order denoted O(f(n)) – Constants k and n0 exist such that – A requires no more than k × f(n) time units – For problem of size n ≥ n0 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

RDBMS - Small Amounts of Data • In RDBMS, indexes are used for performance – Indexes are Balanced Trees stored on disk – Presorted data structures • For large amounts of data, indexes are critical • For small amounts of data, indexes are not needed – MySQL default page size is 16K (can be changed) – Data under 2-3 dozen pages is “small” © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Analysis and Big O Notation • Worst-case analysis – Worst case analysis usually considered – Easier to calculate, thus more common • Average-case analysis – More difficult to perform – Must determine relative probabilities of encountering problems of given size © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Keeping Your Perspective • ADT used makes a difference – Array-based getEntry is O(1) – Link-based getEntry is O(n) • Choosing implementation of ADT – Consider how frequently certain operations will occur – Seldom used but critical operations must also be efficient © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Keeping Your Perspective • If problem size is always small – Possible to ignore algorithm’s efficiency • Weigh trade-offs between – Algorithm’s time and memory requirements • Compare algorithms for style and efficiency © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Efficiency of Searching Algorithms • Sequential search – Worst case: O(n) – Average case: O(n) – Best case: O(1) • Binary search – O(log2n) in worst case – At same time, maintaining array in sorted order requires overhead cost … can be substantial © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Application: Simulation • Simulation, a major application area for computers, is models the behavior of both natural and human-made systems • Mathematicians learned to model events (e.g., the arrival of people) by using techniques from probability theory. • This statistical information is incorporated into the mathematical model of the system and is used to generate events in a way that reflects the real world. • The simulation uses these events and is thus called an event driven simulation. • The use of a data file with predetermined event information is common in simulations. • Problem to solve – Approximate average time bank customer must wait for service from a teller – Decrease in customer wait time with each new teller added © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • Position-oriented ATDs – Stack, list, queue • Value-oriented ADTs – Sorted list - When a new item is added to a sorted list, the sorted list determines the item’s placement according to its value. • Unlike a list, a sorted list cannot be told where to place its items. – Priority queue - orders its entries according to their priorities © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Stack Queue isEmpty - isEmpty push - insert a new item on top enqueue - insert a new item at end pop - removes the most recent item at top of stack dequeue removes the first item, which is at the front of the queue peek - retrieves the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack peekFront - retrieves the first item at the front of the queue

Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • ADT list operations generalize stack and queue operations – getLength – insert – remove – getEntry © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved

Position-Oriented and Value-Oriented ADTs • Comparison of stack and queue operations – isEmpty - see if any items exist in the ADT – push and enqueue - insert a new item into one end (the top and back, respectively) of the ADT – pop and dequeue - removes the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack, and dequeue removes the first item, which is at the front of the queue – peek and peekFront - peek operation retrieves the most recent item, which is at the top of the stack, and peekFront retrieves the first item at the front of the queue © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ.
